The conflict displaced four million people. Many South Sudanese fled to north Sudan, while others became refugees in neighbouring Ethiopia, Kenya, Uganda. Some made it further afield to Europe, Australia and North America.
People were returning to South Sudan with a variety of cultural influences, Kulang said, making it essential "to engage our people to know their original culture".
Garang Dut Garang, a young man from Unity state, agreed that the contact the youth will have through the traditional dance will help boost unity among young people.
"Off course it will promote the unity because it is now making us knowing that we are one people and we are from one mother and one father and so we are really encouraging this cultural dances to be going ahead", Garang told Sudan Tribune on Wednesday.
Solomon Ayiko, an international UN volunteer from Canada who is working on recovery, reintegration and peace building, said that culture can be a key instrument in improving community relations.
"Culture is one of the most powerful means to transform society in Unity state. Culture can be the best instrument that government and the Unity state community can use to confront security issues and to enhance the struggle toward peace and development", added Ayiko.
Ayiko believes that if the cultural exchanges were extended to youth from neighbouring states it would reduce cattle raiding, which has been a major issue in the relations between communities especially along the axis of Unity, Warrap and Lakes states.
"Cultural activities the music and dance and drama, if it is enhanced, if it is supported, it can confront one of the major root causes of insecurity caused by cattle raiding" he said.
Director Kulang says that the government has decided to build peace by bringing people together through youth activities and cultural shows.
"In our mandate we have [a] slogan that says 'peace, unity and stability of the people of Unity state' and this must be achieved through cultural activities, youth activities and sport activities", he said.
"Now you see many people are in the stadium seeing or watching the cultural activities it is peace by itself", said Kulang.
Many people in Bentiu have welcome the decision by the state government to declare Sunday a day for cultural and youth activities.
In a recent discussion on state-owned Bentiu FM the majority of callers expressed happiness that the government was considering using such events to create a more peaceful environment.

Original_Search_Title: Ringo Humbled By Zim Support
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ringo Humbled By Zim Support
Body_Text:
South African musician Ringo Madlingozi has promised a "thank you" package for his Zimbabwean fans when he performs in the country on November 29. The show is set for City Sports Centre and will feature local musicians Oliver Mtukudzi, Winky D and Jah Prayzah.
The gig is dubbed "Thank You Zimbabwe" and Ringo is expected to take time to narrate what he has achieved with Zimbabwe's support.
Ringo's manager, Daniel Mofulwane, told The Herald in an interview that the SA jazz star has a lot to thank Zimbabweans for.
"Zimbabweans have done a lot for Ringo. He has cherished all his shows in Zimbabwe and wishes he could perform for them more often.
"Zimbabwe is like our second home and we are happy to be given a chance to salute our fans for what they have done for us," said Mofulwane.
Ringo made strides on the local music field through his collaboration with Oliver Mtukudzi on the song "Yinto Yam".
The South African star was one of the few regional artistes that did not make it to Mtukudzi tribute concert held in Harare on September 27 to celebrate the Zimbabwean superstar's 61st birthday.
The tribute concert was attended by Mtukudzi's friends in the music fraternity and from South Africa came Hugh Masekela, Judith Sephuma, Steve Dyer and Berita Khumalo.
The Kenyan duo of Eric Wainaina and Suzanna Awiyo as well as Mozambican star Stewart Sukuma also featured.
Mofulwane said Ringo would make up for his absence from Mtukudzi's tribute concert through the November 29 show.
"We attended Mtukudzi's 60th birthday concert last year, but we could not make it to this year's event because we had some shows that weekend. We heard the concert was good and we know we missed a lot.
"We will use the upcoming gig to deliver our celebratory message to Mtukudzi since he will also be performing. We are looking forward to an exciting tour in Zimbabwe and we promise the best on stage," he said.
Organisers of the "Thank You Zimbabwe" gig said they had chosen Ringo for the show because of his appeal to mature fans since the gig seeks to cut across age groups.
"It is no secret that Ringo's collaboration with Mtukudzi is one of the best regional combinations of our time. He is one artiste who has a heart for Zimbabwe and does not want to go for a year without performing here.
"He fits well in our programme that targets different age groups. Winky D is likely to attract a youthful crowd. Mtukudzi and Ringo will serve their mature fans. Jah Prayzah bridges the age gap as he appeals to the young and the mature," said one of the show organisers.

Original_Search_Title: 'FRSC, VIO Not Doing Enough to Curb Road Accidents'
Headline: Nigeria: 'FRSC, VIO Not Doing Enough to Curb Road Accidents'
Body_Text:
Pius Akwashiki, a professional driver, from Nasarawa state, started driving at the age of 15. Currently, he works with ABC transport company Abuja. In this interview, he explains why there are frequent accidents on the road during ember months. Akwashiki who recently won the five-year accident-free driver award from his company, says FRSC and the VIO have done little to curb accidents.
Tell us briefly about your profession.
I am a professional driver who has been working with the ABC transport company, Abuja in the last eight years. I have a very good track record of service. Just last year, I was given an award for the being the only driver who drove for five years at a stretch, (accident-free) plying long distance routes as well , without accident up to this moment. And I drive both local and international routes, across Togo and Ghana. And I have driven all over Nigeria both day and night. I am 32 years old now?
I started driving since 1993, then I was a mechanic and from there I ventured into driving proper. But I started driving professionally in 1995. But throughout my life time and right when I was in primary school, I was driving and even in secondary school. So, after secondary school, I joined the work and even when I was in Polytechnic, I was still driving. So, driving is part of me. So, after my secondary school, I formally learned how to drive because I have passion for the job. So, I started driving when I was in primary six, then I was 15 years.
Since you became a professional driver, you have worked with how many companies now?
I worked in Lagos with a company called Kwanet, an intercity transport company and thereafter, I joined ABC transport. Like I said earlier, I started as a mechanic and when I switched over to professional driving, my company, that is, Kwanet trained me, through the FRSC and VIO.
What has been you experience on the road in your last 15 years of professional driving?
My experience on the road is something I can describe as very strange, especially when I am driving at nights. I have discovered driving professionally require talents, like other professions. But a lot of drivers don't have this talent. The behavior of some commercial drivers particularly on the highway, tells you that they went into it because they lack jobs. They cause most of the problems on the road. But for me, I drive because of the passion I have for it and not because I don't have a job. So, I am totally committed, not necessarily because I want to make money, but I enjoy it.
What particular behaviours of other drivers that offend you or others on the highway?
I assume every commercial driver is trained, but I have discovered that the assumption is wrong. Why? Because most times, it shocks me that commercial would overtake at bends or blind spots. Some don't understand the interpretation of road signs. Whenever I see the driver of a private car commit such common mistakes, it doesn't pain me as much as when a commercial driver commits it. He is supposedly someone who has lived his life driving and should know better. It often leads to fatal accidents and most times, they are avoidable mistakes. So, it pains me that after such deliberate, careless attitudes leading to tragedies on the road, the culprits go scot-free without any form of reprimand from government. Our government has completely neglected the transport sector and safety of its citizens on the road. Forget that there is the FRSC or the VIO. They are just there doing very little or nothing at all. But it is not so in some West Africa countries, like Ghana, Togo or Benin Republic which have just one airport, that flies only international routes. Every government official travels by road to any part of the country and so, the government takes safety on the road, a serious issue. It has more eyes on public transporters and operators conform to set standards, because government is not just interested, but watching what they do and punishes violators. And they have minimal accidents and there is no accident in that country that government will not probe the individual or company and sanction accordingly. But it is not so with us in Nigeria.
Are you saying government probe of road accidents and sanctioning of those concerned would reduce road accidents?
Certainly, yes! If government probes every accident and apply the lawful punishment to culprits, including withdrawal of driving licenses or permanent closure or seizure of operating licenses of transport companies, people would be more careful. We tend to forget that the transport sector employs more people and creates more jobs and therefore, it is the duty of government to protect both passengers and operators
But there are multiple mushroom and big transport companies operating. What is the best way government can handle or regulate their operations for public interest?
It's simple. Government has given guidelines and standards generally, but no one ensures compliance either for big or small companies. It is not enough to create FRSC or the VIO agencies. For me, FRSC wait for accidents to happen and they come to the rescue of victims and VIO only inspects accurateness of vehicle particulars. They hardly bother themselves with the physical condition of vehicles. No! Once your particulars are up-to-date, they salute you loudly and say go, even if your tyres are evidently so worn out that you are not supposed to ply the road. I am sure there are owners of public transport companies in this country that have only heard of the names FRSC or VIO. But they are operating, causing avoidable troubles for everybody on the road. My company for instance, organizes quarterly refresher courses for us on road usage and safety. But how many transport companies do it ? Just a few, out of thousands. I know of companies that have hired untrained drivers, who ply the roads, but who is there to caution them?
Would you say, transport owners are fair to drivers, in terms of welfare and working conditions? Of course, no. Driving is a serious job. It has techniques, which must be understood by every driver. And it is the duty of companies to ensure this and also, the responsibility of FRSC or the VIO as the case may be, to ensure compliance. But our perception in this country is that drivers are the commonest people around individuals or institutions. A driver needs a good pay, adequate comfort and training. It marvels me that there are bosses who travel with their drivers and ask them to sleep in the car, while they sleep in luxury hotel rooms. There are transport companies with no guest houses or even terminals for their drivers or vehicles. A person drives all day to a destination and sleeps in the vehicle, wakes up early the next day to begin a return journey, with the lives of several people at his mercy. He is tired and weak; he has not eaten or eaten properly and psychologically, he feels he is the least human being alive. So, why won't his mind drift off the road and cause accidents for himself and others? There are drivers who have eye defects and should not drive at nights, especially on highways, but who cares? Unknown to many, a driver should also have an eating pattern. There are certain foods you are not supposed to eat at certain times of the day while in transit. Some know and others don't know. A driver plying long distance routes ought to eat light food in the morning to retain his energy and be active, not eba or pounded yam and such stuff. But what is our experience here? A driver leaving for Lagos, wakes up and eats Eba, there is the tendency that he will dose off, less than one hour into the journey. Most managers of transport companies don't feel it's necessary to ask their drivers how they have woken up that day or what is bothering them. The language is, move and make money. That's why some pay drivers according to the trips drivers make (pay-as-you-go), which is very wrong. This policy creates haste in drivers to make several trips a day, especially, during the ember months to earn more money. You know the Nigerian factor; he will want to break more trips to make more money. For such a driver, any other interest on the road is secondary. It is very wrong for government to allow commercial transport operators to operate this policy, if it is really interested in the safety of its citizens on the road. Even those on salary are paid very small amounts of money. You can count private or commercial drivers whose salary is up to the N18, 000, the national minimum wage per month. And that's why some drivers prefer the option of "Pay-as-you-go".
Why are accidents more frequent on the road during these ember months?.
It's a result of a mindset we have that what we have not made from January to August can be recouped during the ember months and we forget issues of safety. There is usually mass movement of people. There is the issue of bad roads and also, some vehicles which are not road worthy, yet allowed to ply the roads, sometimes overloaded even.
Would you say the FRSC or the VIO are doing enough in this regard?
They are not doing enough. That's why I call FRSC "Red Cross" because they wait until accidents happen, before they step in. They are supposed to work to prevent accidents from happening. They ought to have raised structures like say, stationing their officers in parks, to check vehicles that have loaded for trips to ensure they are in good condition. They could discharge passengers and possibly impound vehicles that are not road worthy right at the park, before they embark on trips to cause accidents, before the FRSC comes to the rescue. They ought to have ensured that every commercial driver and transport company is cleared by their offices before it begins operation. But there are no such structures now.

Original_Search_Title: Of Peace, Truth and Injustice
Headline: Nigeria: Of Peace, Truth and Injustice
Body_Text:
In a recent newspaper interview, the young journalist ended with a question: "What is your philosophy of life?" I had no hesitation in replying: JUSTICE. He prompted me to elaborate on my monosyllabic response. I told him that justice is the pre-condition for almost every basic value that a state or the citizen requires to achieve peace and progress. For me justice is the palm oil and red pepper sauce without which we cannot heartily enjoy the roasted yam of peace and harmony.
I emphasised that peace does not necessarily mean the absence of conflict and physical violence; rather peace means the presence of justice, truth and fairness. As the late Pope Paul VI said, if you want peace, work for justice.
The late John F. Kennedy of America put it even more succinctly. In his last State of the Union Address, before he was brutally assassinated in 1963, JFK told the American people: the absence of war does not mean peace. In his contribution to the publication of the National Peace Forum in 2004, our own Donald Duke, then Governor of Cross River State, added his own dimension. Peace, he wrote, is the aftermath of justice and goodwill to all. Duke was basically saying that from the fountain of justice, peace flows. The late Ikemba Nnewi, Emeka Odumegwu Ojukwu, concurred with Duke in the same publication, saying peace is the outcome of social justice.
This is perhaps an appropriate time for our nation to calmly look deep into the goatskin bag of our collective wisdom and consider the true meaning of our creed of justice as enshrined in both our constitution and coat of arms, as we quest for better models and modus vivendi, for existence and co-existence, after 100 years of union. As Nigeria pauses for a moment of stock-taking, she is faced with a huge array of national issues and challenges.
They range from the current menace of Boko Haram to a proposed National Conference on the very future of the federation; from our economic development to the cost of living, and from strikes, university education and the future of our burgeoning population, and indeed including the privatisation of our commonwealth like PHCN, as well as the management of our natural resources. To tackle these issues and challenges over the months and years ahead, the precondition of justice anchored in truth is a sine-qua-non for lasting solutions to these myriad troubles.
But pray what is justice? Who is he? What does he look like? One thing for sure is that he is not the justice of selfish interest, whether ethnic, religious or political. For those that seek this kind of justice, no concession, compromise or concept of truth and fairness will satisfy. For this kind of people, their idea of justice is the one that says what is mine is mine, and what is yours is negotiable! Rather I have in mind the kind of justice, which the great British jurist Lord Denning described in the simplest yet most profound terms as: Giving everyone his due. As I was contemplating this intervention, I came across a blog by a young Nigerian, which was the very antithesis of the Denning definition.
She wrote that injustice is when (1) a law enforcement officer seizes your okada and takes it for himself and even rides it on the very roads banned for okada; (2) the law enforcement officer arbitrarily seizes your okada, sells it to someone else and pockets the money; (3) 200cc motorcycles can be bought only in 'Alausa' if you want particulars for the vehicle.
In this perversion of justice, the okada owner is only a metaphor for the vast majority of Nigerians. If I may venture to give a very personal example, in 2005, in my very last year in office, I got an allocation of a plot of land in Abuja. It was the only one I applied for and the then Minister of FCT, Alhaji Abba Gana, personally approved it and directed his then Permanent Secretary, Dr. Babangida Aliyu, to process the allocation. Eventually he did the honours and I paid all the legal and statutory fees and got my C of O. But as soon as I left office and the district in question was developed with essential infrastructure, thus attracting a high selling price, a fellow Nigerian connived with his kinsman, slipped a pre-dated application into my file and claimed that there had been a "prior-allocation.
" The incumbent minister revoked my allocation without notice and without due process. Considering that my allocation was handled at the highest level, I found it hard to believe that there had been no due diligence to ensure that there were no encumbrances on the plot I was given. It was just simply that someone lied to deny me my due. My greatest pain in the matter was that in the United Kingdom where I was a mere resident, there is no human being from the Queen to the Prime Minister who would arbitrarily revoke my allocation for which I have fulfilled all statutory requirements. In such an unthinkable event, the Member of Parliament who represents my constituency would demand an emergency meeting of the appropriate parliamentary committee to defend my right and entitlement. Similarly, the law enforcement officer who appropriated the okada motorcycle would never wear the uniform of the police force again and might even end up in jail.
The state would be seen to be defending the rights of the citizen and justice would be seen in action. In different ways, on different scales and in various areas of our national life, this and other forms of injustice are the lot of many of our citizens who often have no opportunity for redress. And when this is extrapolated upon the wider communities and ethnic nationalities who in one way or the other feel genuinely deprived, denied or marginalised, the nation would not know peace even if there was no open conflict. A nation ignores such silent discontent at its own peril because still waters run deep. This was the miscalculation by Arab leaders that led to the uprising of the Arab Spring. Its lesson was beware of the silent majority who are suffering and smiling.
On the other hand, in the case of our own Niger Delta, where there was open and protracted violence, there was general acknowledgement that there had been a measure of injustice against the people. President Umaru Musa Yar'Adua, with no vested interest other than the truth of the matter, resolved to address and redress the conflict with his Amnesty programme. It is the same formula that had worked for Britain in resolving the conflict with the militant IRA in Northern Ireland. Northern Ireland and the Niger Delta today enjoy considerable peace, primarily because the existence of injustice was recognised and redressed.
Both proved another Denning aphorism that when justice is done, no person wins or loses; only justice wins. In like manner, President Jonathan has set the machinery in motion to determine, among other things, whether Boko Haram has a legitimate claim to any form of injustice that can and could be addressed. Of course identifying injustice where it truly exists, as distinct from where it is claimed to exist, is the first step towards redressing it, as long as both parties are sincere.
As Nigeria contemplates the future of her union, it is important we recognise the self-evident fact that there cannot be peace in the home, in the community and in the country as a whole without truth, justice and fairness. It is from these two that peace flows and reigns. If I may, let me cite the example of the United Kingdom, a society I know almost as well as my own Nigeria. I never cease to marvel at and be filled with admiration that a country which literally invented the slave trade, colonialism and racism, eventually made moral restitution by turning itself into a multi-ethnic, multi-cultural and tolerant society.
Britain stepped back from her inglorious colonial past and carved out a proud place for herself as a tolerant nation, in a manner that non-colonial powers like Russia, for instance, can never boast of. I am often baffled by the effort British authorities put into protecting the human and civic rights of ethnic and religious minorities. I do not of course discount the continued existence of racism in British society as in many white societies. For while you can legislate against racial discrimination, you cannot make laws against the racial prejudice that exists in the hearts of human beings. But I know no country with more laws, bye-laws, court rulings and precedents against racial and other forms of discrimination, as well as for the protection of the rights of ethnic and other minorities, than the United Kingdom of Great Britain.
As we prepare for the talks that will precede The Talk, it will be an enduring service to the nation if we are truthful to ourselves in addressing the core issues at the root of the persistent discord that afflicts the nation and affects its progress towards greatness. We must recognise that justice and fairness are Siamese twins, which in turn give birth to peace. We must fairly and honestly purge ourselves of ethnic, religious and party political bigotry that often blights our national discourse, and try to embrace the truth that cleanses, purifies the soul and enthrones justice. I wish that in the end a new nation can emerge, one that will be more tolerant and much fairer. For in truth it is a source of great personal pain for me that my rights in the United Kingdom are far better protected by the state than my rights in Nigeria, the country of my birth. Sadly, there must be millions of Nigerians with similar stories of their own.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Adoration Tragedy - the Unseen Forces
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Adoration Tragedy - the Unseen Forces
Body_Text:
Certainly, the last monthly adoration crusade at the Holy Ghost Adoration Ministry, Uke in Idemili South local government area of Anambra state held on November 1, 2013, will for a long time remain a source of intense sadness and sorrow for the worshipers and Christian faithfuls at large.
The adoration crusade organized first Friday of every month, offers spiritual and physical protection to the devotees. Christians from different denominations attend the adoration crusade to get healings for spiritual and physical sicknesses and liberation from retrogressive forces.
However, the last ceremony for some of the teeming worshipers rather turned out like a case of jumping from frying pan into the fire as about 25 of them lost their lives and many others received various degrees of injuries and were rushed to hospitals as a result of a stampede that occurred at the end of the ceremony. The centre is being coordinated by a fiery Catholic priest, Revd. Fr. Emmanuel Obimma, popular as Ebube Monso (Glory of the Holy Spirit).
The most worrisome issues concerning the unfortunate incident is the manner the cause of the stampede is being linked to some political forces involved in the forth-coming November 16, governorship election in the state.
Though the leadership of the catholic church in the state do not want to link the cause of the stampede to any deliberate act of sabotage ,especially the political underhand being suspected, two of the frontline political parties in the forthcoming governorship election in the state, including the state controlled All Progressives Grand Alliance (APGA) and the All Progressives Congress (APC), are already accusing each other of fomenting the pandemonium that claimed lives of scores of the worshipers, living many others seriously wounded.
The Director of Social Communications, Catholic Archdiocese of Onitsha, Revd Fr. Pius Ukor who addressed newsmen in respect of the incident on behalf of the Archbishop, Most Rev. Valerian Okeke, stated that the stampede was caused by a "false alarm raised by somebody in the crowd along one of the narrow roads out of the town", adding, "the crowd was so much that people wanting to leave all at the same time, made it very difficult for people to find enough space to move".
The Catholic Church made it clear that the incident happened outside the adoration crusade ground and that it occurred after the ceremony had ended.
Incidentally, the state governor, Mr. Peter Obi, the governorship candidate of his party, the APGA, Chief Willie Obiano and the party's national chairman, Sir Victor Umeh, all devotee Catholics attended the adoration crusade. The day was also the solemnity of All Saints Day, a holy day of obligation for Catholic faithfuls. And as a mark of his observance of that holy day, the governor had cancelled all events lined up for him on that day, including political campaigns and other
road construction flag off ceremonies. He had gone to the adoration centre at about 10.30pm on that fateful Friday accompanied by nine others and later left in the early hours of the following day with his team at about 3.30am -about one and a half hours before the close of the ceremony.
Before leaving the venue, the governor was said to have been invited to speak by the organizers and that as he was made to speak, some persons identified as supporters of the APC governorship candidate, Senator Chris Ngige, who were wearing his campaign clothes, waving brooms, the party's campaign material, attempted to disrupt his speech by waving the brooms and singing praises of Ngige, but that the governor did not mind them but left the venue immediately after making his speech. He was said to have made a promise that he will sponsor about 20 members of the adoration centre to Israel on pilgrimage, construct roads leading to the centre as well as tackle gully erosion threat in the area.
Already, the APGA national chairman, Umeh had already accused Ngige of sponsoring the stampede at the centre and urged the police to arrest and thoroughly investigate him in connection with the incident.
Umeh who spoke to newsmen on Sunday in Awka shortly after Governor Obi declared a three-day mourning and prayer in a state-wide broadcast for the peaceful repose of the souls of those who lost their lives and quick recovery of the wounded at the adoration centre stampede, stated, "Ngige should be immediately arrested because the signs were there that he planned and executed this unfortunate tragedy for the fact that people who infiltrated the arena carried his brooms and campaign posters and chanted solidarity songs without any justification".
Ngige and the APC would rather accuse Governor Obi and his party, APGA of being responsible for the stampede. Ngige accused Governor Obi and his party of going to campaign at the adoration centre at night, accompanied by a large entourage of supporters.
Ngige, however stated that when he visited the centre about two months ago, he only went with "few people and it was in the day time".
One of the worshipers at the adoration centre, Mr. Emeka Ilodiuba, who lost his wife in the stampede stated how the incident occurred, "When the prayer ended at about 5.30am, we stood up to go. Before then, the Governor (Mr. Peter Obi) and his entourage had left the venue. So, when we reached at the entrance, there was a huge crowd and everybody was struggling to get out. It was then that many people started falling on each other. That was when my wife fell down and people fell on top of her. She died in the process".
The International Society for the Civil Liberties and Rule of Law (Intersociety), in a statement, said that one of its members who attended the adoration crusade stated that the stampede was caused
when a sudden flame was noticed in the area. The flame, according to the human rights group, occurred when the from the frying pan of one of the women frying yam and "akara" around the adoration centre area poured into the fire, and somebody now raised alarm shouting, fire, fire!
This alarm now forced many people to scamper for safety. But, though, another eyewitness corroborated the Intersociety's fire account, he, however insisted that it was the handiwork of some "paid agents" who did not know that the governor and his entourage had left the venue and had wanted to create an atmosphere of chaos so that the governor and his entourage would be attacked.
According to the witness, "Trouble started when a group of people behaving like paid agents started shouting 'Fire, fire, fire' which made people to scamper for safety. They probably did not know that Governor Obi who came there with his party's governorship candidate and his entourage had left the place. You know that Obi doesn't blow siren when he is moving, and the number of people there was more than 100,000. So the people didn't know that they had left and wanted to cause confusion because they wanted to attack them (the Governor and his entourage)".
Meanwhile, some other sources hinted that the stampede was caused after a big snake was sighted at the adoration ground while others claimed it was a cow.
Another account even had it that it was the security aides of the governor who caused the stampede; that they fired tear-gas at the venue when some people allegedly shouted down the Governor when he was invited to speak, shouting," No, no, no" and rather singing
praises of Ngige saying that it was Ngige that they wanted.
In his state-wide broadcast, Governor Obi announced plans by his government to set up a panel of inquiry to unearth the immediate and remote cause of the tragedy.
But Ngige faulted the plans, insisting that because the Governor was at the venue, it was not proper for him to set up the inquiry, otherwise he would be a judge in his own case.
Interestingly, the incident occurred right under the nose of two Deputy Inspectors General of Police (DIGs), Mr. Kachy Udorji and Mr. Philemon Leha who were in the state to appraise the level of preparedness of the police in respect of the forthcoming governorship election in the state.
The two very senior police officers immediately the news of the incident broke out accompanied Governor Obi back to the adoration centre and hospitals in Nkpor where the victims of the incident were being treated.
Observers express confidence because the incident happened right under the purview of the two senior police officers it would not be difficult for the nation's security body to unravel the actual circumstances that led to the incident.

Original_Search_Title: Motorists, Commuters Stranded Along Abakaliki-Afikpo Road
Headline: Nigeria: Motorists, Commuters Stranded Along Abakaliki-Afikpo Road
Body_Text:
Hundreds of motorists and commuters plying along the Abakaliki-Afikpo express road were yesterday stranded for more than 5 hours at Amuzu-Aboamege axis of the express road as two heavy duty vehicles carrying yams and cement over turned as a result of the bad state of the express road.
Some of the commuters mostly women and children who were returning to Abakaliki after their weekend at Afikpo had to trek long distances in a bid to possibly board another vehicle having waited for more than 5 hours without any hope or solution in sight.
When our correspondent visited the scene at about 5pm, some of the motorists and commuters waited helplessly while others were seen putting calls across to their family members and relations to either come to the scene of the road block to pick them or send a vehicle to convey them to Abakaliki.
Some of the commuters who spoke to Leadership described the express road as a death trap and called on President Goodluck Jonathan, the Minister for Transport, the Senate Committee Chairman on Works and National Assembly members from Ebonyi State to investigate the delay in the completion of the express road more than five years after it was awarded.
According to Mr. Nnachi Agwu; "we have been here since 1pm and the time now is 5.30pm. The most annoying part of it is that, all effort made to push the two vehicles out of the road has proved abortive.
Some of us had to leave our vehicles to possibly see if we can push the vehicles out of the road but all our efforts proved abortive".

Original_Search_Title: Who Says Nigeria Will Not Win the World Cup in 2014?
Headline: Nigeria: Who Says Nigeria Will Not Win the World Cup in 2014?
Body_Text:
My good friend, Jide Akintunde, Managing-Director of Financial Nigeria Limited, is what is known as a football aficionado. That means in simple English he loves the game. There is only one problem: Jide is a Nigerian and, in Nigeria, football aficionados are orphans. They live in motherless babies homes. They are football lovers without football teams. They are pounded-yam in search of edikaikong soup.
Should Jide say something like: "we are doing so much better this new season," don't be confused. He would not be referring to anything really belonging to him. He would be referring to Arsenal; a football club that happens to be in London, England. Arsenal is this season's revelation in the English Premier League. Surprisingly, they are currently sitting unusually pretty at the top of the pops. But as I said, Jide is a Nigerian: he is not English. The anomaly is even more pronounced because he does not live in London. He lives in Lagos. Nevertheless, Jide has so adopted Arsenal completely as his club; he sees no contradiction in referring to it with a possessive "we."
Can you imagine a white Briton in far-away London referring to Enugu Rangers as "we?" Can you imagine him wearing in London a t-shirt with the caption "Kano Pillars" emblazoned on it? I don't think so! However, the Arsenal football jersey is a common feature in the streets of Lagos. So are those of Chelsea and Manchester United. Why is it that we, as Nigerians, are football orphans, forced to adopt foreign clubs as if they were ours? In 2011, Andrew Uba, residing in far away Okigwem in Imo State, Nigeria, even went as far as commit suicide because Manchester United failed to defeat Barcelona in the European Champions League final.
Fickle supporters
Years ago, I stopped watching the Super Eagles play. I only make sure I know the results of their matches as soon as possible. At best, I watch the highlights, especially if they happen to win. I came to this resolution because watching them play can be dangerous to the health of a Nigerian. You might get a heart attack out of sheer anxiety, especially given their shoddy style of play. According to reports, those who watched the last World Cup qualifier between Nigeria and Ethiopia nearly died of high blood pressure in the first half. That is the predicament of Nigeria's teeming football lovers. They die many deaths during national football matches.
As a matter of fact, the Super Eagles hardly ever play their home matches in Lagos any more. The Lagos crowd is notoriously fickle. If the national team does not perform to expectation in the first few minutes, the orphan mentality of Nigerians quickly comes into play. The Nigerian crowd jettisons the national team and adopts the opposition. Every Nigerian move is jeered. Every opposition move is cheered. What this means is that if Nigeria plays against Cameroon in Lagos, it could very easily become home-court disadvantage. With a few hitches and glitches by the Super Eagles, Nigerians become Cameroonians in a heartbeat; cheering the visitors to victory.
Bad administration
Nigerians have never believed we can win the World Cup; and success must start at the level of belief. Since we refuse to believe, we have never prepared to win. On the contrary, we always prepare to fail. Indeed, we are such good failures, we succeed at failing. It takes four years to prepare for preeminence at the World Cup. But in Nigeria, we take four years to fail. We choose our coaches at the last minute. We choose coaches that are not world standard. We choose our players politically. At the last African Cup of Nations, we went without some of our best players. We failed on that occasion because we won, in spite of putting in our best effort to fail.
We don't think it matters if the players are not familiar with one another. We only reward them if they win; otherwise they are unlikely to get paid, or to be paid on time. Recently, Stephen Keshi, the current coach, complained that he has not been paid for seven months. That is how we ensure he will not be motivated. But you can be sure the Nigerian Football Federation administrators are paying themselves even what they don't deserve.
According to Sunday Oliseh, when Governor Peter Odili of Rivers State gave the Super Eagles money for qualifying for the 2002 World Cup, the NFF pocketed the money and refused to give it to the players. Therefore, on the eve of a friendly match with Egypt, the players threatened to boycott the game. As in everything in Nigeria, we are simply not serious. You may well ask why we should take football seriously until you realize that the game is one veritable instrument of national unity in Nigeria. It is the one thing about which virtually all Nigerians are in agreement: we want to win.
Nigerian prodigies
Football is also a sport where Nigeria can definitely excel. Indeed, I daresay, some of the very best footballers in the world are, and have been, Nigerians. We are the country of the football prodigies, such as Nwankwo Kanu, Austin Jay Jay Okocha, Rashidi Yekini, Segun Odegbami and Stephen Keshi. We are still producing quality players today, including Victor Moses, Vincent Enyeama, John Mikel Obi, Emmanuel Emenike, and Osaze Odemwingie. So why is it that, in this particular area, Nigeria is not on top of the world? That is one of the many tragedies of this dear country called Nigeria.
On three different occasions, Nigeria actually won the under 16/17 World Cup. It is on record that we have won it three times; in 1985, 1993 and 2007. We have also been runners up three times. Indeed, Nigeria is the most successful country in that competition; better than even Brazil, the second most successful country. This year, we have already qualified for the semi-finals taking place today in Dubai. Even more significant is the fact that Nigeria won the gold medal in football at the 1996 Olympic Games, beating Brazil and Argentina in the semi-final and final respectively. This is itself is eloquent testimony that we have what it takes to win the adult World Cup.
When we first won the under 16 tournament in 1985, many saw the handwriting on the wall. Edson Arantes Do Nascimento, otherwise known as Pele, predicted that Nigeria would soon be a major football nation. We named a road in Lagos after Fatai Atere, one of the star performers. Thereafter, virtually all the players in that victorious team fell off the football raider. What exactly happened? The inside story is that they went into retirement. We called them under 16s, but they were actually already over thirty.
Keshi's limitations
What needs to be done? In the short-term; Nigeria needs to get a world-class foreign coach. Stephen Keshi reigned supreme at the African Cup of Nations, but by the time we featured at the Confederations Cup in Brazil, he was completely out-foxed. Keshi does not have what it takes to tactically outsmart coaches like Brazil's Luiz Felipe Scolari or Spain's Vicente Del Bosque. Football matches are not only played between teams: more fundamentally, they are tactical contests between coaches. In the Confederations Cup, Keshi was tactically outplayed where it mattered. We went down to a woeful 3-0 defeat by Spain.
There is no room for misguided nationalism with regard to the choice of a coach or technical adviser. He does not have to be Nigerian. Luiz Scolari is Brazilian; nevertheless, he coached the Portuguese national team. Fabio Capello is Italian; nevertheless he coached the British national team. It is not about being nationalistically pig-headed. It is about insisting only the best is good enough for Nigeria. Stephen Keshi was good enough to get Nigeria to win the African Cup. But he is not good enough to get us to win the World Cup. To retain him without sophisticated technical backing for the World Cup is to resolve we cannot win.
There is no indication that the Super Eagles have improved since the Confederations fiasco. You don't improve by playing friendly matches against nonentity football nations like Jordan. Jordan has never ever featured in the World Cup. So what exactly was the point of Nigeria playing against the Jordanian team as we did recently? How do we explain the fact that we even lost? You don't improve by defeating non-descript Ethiopia by sheer luck as we did recently, with an Ethiopian goal that clearly crossed the line mistakenly disallowed.
Keshi is also temperamentally unsuitable for the World Cup. He is a politician. He went to the African Nations Cup without star players like Odemwingie, and somehow got away with it. But this proved to be disastrous at the Confederations Cup where it became clear that both the coach and the players lacked experience. Can you imagine Argentina going to the World Cup without Lionel Messi? A coach must be an adroit manager of men. Apparently, Keshi cannot handle the temperamental but experienced players. It is the job of a good coach to tame them.
Disbanding the NFF
In the medium-term, Nigerians of integrity need to take over the NFF. At the moment, it is yet another institution where money-grubbing Nigerians go to make money. That needs to come to an end. The onus here is not on the government to bring this about. The government is not allowed to interfere in football according to FIFA rules. The onus is on us, Nigerians, deciding that enough is enough and then mobilizing to get serious-minded people elected into the NFF. One of their jobs will then be to completely overhaul the local league. Let us bring back the glory days of the Stationary Stores. Let us get the Jide Akintundes of Nigeria excited about Nigerian football and not just the foreign English Premier League. Let us use football as a test-case. If we cannot excel at football, then we cannot excel at anything.

Original_Search_Title: Plea Bargain - Adoption of a Controversial Remedy
Headline: Nigeria: Plea Bargain - Adoption of a Controversial Remedy
Body_Text:
All is now set for the introduction of some sweeping changes in the administration of criminal justice in Nigeria. One of the expected changes is the formal adoption of the plea bargain as an instrument in the fight against corruption. Onwuka Nzeshi reports on the move and concerns surrounding its usage.
Barring any last minute changes, the plea bargain will soon become a full fledged legal instrument in the criminal justice administration in Nigeria. This is because of the ongoing moves in the National Assembly to formally bring it into the nation's statute books and make it applicable in law courts across the country.
Last week, the House of Representatives adopted the report of its Committee on Justice on a bill for an Act to repeal the Criminal Procedure Act and enact the administration of Criminal Justice Act. The new piece of legislation is designed to enhance speedy dispensation and efficient administration of criminal justice. It promises to infuse in our judicial system, a wide range of reforms including the use of the plea bargain as an instrument in criminal justice administration.
The Chairman, House Committee on Justice, Hon. Ali Ahmad, said the new legislation has a total of 494 clauses and contains new guidelines and procedures that could transform the judicial landscape. Ahmad disclosed that the bill would regulate the practice and procedure in criminal matters in all courts across Nigeria as well as replace the dual system instituted in the colonial era which has made criminal justice administration in the Northern Nigeria different from what obtains in the South.
"This is a high impact bill that is being awaited at the highest levels of the judiciary and the bar. For the first time, the interest of the victims of crime as it does not only take into account the interest of the accused. Its main objective is to address the biggest problem of delay in the administration of criminal justice. The law also addresses another intractable issue of court and prison congestion," Ahmad said.
In pursuit of its primary goal of enhancing speedy dispensation of justice, the bill seeks to impose restriction on stay of proceedings, restriction on number and interval of adjournments and inapplicability of trial de novo when a matter has been partly heard in court. It also imposes a duty on the police to electronically record confessional statements to avoid situations where statements are extracted from suspects under duress. It further provides alternatives to imprisonment such as community sentence, parole and suspended sentence for minors and first time offenders. Quite frankly, this legislation in the making is believed to have a lot of goodies but the inclusion of plea bargain in the bag is bound to generate a lot of questions.
What is Plea Bargain? It is a process that allows a defendant in a criminal case to enter into negotiation with the trial judge to make restitution or forfeit part of the proceeds of crime in exchange particularly in high profile cases of corruption. Plea bargain was introduced in Nigeria by the Economic and Financial Crimes Commission (EFCC) as part of procedures to tackle corruption and looting of public treasury.
The application of plea bargain in the prosecution of corruption charges is a familiar practice in some western countries. However, it has not been particularly a pleasant experience in Nigeria. The EFCC launched the instrument when it arrested and prosecuted a former Inspector General of Police, Mr. Tafa Balogun, for corruptly enriching himself with funds meant for the Nigeria Police. The plea bargain instrument also became handy in the prosecution of a former governor of Bayelsa State, Chief Diepriye Alamieyeseigha, who was arrested by the Metropolitan Police in London on charges of money laundering.
Although he managed to escape from London, the EFCC took up the case in Nigeria and Alamieyeseigha forfeited a number of assets said to be proceeds of crime, but has since been pardoned by the president, Dr. Goodluck Jonathan.
Former Governor of Edo State, Chief Lucky Igbinedion, and former Managing Director of Oceanic Bank, Mrs. Cecilia Ibru, were also candidates of plea bargain when the EFCC brought charges of corruption against them. In each of these cases, the fact that those accused of corruption never faced the full wrath of the law attracted a lot of public outcry.
Although the EFCC defended itself on the use of plea bargain in these cases, many Nigerians were not convinced it was the right thing to do.
Even at the moment, the perception among most Nigerians is that plea bargain is a tool designed by the anti-corruption agencies to assist corrupt public office holders escape punishment for their crimes.
As if these were not enough, the recent Police Pension Fund scam in which some civil servants allegedly looted the funds meant to pay pensioners to the tune of N40billion but did not go to jail is still fresh in the minds of many Nigerians. The suspects namely Atiku Abubakar Kigho and Uzoma Cyril Attang were said to have used the stolen funds to acquire landed property in choice areas across the country. However, in the spirit of plea bargain, they might end up retaining some of these assets.
Another sore point in the implementation of plea bargain was the case of a former head of the Police Pension Board, Mr. Yakubu Yusuf, who confessed to embezzling the sum of N23billion under his watch. The accused had pleaded guilty to the charges levelled against him and through plea bargain forfeited property valued at N325million. He was sentenced to two years in prison with an option to pay a fine of N250,000 on each of the three count charges. As soon as the judgment was delivered, Yusuf simply dipped his hands in his pockets, brought out the money and paid the fine. In the full glare of the judge, EFCC officials and television cameras, the convict walked briskly to a waiting limousine and drove away.
As expected, this perceived arrogance of the pension thief provoked instant public outcry. In the eyes of the citizenry, what the judiciary had done on that day was a perversion of justice and judicial endorsement of the looting of public treasury.
Does Nigeria Need It? These latest incidents have further brought to the fore, the arguments against the application of plea bargain and the exercise of discretion by judges in high profile corruption trials. The questions on the lips of Nigerians are: 'Do we need plea bargain in Nigeria given the level of corruption in our country'? 'Is the country ripe for plea bargain given our peculiar attitude to circumvent the law'?
Amongst lawyers, civil society groups, academia, organised labour and politicians, opinions are divided on the issue of plea bargain. Some have argued that the instrument has been in use in other countries and has helped such countries to quicken the pace of dispensing justice. However, there are those who believe that making plea bargain an integral part of Nigeria's criminal law would amount to an invitation to public servants and politicians indulging in corruption, knowing full well that they have a ready-made escape route.
The Safeguards In the light of the fears of possible abuse of the instrument, the House of Representatives has erected some road blocks on the way to plea bargain. The House Committee on Justice which crafted the bill under review disclosed that plea bargain would not be an item to be picked from the shelves at will by anybody. It must be on merit and dependent on the fulfillment of certain conditions. In adopting the controversial process, the House of Representatives had put in place, nine pillars to guard against possible abuse.
Under the new law, the plea bargain would come into play when the defendant demonstrates unflinching willingness to cooperate in the investigation or prosecution of others who are accomplices in the crime; the defendant's history with respect to criminal activity must be put into consideration; the defendant must demonstrate genuine remorse and willingness to assume responsibility for his or her conduct and there must be a pressing desire by the parties to the case for a prompt and certain disposition of the case.
Other factors that must be considered in granting plea bargain are the likelihood of obtaining a conviction at trial; the probable effect on witnesses; the probable sentence or other consequences if the defendant is convicted; the need to avoid delay in the disposition of other pending cases; the expense of the trial and appeal as well as the willingness of the defendant to make restitution or pay compensation to the victim where appropriate.
Now, the Reality Even with the safeguards, it is imperative to observe that there are still some loopholes in the use of the plea bargain. Some of the conditions appear subjective and therefore not foolproof. For instance, who determines when a man has shown enough remorse for his crimes? Can remorse really be measured or is it ascertained from the countenance of the accused?
The truth is that the law may be good but certain peculiarities in the Nigerian society may rubbish all its essence at the end of the day. It is a known fact that oftentimes, corruption assumes political and economic colours in Nigeria. It also has socio-cultural and ethnic dimensions such that one man under trial for charges of corruption might be a villain to some people and a hero to others.
Under such a complex atmosphere, the use of the plea bargain may remain a contentious issue as there could be protests when one suspect is allowed plea bargain while another is not. Would these new safeguards, for example, make the plea bargain accessible to a poor villager who invades the community ban and steals a few tubers of yam? Or will the plea bargain remain the exclusive preserve of the political elite that had stolen enough and acquired intimidating assets in Nigeria and beyond?
Is it true that the plea bargain is one sure way for some unscrupulous lawyers, judges, policemen and other stewards in the temple of justice to have their own share of the loot? It does not really matter if plea bargain works in the United Kingdom and United States, what matters is whether Nigeria's socio-cultural peculiarities, economic realities and political tendencies would permit the idea to thrive and serve the interest of justice for the common good of the people.

Original_Search_Title: Mahesh Sachdev - If Given the Chance, He'd Serve Again
Headline: Nigeria: Mahesh Sachdev - If Given the Chance, He'd Serve Again
Body_Text:
UNTIL LAST WEEKEND, MAHESH SACHDEV WAS INDIAN HIGH COMMISSIONER TO NIGERIA AND CAMEROON. HE WAS ALSO INDIAN AMBASSADOR TO BENIN and CHAD. in a chat with DAMILOLA OYEDELE, he speaks aBOUT HIS FIVE YEARS EXPERIENCE AS AN AMBASSADOR IN NIGERIA
"Oh, I am sorry, she brought your tea without asking how you wanted it; she has already put milk. Let her take this back and bring you another cup if you don't like this". This writer declined and insisted the cup of tea was okay. That was the first personality trait of Mahesh Sachdev. To those who know him, he is someone who likes to make others comfortable.
He remembers local names, and was not one of those expats who took the easy way out and shortened local Nigerian names to the first two or three syllables; he used the names in full.
Most diplomatic reporters would always recall that Sachdev was one of the not-so-many Ambassadors or High Commissioners who gave journalists easy access to themselves. It made the work of these journalists easier. He picked his calls or returns them where missed. Seasonally, he personally signs cards to thank journalists for their "hard work." As a well trained diplomat, he never refused to answer questions; rather he would modify them or carefully choose his words in a manner characteristic of his profession.
During an interview with this reporter in August, 2013, he had promised to feature on Expats before leaving Nigeria at the year's end. He made the call himself when the time was due, and he agreed to the interview in less than four hours. Sachdev arrived in Nigeria five years ago as High Commissioner and has just retired from his country's diplomatic service after 35 years. He has previously served in various positions in Syria, Hong Kong and Brussels. He served as Ambassador to Algeria, Norway and then Nigeria.
He recalls that his posting to Nigeria came as a surprise as he had never been to sub-Saharan Africa. He therefore quickly took steps to familiarise himself with Nigeria six months ahead. But in Oslo where he was at the time, there was not much information on Nigeria.
"The resources were limited; there was no Nigerian High Commission or Embassy there. There were no Nigerians that I knew," he said. He turned to books and after some difficulties, he was able to get Chinua Achebe's Things Fall Apart, and Chimamanda Adichie's Half of a Yellow Sun. From these books, Sachdev was able to establish some social picture of Nigeria dating 200 years ago.
With a laugh, he recalls: "But one thing that could not be established on the basis of books was how kolanut tasted, what the difference between yam and cocoyam was, and the difference between red Jollof and white rice, the tastes of pepper-soup, garri and others."
It was therefore with already piqued interest that he stepped on the shores of Nigeria and got to taste some these foods which he read so much about.
He describes his first brushes with Nigerian food: "One of the first lessons was that I developed great deal of respect for Nigerian advanced technology to hide the colour of your spices. Your foods, especially Jollof rice can be so deceptively spicy that even those Indians who have lived on spiced food all through their lives, whose country was colonized for the spices (among other things) cannot handle it."
"I should say the landing was very soft because English was the lingua franca, climate was benign when compared to what is in India and the society was quiet similar with regards to being in developmental stage. What you call 419 in Nigeria is what we call 420 in India. So, you are only one step ahead of us in that respect; but that shows how similar the two societies are," he added with a barely concealed smile which threatened to erupt into laughter.
He quickly settled in to his new assignment, according to him, with the mind that Nigeria had to be understood in its own entirety and integrity from a blank slate, not as India plus or minus but by itself as Nigeria.
"You cannot take economic, political or social situations in India and add few dashes here or colours there, and assume that is Nigeria. It would be false to base yourself on an external model and add or subtract something to get to know Nigeria. I have not known any other country like this and that is why your uniqueness has to be appreciated."
He got to visit some cities in the country noting that most had their own special appeal; Abuja with its ease of logistics in moving around, Kano with its own ethnic charm, Jos was nice and he liked Calabar a bit. Although he spent three days in Port Harcourt, he did not get to experience the city enough to form an opinion. His career as a diplomat accompanied by sojourns in several countries provided the extra gift of other languages: Arabic and a little French. He speaks Arabic reasonably well albeit limited to classical language.
He narrates a very defining moment of his many remarkable experiences in Nigeria. In December 2009, I received a call in my office. The man at the other end was almost hysterical. He said his daughter who was training to be a doctor in Houston was in Nigeria on Christmas holidays and was suddenly afflicted by a rare disease that lead to water accumulation in her chest, threatening her heart and lungs. She needed to be evacuated immediately to India and unless this was done in 24 hours, her life was in danger. He wanted me to give visa to the sick daughter and the mother. I told him the visa would be issued within 15 minutes of the application being made; but there was to be a condition. The voice at the other end was almost hysterical and said 'what is the condition?' I said once your daughter returns, you, she and her mother would come to have dinner in my house.
"I am sure the person at the other end thought I was either being flippant or crazy and had no difficulty in agreeing to the proposal. He just wanted his daughter to survive. The dinner at High Commissioner's house could wait.
"On my part, "the condition" was mere ruse to instill precious hope that the daughter will be back in good health from Indian hospital. Later, I learnt that while we fast- tracked the visas given the seriousness of the situation; for the same reason, the airline refused to carry her because she may die on the flight. She almost did not make it. The father was influential enough to persuade the airline to carry the patient. She went with her mother to India, was treated to health and came back home within a month. We all had dinner in my house as was promised. At my request, the young lady also wrote an article about her experience as a patient as well as a doctor in the making in India. We published it in our annual magazine called "Infinities" 2010 edition.
That experience left me wondering how many such patients would have been unable to reach Indian destinations because of delay in visas, airlines refusing to carry them etc. That motivated me to work more towards bringing Indian healthcare to Nigeria."
If given the chance, Mahesh would definitely choose to serve in Nigeria again due to what he described as Nigeria's magnetic pull once a person has experienced it. He believes he may not have enjoyed his posting if it was a more comfortable but smaller or remote country with less to do.
Now, it's time to return to Delhi, and this professional diplomat reveals what he would miss most about Nigeria. "The moderate weather; Delhi weather can sometimes be too hot or too cold. I would also miss the gregarious nature of Nigerians, relative lack for complicated personalities. For good or for bad, most of the time good, Nigerians are not multi-layered people. They do not hide behind certain thick exteriors and there is a lack of hidden agenda. It is quite easy to convince them if you have a good case. Of course, there are exceptions, but I would also miss Abuja's relative ease of logistics."

Original_Search_Title: Tourism As Viable Option for Nigeria's Socio-Economic Growth
Headline: Nigeria: Tourism As Viable Option for Nigeria's Socio-Economic Growth
Body_Text:
The economic impact which tourism has as a foreign exchange earner and job creator has made many nations of the world to lean towards the development of the sector. In this report GEORGE OKOJIE, examines the challenges confronting the Nigeria's hospitality industry and by extension, tourism sector and proffer ways out of the woods
When the Manila Declaration on World Tourism in 1980 tipped tourism as an activity essential to the life of nations because of its direct effects on the social, cultural, educational, and economic sectors of national societies and on their international relations many observers and economic pundits had thought that Nigeria will tap into the potentials it offers to diversify its economy and emerge as one of the big economies of the world.
Amid the enormous resources, cultural heritages and rich tourist sites the country is endowed with the realities on ground after three decades of that declaration seem to suggest that the wisdom, commitment and political will to explore the opportunities is lost in the scheme of things in the country.
Is it the land mass of different flora and fauna, the naturally occurring structures, a wide range of cultures with attendant ceremonies, or the festivals, artifacts and craft, cuisines, and activities that can entice both domestic and foreign tourists, name it.
Nigeria boasts of the globally known Osun festival in Oshogbo and the Ojude Oba festival in Ogun State, the Igue festival in Benin, the Kwanghir Masquerade festival in Benue State and the Argungu Fishing festival in Sokoto State to the Igboukwu New Yam festival in Anambra State, the Katsina Durbar and the Calabar Dance festival, to mention few that are enough to put the country on world tourism map.
$4bn Domestic Tourism Sector
With quite a number of fundamentals vital to the development of the sector still lacking in the country, experts are skeptical that the aims to grow the domestic tourism sub-sector of the nation's economy into a US$4 billion industry may be elusive. More so that assurances by the management of the Nigerian Tourism Development Corporation to establish nationwide travel hubs as well as one-stop tourism centres in the six geo-political zones has remained theoretical.
On this note, observers say the picture of what could be achieved in the nation's tourism sector seems more gloomy in the face of the present challenges, because no leisure travellers, whether domestic or foreign will visit countries like Nigeria that boasts of high caliber of kidnappers, religious terrorists and generally unsafe modes of transportation.
This fact coupled with the reality on ground that only countries that spend heavily in the development of her tourism sector reaps bountifully economically tend to rule out Nigeria's chances. Statistics showed that such countries that boosted their economic prosperity include China reaping a whooping ($102 billion), United States ($83.7 billion) and Germany ($83.8 billion) annually.
Available statistics also showed that, the most visited countries by other nationals in 2012 include France (83 million) people, the United States (67 million), China (57.7 million) and Malaysia (25 million). Since Nigeria has nothing substantive to offer to attract tourists, the figures from the World Tourism Organisation showed that, only 715,000 tourists came to Nigeria's shores in 2011, less than half of the 1.5 million that came calling a year earlier, and a far cry from the over one million since 2006.
The statistics further revealed that between 1995 and 2011, Nigeria's highest earning from tourism was $688 million in 2008. Secondly, international tourism income was reportedly a meagre 0.72 per cent of Nigeria's total export earnings in 2011. Worst still according to the WTO ranking, Nigeria does not feature at all in the top 10 tourism destinations of choice in Africa where Morocco and South Africa lead and Zimbabwe and Kenya follow in that progression.
Bill Clinton's Prediction
Despite all these fundamental posers, informed stakeholders have said the country can be a tourists' destination. As far as they are concerned it all depends on the determination of the leadership of the country to make it happen. Towing this line of reasoning, the 42nd President of the United State of America Hon. Bill Clinton having seen efforts by the Lagos State Government and private sector to do what many people felt was impossible, threw his weight behind efforts to make Lagos a world tourists' destination.
The global eminent personality said at the dedication of the 5,000,000 square metres reclaimed land of the Eko Atlantic City that when completed will bring people all over the world to study the technology and pattern used in building a city of such caliber. Clinton specifically said, "The Lagos Government has reclaimed five million square metres of the land initially lost to the sea.
"That marks the beginning of this amazing wall, they have kept their commitment and it will work to improve the economy of Nigeria. All over the world it will bring enormous opportunities. I am convinced that within five years people will be coming over the world to see this wall", Clinton said. When they eventually come like former American president predicts, is the country prepared to receive them?
Abundant Job Opportunities
That is the question Bayo Odunlami, Director Milan Industries asked LEADERSHIP Sunday in an exclusive chat recently shortly after the launch of Intercontinental Hotel, located in Victoria Island, Lagos. Odunlami lamented that despite the building of the first five-star hotel reputed to be the tallest hotel in West Africa with 23 floors, trained Nigerians in hospitality and tourism management were not available to take up employments in the sector.
Despite the hues and cries about unemployment in the country, Odunlami who pointed out that he was equally working in about seven big hotels across the country noted that there were no trained Nigerians to fill the vacancies. He said it was high time Nigerians get serious and get trained in Tourism management rather than taking to the riding of commercial motorcycles to make quick money.
Odigie Amos, a hotelier told LEADERSHIP Sunday that a basic qualification in Hotel and Catering Operations and Management, Fast Food Operations and Management, and Night Club guarantees a job in the hospitality sector. He said, "It is expected that an ideal tourism worker should be one that have the ability to work with all kinds of people from foreign tourists to people with disabilities and must be friendly. That is why some training is needed so that after completing their programme, they could work as Tourism Officers/Managers.
"Some management abilities such as organisation and delegation skills are required of personnel in this sector and they must also possess good memory and positive attitude towards people. Workers here must also possess effective communication/language skills as well as enthusiasm to interact with people and social etiquettes", Amos stated. He affirmed that the career prospects of graduates of tourism cannot be over emphasised, saying the private and public sectors are good playing ground for such graduates.
While encouraging Nigerians to rise up to the challenges of filling the vacancies in the sector, Amos said job opportunities await them in government establishments like (Ministries, State and Local Government Tourism Committees), private-oriented outfits ,airlines, tour operators, travel agencies, hotels, and transportation sector.
Need for manpower investment
Corroborating this view on the need for huge investment in the sector, tourism and hospitality expert from the Institute of Hospitality, United Kingdom, Professor Peter Jones said there is urgent need to invest more in the training of hospitality management experts in the country. He emphasised that investment in manpower training in the sector will enable the country to compete with the rest of the world, even as he stressed the need to expose the nation's industry professionals to globally acknowledged trends in the sector.
Jones noted that the country with its huge opportunities and resources, should not be found wanting in the new trends meant to shape the industry as was being practiced all over the world. "The training today is looking at different goals and trends in hospitality, both from the industry point of view and education point of view. And so, we are trying to pick our own example and practice across the world and how that influences the way we develop educational training that the industry needs to meet the need for the future.
"We need to become very comparable with what is going on with the rest of the world. So, you've got to see yourself as being part of the international industry and not Nigeria's industry because if you see yourself as just being Nigeria, you are not going to be in a competitive position." The expert remarked that the hospitality industry, being the sixth largest in the world, contributed immensely to economic growth of every nation.
According to Jones, "There is absolutely a huge connection between economic growth and the hospitality industry and tourism. If you look at what has been going on, hospitality and tourism is the sixth largest industry in the world. So, it is a huge industry. "For Nigeria's hospitality industry and tourism to compete favourably, there is the need to invest in the people."
Enabling Environment
Harping on the need for an enabling environment for there to be positive impact on the economy, frontline tourism practitioner and elder statesman, Chief Mike Amachree urged the Federal Government to tackle the menace of the Boko Haram sect in the country. Amachree noted that tourism and leisure business across the country would be a thing of the past if the insurgence of the Boko Haram was not checked by the apex government, saying incessant attacks by the sect may pose a threat to development in the sector.
No matter the investment in tourism sector, he emphasised that with Boko Haram, foreign and local tourists would avoid travelling to zones that are noted for attacks causing tourism operators in such areas to be frustrated out of business.
On the need for infrastructure he said, "No amount of carnivals or festivals will bring tourism to Nigerians. All what we need are tourism infrastructure that can attract people from outside the country. All I am saying is that mere parade of masquerades or horses, or even young men and women in foreign attires will not promote tourism.
"Our governments at federal and state levels must begin to think about erecting permanent and elegant structures that are capable of attracting people from other countries. Examples of such structures are The Pyramid in Egypt and the Wild life Park in Kenya. We too can have these monuments that people would come and see rather than for Nigerians to be travelling to see them in other places."
Meanwhile, the Minister for Tourism, Culture and National Orientation, Chief Edem Duke has continued to assure the citizenry at any given forum of the resolve of the Federal Government to revitalise the tourism sector transformation agenda of the President Goodluck Jonathan government. As the years roll by, whether these assurances from the minister would end up in physical developments and economic prosperity of the country or remain mere political propaganda, Nigerians are waiting to see.

Original_Search_Title: Displaced Umuahia Market Traders Relocate to Streets
Headline: Nigeria: Displaced Umuahia Market Traders Relocate to Streets
Body_Text:
Umuahia — Many traders displaced from the Umuahia main market following its closure four days ago have relocated to major streets in the state capital.
The market was relocated to Ubani Ibeku, near Umuahia, by the state government as part its efforts to give Umuahia a facelift.
The affected traders, who still loiter around the market along Umuwaya Road, include squatters and petty traders, who usually displayed their wares within the market area and along the rail line.
A cross-section of the traders told reporters on Friday that the relocation of the market had crippled their businesses and caused untold hardships to their families.
According to them, a shop goes for N400, 000 for traders who owned shops in the old market and N500, 000 for those who did not own any.
They, however, claimed that they did not have the money to make installmental payment of N120, 000 per shop at the new market.
Mr Innocent Ogbu, a second-hand cloth trader, said the relocation was laudable, but "ill-timed".
"Government should have allowed us to finish the Christmas season before relocating the market.
"As you can see, my capital is not up to N120, 000, so we are in a state of uncertainty over the relocation", he said.
Corroborating Ogbu, a yam dealer, who identified herself simply as "Nkechi", said that the relocation had worsened her health condition as her wares were parked in an open place in her residence.
"My health condition has worsened since Monday because of the closure of the market.
"I have no money to get a shop in the new market and above all, the new market is not ready yet so I parked my yams in my compound.
"Government should have waited until the new market has been fully completed so that traders who paid for shops at Ubani Ibeku will move straight into their new shops", she said.
Mr Anya Ndukwe, an undergraduate, whose mother was affected by the order, said that the relocation posed a serious economic challenge to his widowed-mother.
Ndukwe said that his mother depended on her petty trading to fend for the family and his university education and that the situation would affect their means of livelihood.
"The market is our only source of livelihood and now my mother's wares are dumped in our small apartment. We find it difficult to survive.
"If the universities reopen today, I will find it difficult to go back because my mother cannot afford the money need to return to school", he said.

Original_Search_Title: Is Communal Eating Fading Away in Our Homes?
Headline: Nigeria: Is Communal Eating Fading Away in Our Homes?
Body_Text:
Communalism is a concept which emphasizes togetherness, solidarity and unity among the people. It is the principle of living together, sharing interests, possessions and responsibilities rather than individual affairs.
Communal eating involves several people sitting together, to eat from the same plate or table and share available drinks among themselves. This goes to create a picture of unity, spirit of togetherness and strengthens relationships among the people.
Communal eating has been the tradition of many ethnic groups in Nigeria especially, the Tiv people of Benue State and constitute significant percentage of the population of other states like Taraba, Nasarawa, Niger, and Plateau among others.
The kindred head of Ukum (Ortaregh u Ukum) in Ananum community, Donga Local Government Area of Taraba State, Chief John S. Aondona popularly called Zaki John God, told our reporter that the act of people eating and drinking together is a practice that should be cherished and sustained in the interest of unity and peaceful-coexistence.
"In Tiv land, we believe in unity of purpose and togetherness. People who don't eat together with their brothers in our land are regarded as selfish people who don't want others to eat their wives' food. We have enough food and we eat together to appreciate what God has given to us as a people and to show that we are united," he explained.
Zaki John God said eating together builds a sense of togetherness, unity, solidarity and inculcate moral virtues into the younger generation and how they can identify with the tradition and tenets of the Tiv nation.
He added that women are also saved from the challenges of dividing food into several plates for the huge number of people in the families and reduces their burden of washing and buying plates all the time.
