Also, Adebayo Aremu, an octogenarian, believes that communal eating is important despite the experiences of modernization and civilization which has cut across all the strata and fabrics of the society.
He said communal eating provides a sense of security and cohesion among the people and should never be relegated to the background.
He maintained that communal eating unites the people and helps in nurturing the younger ones or children in families on its benefits.
He argued that it has positive impact on children's values, motivation, personal identity and self discipline.
Similarly, a medical practitioner, Mr. Daniel Onu Chukwu who noted that communal eating should not be allowed to fade off in homes extrapolated that it encourages healthy eating habit, and provides a model for children to carry into adulthood.
He also elucidated that the younger people who eat from the same plate with their elders are likely to comprehend, appreciate, cherish the boundaries, expectations and the traditional standards set for the overall development of the culture of the people.
Furthermore, a civil servant, Mr. Unom Samuel Shiaondo, told our reporter that communal eating is important in many ways and must be encouraged among the diverse ethnic groups in the country to promote unity in diversity.
He explained that communal eating provides the opportunity for the family members to bond, love one another, share a unifying experience, discuss their likes and dislikes for certain kinds of foods and determine the kind of food they want to eat next.
Unom stressed that communal eating motivates many people to take in more quantity of food than if they were to eat alone adding that when people eat from the same plate and share the same drink, it provides them social relief, happiness and rekindles their sense of brotherhood.
However, Mrs. Grace T. Johnson, who said she is familiar with the communal eating culture of villagers and urbanites observed that the act of sharing food from the same plate by quite a number of people is stronger in the villages than in the cities.
According to her, people who live in cities like Abuja, Lagos, Kano, Port Harcourt among others prefer eating foods like rice in separate dishes and water from different cups but when it is other foods like semo or 'eba' they eat together from the same plate.
"I live in Abuja with my husband. I learnt that the culture is not actually fading away but in terms of drinking water from the same cup, it is completely different in the city. In our villages, you discover that people eat from one big dish and at the same time share water from a big cup but here in the city, everybody has his or her own cup, so that is just the difference," she said.
While it is argued in some quarters that the communal eating culture is gradually fading off in many homes, Terlumun Akura Igo, a man from the Tiv extraction argued that communal eating is still valid in the Tiv land despite what he termed "new age".
According to him, the Tiv have a unique tradition of eating from the same plate of food with their brothers, children, uncles, relations and even wives and sharing the same water.
He said that he knew a Tiv man from Tor Donga in Katsina- Ala local government area of Benue State who married 53 wives and had hundreds of children.
He said about ten women used to cook simultaneously in that family, producing basins of food for the family members at once, stressing that the man's children, brothers and other relatives sit round a basin of food and share it together.
He noted that the man who got married to these women (young and old) later died but left his family members united and they still eat together from the same basin of food now.
Kehinde Jaiyeola a Yoruba man, said he was very surprised to see the communal eating culture among the Tiv when he visited a Tiv community in Nasarawa State.
"When I went to a Tiv community in Nasarawa State, a woman cooked pounded yam and before you know it about 12 men sat round in a resting room and when the food was brought, they ate it from the same plate and shared water from a big cup, I was surprised," he said.
He explained that when the water was used up by the people eating the food, the woman who cooked the food was called upon to bring more water in the same cup to contain all the people sharing the meal and when the soup got finished she was also called upon to bring more for people to eat.
It is gathered that sometimes, children who constitute part of the communal eating return the plate to the woman who cooked the food to put more food for the people to eat. Men sit in one place while women organize themselves in another direction eating together and sharing drinking water from the same cup.
Mr. Agaba Oche, a bus driver, told our reporter that eating together is gradually fading away in homes and especially in the cities while in the villages, the culture of communal eating is still cherished by the people and most especially in Benue State.
It is very lucid that communal eating is important in uniting people, strengthening relationships, discussing their likes and dislikes for food, enhancing the eating behavior of the people, promoting love, inculcating discipline into the younger ones, educating one another on the cultural practices and communal eating culture of a people and appreciating God among others.

Original_Search_Title: Nkubu Food Hawking Ban
Headline: Kenya: Nkubu Food Hawking Ban
Body_Text:
Nkubu sub county public health officer has banned hawking of food in town. Paul Kimathi told a public meeting attended by the area MP Kathuri Murungi that he had made the decision to prevent an outbreak of disease.
"There are so many people selling food that is uncovered in the open and it's dangerous to the consumers' health with risks of cholera and typhoid," Kimathi said.
He was booed and shouted down by the vendors who threatened to walk out of the meeting. Among the foodstuffs slapped with the ban were peeled fruits, sausages, mutura, roasted maize & yams and porridge.
"We know they are unhappy with this directive but that's the law and people have to stick to it because we have to safeguard the health of everybody. We are not going to relent until they adhere", said Kimathi.
He called upon the leaders to get on board the campaign and look for means to assist the food vendors to sell their items without endangering other people's lives by designating them to specific places which will provide sanitary services like hand washing and ensuring the food is not exposed to dirt.
However the food hawkers were displeased with the directive and called upn the MP and County Rep Nick Mburugu to intervene and have the order reviewed so that they are not left jobless.
They complained that the public health was not fair in effecting the ban because it was their only source of living and instead the leaders should set a place for them to sell their stuff instead of banning the sale.
"We are like refugees in this town because neither the public health nor the council is giving us solutions despite our attempts to talk with them and paying the fees required", said John Muchiiri a representative of Nkubu Roadside Self help group.
Their ward rep Mburugu cautioned the departments involved in the exercise against ambushing the residents without warning or sensitizing them on any action and laws to avoid conflict.
"The officers should inform the community on the reasons they are arresting the people and make them aware of the rules and regulations that they should adhere instead of making it political which makes a simple procedure seem ugly" said Mburugu

Original_Search_Title: Fashola, Oshiomhole Say High Population Hampers National Devt
Headline: Nigeria: Fashola, Oshiomhole Say High Population Hampers National Devt
Body_Text:
Lagos — The Governors of Lagos and Edo states, Babatunde Fashola and Adams Oshiomole respectively, have identified the need to tackle poverty by addressing Nigeria's population growth.
The duo, who expressed concern over Nigeria's spiralling population, decried that it was taking a toll on governance while also reducing quality of lives of Nigerians.
Also as Nigerians and politicians anxiously await the 2015 general elections, Governor Babatunde Fashola of Lagos State and his Edo State counterpart, Adams Oshiomhole, yesterday, advocated the need for a woman president in the country, saying time has come for the country to be ruled by women.
Oshiomhole, however, said that the current face-off between the Academic Staff Union of Universities, ASUU, and the Federal Government was a pointer to the leadership challenge confronting the country.
The governors spoke, yesterday, in Lagos, at the flag-off of the 12th annual national conference by the Committee of Wives of Lagos State Officials, COWLSO.
Fashola emphasised the need for women to begin to make conscious efforts towards cutting down on reproduction.
"You have both the yam and knife. We can only have a real chance to solve the problem of poverty if we slow down on our population.
"If two in three persons are already living in poverty in our world, what chances exists for those who are coming? Poverty should rather be reducing to one in three!
"In 1970, London had a population of about 8.5 million people while Lagos had 1.4 million people. Today, the city of London has dropped to 8.1 million people while Lagos has grown to 21 million! Is our land growing? Are our water resources increasing? Those are the resources we need to sustain life!", Governor Fashola said at the conference tagged 'See Beyond Today: Enhance the Quality of Life.'
Admitting that an input must occur before any output, Governor Adams Oshiomole advised men in the country to devise every possible strategy of easing population reduction, even if it requires medical intervention, adding, "we are about a quarter of all the black people around the world, but as men, let us be more concerned about the quality of this number."

Original_Search_Title: Black Man's Dilemma
Headline: Nigeria: Black Man's Dilemma
Body_Text:
There is an increasing sense of objectivity and realism amongst African writers and leaders. A remarkable African writer who is widely regarded as factual and constructive, is the Nigerian called Chief Areoye Oyebola. In his book, Black Man's Dilemma, published 1976 in Nigeria, he made some of the most realistic and objective analyses of the problems of black Africa and the Negro race to be found anywhere.
According to him, there are three intriguing facts about the black race: They are facts which according to him, most black and "coloured" people and some liberal whites would prefer not to face honestly and squarely. "One of such fact is that no black country has ever made a breakthrough in modernity. The second intriguing fact about the black race is that it was the only race in history which had between 14.6 million and 20 million of its members physically transported as slaves from Africa to completely new area. Equally strange was the active support which the African chiefs and middle class gave to the capturing and transportation of their fellow blacks to America."
The third perplexing fact, according to Oyebola will undoubtedly arouse the resentment of my black and coloured brothers and sisters. "Relying on the fact that the different races of the world came into existence at the same time, I have come to this sad, but valid conclusion that the black man has made little or no contribution to world civilization, as we have become fully convinced that the much vaunted black civilization of ancient Ghana, Shongai, Mali, Zimabwe, Husa/Fulani, Yoruba, Benin and other areas were quite inferior to the civilization of other races."
Oyebola regards the writings of Western liberal scholars and black historians on the greatness, glory and achievements of ancient African kingdoms, empires and emirates, as a deliberate morale booster for the dehumanized black race.
In the book, the Author points out that many western liberals are aware of the fact that white countries have a lot to lose if the black man faces the reality of his situation and accepts the fact that he hadn't a past that he can be proud of: "The whites have a lot to lose if the blacks henceforth work hard to place their membership of the human family on the basis of genuine equality. White experts publish books about our past glory, grandeur and achievements. They've got willing disciples among black intelligentsia who have lost the candour of any critical self-searching. The absurd view has been expressed that colonialism and slavery led to the death of black Africa's cultural heritage. A culture can only die if a whole people can be exterminated as the Romans did in the annihilation of cartage."
One other problem of the black man, according to Oyebola is that he is today not aware of his basic human weaknesses and problems: "The black Man's Dilemma is simply about the problems of the black race, one based on ingenuine equality.
Nowhere in Africa can one find the type of heritage I have seen in Europe and Asia. And the intriguing questions persistently crossed my mind are: what efforts were my black ancestors making when such great architectural, technological and scientific achievements were taking place in Europe and Asia? Did my black ancestors achieve any original and durable form of political and social organisation comparable to those written records we see in Europe, Asia, and North Africa?
"My answer to each of the questions raised above is in the negative, and I go further to say that had there not been the 20th century means of communication by sea, air, telephone, radio, television, video, newspapers, internet, the shrinkage of the world as it is, things in the tropical lands would have continued to be as they were during the previous one thousand years. The concorde alone shakes man more to the reality of life than is possible by any other thing. A letter written in Paris and put into the Concorde would arrive Los ngeles destination on a date before it was written. "
The book, a bomb which exploded in 1976 under the sophisticated nostrils of Nigeria's ivory tower historians saw bows and arrows squatting in all available dark corners against poor Oyebola's jugular veins.
Oyebola laments that we are incapable of dealing a death blow on the mosquitoes that takes such a big toll on our lives, but he is more hurt still by the fact we cannot invent a machine to pound the yam which only we eat.
"Nothing is more soporific than the tropical climate. You don't have to bestir yourself too much in tropics. Your land lady hands you a notice to quit your lodging. You can pass the next few nights outside, under the Eko Bridge. You even get more fresh air there than you rented room provided. Whereas to be driven out of your lodging at whatever time of the year anywhere in the high latitudes, you have to think and think fast, on how to get an alternative accommodation, or you get exposed to biting winter or cold and stinging rain or high winds."
The Author is afire with righteous indignation, and he wonders what we had been doing to have remained so pedestrian as a nation. It is attitudes like Oyebola's that can fillip our country, Nigeria, from its position of time-honoured stagnation and sterility to one of self-recovery and purposeful march towards acceptable universal human progress.
The Author raises hard questions which challenge the nature of the black society, its long-standing values, beliefs and institutions. Oyebola is concerned about continuing growing foreign domination, even after independence, and feels there is white conspiracy, imperialistic influence and sabotage at work designed to maintain white supremacy on a neo-colonial basis. One is particularly impressed by the repeated plea for increased discipline at all levels.
Areoye must be congratulated for his courage to have written Black Man's Dilemma. There is no doubt that he feels strongly about the well- being of the black man on contemporary earth. In this book, he attempted to say loud and clear what he believes to be the root cause of contemporary Negro situation.
"That the black man is usually seen as somewhat different in a negative way by other races is not new and that he has suffered as a result of misconceptions and outright falsehood is also not debatable. The problem is that the black man sometimes sees himself like others see him.
"From eminent philosophers such as Aristotle and church fathers such as Origen and Nobel laureates such as James Watson, the "problem" has always been how to put us down or at least "define the black man's place in the scheme of things". An Egyptian historian Al-Abshibi once wrote that when the black man is hungry he steals and when sated he fornicates.
"Other Arab writers (Al-Farabi and Avicenna) did not think too highly of blacks, despite the clear injunctions of the Islam to treat all people equally. This is probably one of the premises for the oppression of Sudanese blacks today with the active connivance of its central government. But let us take this issue in context. I have lived long in Nigeria and have been around the world a bit also. There is this saying that "no one can make you inferior without your consent". Without fear of contradiction, I dare say that the black man probably more than any other race on the planet has been the most oppressed and dehumanized. But he has probably contributed to his sorry state to some extent despite the predation and subjugation from outside (European and Arab) the continent.
"Today the mindset of some of the other races - despite the "progress" made in science and the arts is that we are sub-human. The question however is not what others say about us but what we say about ourselves. The abject backwardness of Africa despite its unspeakable riches is a testament to the fact that something is not entirely right with us as a race. Let me say right now that I do not for one moment believe that the black man is in any way inferior to other races but we must also ask ourselves why we tend to be the object of scorn and derision by the rest of the world. When you consider the senseless wars, coups, ethnic and religious clashes that have engulfed the continent over the decades you are bound to ask where we got it all wrong.
"A recent edition of Forbes magazine listed some of the dirtiest capitals in the world and African cities were predominant. Governments on the continent act like a cabal of predators, destroying the common wealth and stealing their nations' blind while appealing to petty nuances that do not stand to logic and reason. We are not a continent of contented and happy men but more like an overgrown colony of quarreling monkeys. And yet, I think we have the greatest chance to change that view and outlook in our time.
"History teaches us that the black race was first in arts, science, philosophy and an appreciation of the transcendent, irrespective of what current educational and 'scientific' nuances suggest. Black history can be traced from ancient civilizations in the interior of Africa to its apogee in the desert of ancient Egypt. "Today, the average black African has no connection to, or appreciation for what he is or what he was unless someone from outside of the continent educates him and this allows for mischief. When the white man says yes, we say yes and when he says no we agree with him."

Original_Search_Title: Gunmen Kill 80-Year-Old German Nun in Delta
Headline: Nigeria: Gunmen Kill 80-Year-Old German Nun in Delta
Body_Text:
An 80-year-old German missionary, Rev. Sr. Marcelina Alfra, popularly referred to as Regina Mundi, was attacked and killed by unknown gunmen in Ogwashi-Uku, in Aniocha South Local Government Area of Delta State.
The German-born nun, who had lived for over 50 years in Ogwashi-Uku and carried out philanthropic and humanitarian works, was reportedly attacked in her Ogwashi-Uku residence by the hoodlums, who invaded her room through the roof and slashed her with machetes in the head.
She was rushed to the University of Benin Teaching Hospital where she died.
Addressing a press conference, the Obi of Ogwashi-Uku condemned the act, lamenting that "some dissidents" in the community were behind the violence and killings that have engulfed the once peaceful community since his installation as the traditional ruler.
He said: "It was last Thursday, after I had performed the traditional rite of eating the roasted new yam to signal the New Yam Festival, that the news came that unidentified gunmen went into the room of a German reverend sister, who had lived here for a long time. She resisted and they inflicted machete cuts to her head. She was taken to UBTH where she died shortly. We have just been informed that another person died in the attack," Obi Okonjo narrated.
He told journalists that 'the dissidents' had orchestrated an atmosphere of violence during the annual Ine Festival, in which someone was killed, an incident that would have degenerated to a bloodbath had it not been averted.
Prof. Okonjo prayed God to forgive them for "they do not know what they do", adding that ignorance and lack of exposure to civilisation have kept certain people in the community backward.

Original_Search_Title: Atebubu-Amantin Assembly MP Support Foryawo Festival
Headline: Ghana: Atebubu-Amantin Assembly MP Support Foryawo Festival
Body_Text:
The Atebubu-Amantin District Chief Executive Mr Sampson Owusu Boateng together with the Member of Parliament MP Mr Sanja Nanja have jointly made a donation to the traditional council in support of this year's "Foryawo" yam Festival.
At a brief presentation ceremony at the Omanhene's palace the two parties jointly presented two bottles of schnapps and an unspecified amount of cash to the Atebubu traditional council in support of the festival which ends on Sunday October 13th 2013.
In a brief remark, Mr Owusu Boateng called for an elaborate and timely planning of the festival in collaboration with the Assembly in order to attract government functionaries and also market the district.
He said contrary to what some will want people to believe, he has a very cordial working relationship with the MP which has culminated in the delivery of nine power transformers to the district which among others will help solve the water problem faced by the people living in Atebubu and its environs.
Mr Sanja Nanja on his part recounted the circumstances leading to the allocation of the transformers adding that they were an allocation under Self Help Electrification Project SHEP 4.
He added that he was also working to secure some assistance for the laying of the main transmission pipelines to ensure the people enjoy uninterrupted water supply.
He promised to assist the DCE in every possible way to ensure the development of the district.
The President of the Atebubu traditional council Nana Owusu Akyeaw Brempong II welcomed the suggestion of the DCE and bemoaned the traditional council's inability to attract decision makers to the festival over the years.
He urged the DCE and the MP to unite for the development of the district.
Source: ISD (Daniel Oduro-Stewart)

Original_Search_Title: New Market Changes Wandegeya Business
Headline: Uganda: New Market Changes Wandegeya Business
Body_Text:
President Yoweri Museveni has said that ownership and authority over markets in Kampala should be under Kampala Capital City Authority and not left to vendors.
He says the idea of leaving markets to be run by vendors is not sustainable.
"If you give markets to sitting tenants, how about those who will come later? Markets should stay in the hands of KCCA so when vendors leave, others come so that vendors continue to pay rent and leave developing and management to government and KCCA," he said.
He made these remarks while commissioning the new Wandegeya market, in that city suburb that hardly goes to sleep. Local Government Minister Adolf Mwesige said Shs 21 billion had been spent to develop the market. Government borrowed $70 million to develop markets. Museveni will also commission markets in Fort Portal, Hoima, Jinja, Mbale, Lira and Gulu.
Mwesige said government needed another $21 million to develop markets in Busia, Tororo, Soroti, Moroto, Kitgum, Arua, Kasese, Mbarara, Lugazi, Ntinda, Kansanga, Nakulabye and Kasubi. The new market will not only change Wandegeya town's skyline but also redefine the way business is conducted. The market, which will accommodate more than 1,200 vendors, will house food stalls, restaurants, banks, salons, fruit and vegetable stands, butcheries and a day care centre.
For starters, customers are assured of better sanitation and hygiene. Two years ago, the market was sitting in filth and garbage. Floors were muddy and whether it rained or not, the market was always dirty with pools of water and mud blocking walkways. Flies filled the market and food stalls as vendors dumped their garbage wherever they wanted.
But now, the whole market is cemented with drainage, toilets and clear dumping sites for plastics, foodstuffs and medical waste. The management and maintenance of the market has also been left under KCCA to ensure sanitation and orderliness. The market has also been organized in such a way that vendors dealing in the same item will be sitting on the same floor.
For example in the South wing, butcheries, fish stands, foods stands for fresh food like matooke, potatoes, yams will sit on the first floor. The second floor will be occupied mainly by traders dealing in fruits and vegetables. The third floor is for lockup shops dealing in dry foodstuffs like beans, millet, rice, posho while the last floor houses a day care centre.
In the North wing, the first floor is the main kitchen for food vendors and customers will sit in restaurants on the second floor. Jennifer Musisi the Executive Director of Kampala Capital City Authority (KCCA) told The Observer that priority to occupy will go to vendors of the old market who signed a memorandum of understanding (MOU) with KCCA.
"All the people who were here are coming back as long as they signed an MOU with us. We are going to sort out those who do not have MOUs but wish to rent stalls. We will rent out the stalls at a commercial rate," Musisi said.
She says completing the market has been a battle because many politicians fought its development.

Original_Search_Title: MTN Adds Glamour to Asogli Yam Festival 2013
Headline: Ghana: MTN Adds Glamour to Asogli Yam Festival 2013
Body_Text:
The 2013 Asogli Yam festival was celebrated amidst pomp and pageantry, which saw the display of the rich culture and heritage of the Asogli State on Saturday 21 September 2013.
The festival also coincided with the 10th Anniversary celebration of the installation of Togbe Afede XIV as the Agbogbomefia of Asogli State. The rich cultural display at the durbar grounds demonstrated the unique cultural heritage of the Asogli state.
As one of the sponsors of this year's Asogli Yam festival, MTN organised various activities such as MTN Football Gala and Chill Zone to provide entertainment for the over thousand patrons who had traveled from far and near to celebrate the festival.
There was a football gala organised between eight teams within the Asogli State, and the winner won a coveted trophy of GH¢400 and MTN souvenirs. The Depot team won the MTN Football gala after a penalty shoot-out' with the Asogli team.
MTN also supported the festival activities with the organisation of a durbar of the chiefs and people of Asogli State at the Ho Sports Stadium. The beauty of the grand durbar was the display through the various types of rich and colourful kente adorned by the traditional rulers and people at the durbar grounds. MTN added to the beauty of traditional colour display with the provision of MTN's Y'ello canopies and tear-drop flags. In addition to supporting the durbar, MTN further added to excitement of the Asogli Yam festival with the famous MTN Chill Zone entertainment events, with performances by local musical artistes such as Edodzi, Hector and '247'. The energy and excitement from the performance kept patrons dancing until the wee hours of the morning.
Before the festival celebration, MTN made a donation to the Asogli Traditional Council to support its activities. MTN presented items, comprising of a cheque of GH¢5,000, MTN recharge vouchers worth GH¢1,000 cedis, a drinks hamper and MTN souvenirs. The items were received by the Paramount Chief of Kpenoe, Togbe Kotoku XI, and members of the Festival Planning Committee.
Presenting the items to the chiefs and elders of the Asogli State, Mr. Peter Bimpeh, Mobile Money Implementation Manager South East Business District of MTN, expressed his excitement about the relationship that had existed between the Asogli State and MTN. He also used the opportunity to formally congratulate Togbe Afede XIV on his 10th Anniversary as the Agbogbomefia of the Asogli State.
In his response, Togbe Kotoku XI, on behalf of the Asogli Traditional Council, thanked MTN for the kind gesture, and was hopeful that it would go a long way to support them, and also hoped the relation would develop further in the coming years.
He also urged MTN to expand the support to include the building of a school to commemorate Togbe Afede's 10th Anniversary celebration. Short URL: http://thechronicle.com.gh/?p=63861

Original_Search_Title: Between Governor Amaechi and Cassius
Headline: Nigeria: Between Governor Amaechi and Cassius
Body_Text:
These are not the best of times for our dear country. What is happening to Nigeria is not the desire of anybody. We had hoped that by now Nigeria would be among the best economies on earth.
Nigeria of our dream should have at least two Harvard grade universities and dwarfing the exotic infrastructure in the Abu Dhabi enclave. And with the surging activities tumbling over each other on planet earth, both positive and negative, Nigerians just want nice things here. I don't wish for less. But there must be a sacrifice.
It is a trite behavioural pattern to heap the blame on the president of the country. We easily look up to him, and the first question that comes to mind is, why are we not enjoying? This is the freshwater Rotimi Amaechi swims in.
It is like Cassius who tried to convince lay Romans and, in fact, Caesar's best friend, that Caesar is incurably ambitious.
Climatic conditions even favour his designs; the ides of a time-march. Amaechi is striking the President at the ides of 2015. Amaechi came to be governor of Rivers State through strange circumstances.
I am not questioning it because of my stance in supporting the sanctity of the judiciary. He craftily struck when the metal is hot. He has arrogated to himself the responsibility of being the mouthpiece of the South-south on President Goodluck Jonathan's perceived infraction in the Niger Delta.
He is on an expedition to belittle the ultimate prize South-south won by being the occupant in Aso Rock. That was a bit sentimental from me. My dad, mentor, and proud son of the land being in the villa as the president. Maybe that was why I decided to put pen on paper to write off Amaechi. That is precisely not the case.
To drive that point home, every human being has a bit of sentimental syrup in him or her. For us to gloat over the election of Barack Obama and term him the pride of Africa, blacks, mankind is the height of sentimentalism. Having thrashed the issue of sentiment, I can be excused to feel sentimental over an achiever.
Amaechi seems nervous that his profligacy with state funds would soon be found out, and he cleverly devised a means to divert our attention. He acquired a sophisticated jet with its procurement papers routed in questionable circumstances.
Why would he or any other Executive Governor expend about $50m plus to acquire a state-of-the art jet when he could have channelled such money to areas the president, in his opinion, has neglected in Rivers State. His theory that the region, which has their son at the helm of affairs, has been neglected is unfounded.
Pray, Amaechi, what wouldn't N9.5billion do in a state that it was paltry enough for you to divert to purchase a jet? He is insisting that the president must put the South-south on the pedestal of development; that is actually a long way for the president to guillotine himself without qualms.
In his opinion, Mr President is not doing anything, yet his co-musketeers in the north are saying that he is carrying out projects. His drummers in the north say they want the president out because GEJ has forgotten them, who were born to rule (sic) and he is instead littering developmental projects in Rivers, Bayelsa and environs.
Yet Amaechi, their cell of conquest in the south, is saying a different thing. Amaechi, wake up from your slumber. It is apparent that Amaechi and them have parted ways in terms of conception.
Nigeria's president is not the one that pumps a community with projects while other areas clap for him. If you do, you would be in trouble and if you don't too, you would equally court trouble. Even at that, I believe God would grant the president the wisdom to develop every region in Nigeria.
Rivers State earns about N20billion monthly, apart from revenues and sundry income from multinationals domiciled in the state. Did I hear myself say N20billion? Yes N20billion.
Apart from that, internally generated revenue from other sources brings the state's income to about N25billion. This is again about the highest since Nigeria was amalgamated in 1914 when Rivers State moved progressively from a district to province to its present state.
But the significant thing to note here is that some states in Nigeria who get about 10% of that amount get more mentioned in infrastructural development than Rivers State. Is that not the eye with a beam attempting to see a moth in President Goodluck Jonathan's eyes? If I were Amaechi, I would usurp the federal government's duty in Rivers State in terms of infrastructural development and go to jail at the end of the day.
Good, illegal duty is tenable in Nigeria because Nigeria is the kindest country on earth where the federal government is saddled with primary school education, primary healthcare and other frivolous government parastatals that Chief Orosanya sought to quash but did not succeed because we are in Nigeria.
Amaechi has suddenly become an advertisement expert that would make coca-cola green with envy. He employed 13,000 teachers and then went to the marketplace to advertise it.
Employment letters are usually given in offices where government businesses take place. But he went to town to tell the whole world the favour he is doing to the people of Rivers State.
That sounds like Cassius attempting to hoodwink Romans. Amaechi scored mighty points with this open theatre of mass employment in the teaching service for once in his many years in office. Note the significance of catching this straw now that he is drowning. No drowning no employment.
It is strange that when the President courted civility by calling for a marking scheme of his administration, the opposition said it was impossible for him to have performed.
But the opposition, especially those who want GEJ's seat, are scoring Amaechi very high for fighting the President. The people of Rivers State expect him to develop their state and not to overheat the polity.
Going by the reality in Nigeria, no President had ever performed. For sectional, religious, ethnic interests, etc, nobody is capable of performing.This is the albatross on Jonathan. On a personal note, I think there were other presidents in the country who did very well but GEJ is doing better. Rivers State has the capacity to build refineries, international airport, seaport and other mega-structures. They can even fund community policing and many others.
His cry of victimisation now that we are in the ides of 2015 is like a music sung by the Seraph and the Cherub to some elements in the north who are born to rule.
He broke a gentleman agreement and precedence to re-contest the chairmanship of NGF. This is a position that has never been subjected to contest before, the chairman is picked through consensus and after two years the person quits. But Amaechi re-contested and, in fact, supervised himself into becoming chairman a second time.
Since he was always attacking the President, he easily found allied forces in the stable of the All Progressives Congress (APC). I advise Governor Jang-led NGF not to shift ground.
If anyone is aggrieved, he can seek redress in the law courts. Since he fights the president, he has garnered support from an itchy media whose stock-in-trade is to carry news at variance with the Presidency. He has also gleaned support from desperate politicians.
Presently, he has succeeded in getting an indefinite sabbatical leave for the State House of Assembly. What a way of short-changing the state.
If he claimed only five members of the State Assembly were against him, why is he so uncomfortable with such microscopic few? Truth is, the state doesn't want him for fighting the president whom they are pleased with. And with the State Assembly in session, he would have been history by now. He trumpets loudly that the police are after him. He made a mountain out of the molehill of the police stopping him from accessing his official residence.
The police were only acting on court order that has restrained nPDP from constituting a nuisance in the society. In flagrant disobedience of the court, they opened an office just a stone throw from his office.
If Celestine Omehia had disobeyed a court order with the police backing where would he have being today? The secret service was said to have restricted the movement of the President of the United State of America during the bombing of the World Trade Centre.
He was literally 'kidnapped' to Air Force headquarters, the maximum garrison on the planet. All that restricted the movement of the most powerful man on earth - yet for security reasons - and for the pride of America.
How much more should the police in Nigeria act in protecting the judiciary, the hope of the commonman. The Commissioner of Police only acted out of the order from above, the court of law.
APC has lost so much heat recently. For APC, it is a case of a babe who cooked for her would-be husband a delicious dish and instead of him getting the table to devour it, he goes around looking for snacks to eat. APC had hoped for instances like this in the PDP when a G-some from PDP would migrate to her table to the pounded yam she had prepared.
The boyfriend goes about looking for snacks such as nPDP and PDM. This should be a great embarrassment to APC. In fact, a rejection.
Now who can rescue ANPP and some APGA elements from this cesspool? PDP, a mega party, sustains herself through hard work and a corridor of internal dissension to give her members a sense of belonging. Let us reason this out. Governor Fashola of Lagos State deported some Nigerians whose identities have been traced to Anambra State. Then there was no impending election in sight in the state.
But it was soon discovered that an election was drawing near and APC would be taught a lesson of her life. Fashola quickly apologised for the sake of Chris Ngige and not to Anambrarians. We should remember that Ngige whom the state brands as a sleepy senator actually defended Fashola for the deportation.
This is an insult to the Ndigbo nation. Truth is, Ngige who is fixated to the leadership would never disagree with them even if their action undermined and embarrassed the people of Ndigbo.
It is apparent that the apology came for the sole reason of winning the governorship election in Anambra State. May we not forget that Fashola did not deem it fit to apologise to Rauf Aregbesola, the heir of Bola Tinubu political machine.
The reason there is no need for Fashola/APC to apologise to Osun State is because Osun is a manor of Lagos. It has been captured and vassalled to the lords of Lagos. Of course, there is no strategic gain to apologise to the other states. We don't know what nPDP wants.
They started from lack of internal democracy in the PDP. They sprinkled it by voicing a secret pact GEJ signed that he would not contest in 2015. They then migrated to a demand that Okonjo Oweala be sacked.
Before we could comprehend them, they started saying it was the petroleum minister they meant. They then went after His Excellency, the Vice President, Arc. Namadi Sambo.
Their grouse with him is that he is too loyal to the President. Again, they have also gone after Bamanga Tukur, saying he is too dictatorial.
As a matter of fact, we are waiting for them to come out clearly and tell us what exactly they want.

Original_Search_Title: Originally From Lebanon, Nigeria Is Now Home
Headline: Nigeria: Originally From Lebanon, Nigeria Is Now Home
Body_Text:
Ahmed Kochen has spent 25 of his 43 years in Africa. Although he grew up in Senegal, Nigeria is now his home.
Ahmed's easy going attitude came through during the telephone conversation to fix an appointment for Expats. "Since you do not really know Katsina, I can send my driver to pick you up," he offered.
As he sat through the 23 minute interview, his responses were interjected with bouts of laughter even when he narrated the only scary experience he has had in Nigeria since he moved five years ago.
Sometime ago, Ahmed, alongside his driver were heading for Abuja from Jos where he resided at the time. They departed at 2.30pm and calculated that the journey would not exceed the usual three hours.
Unfortunately, halfway to Abuja, a broken down truck had blocked the road and made it impossible for vehicles to pass through.
Everyone joined efforts to see if they could remove the truck, but after two hours they gave up and carved a makeshift passage through the bush. They continued to Abuja and were approaching Keffi.
"They were dressed like police officers, but it was immediately clear that they were not policemen because they blocked the road. There were a couple of cars which they already stopped and the people were lying on the ground. It was scary, we could not go back. My driver asked me what to do, and I told him to reduce his speed so that we can think," Ahmed said as he burst into laughter.
Then a small van shot past them at breakneck speed and drove through the road block. "My driver using his reflexes just followed the van at top speed also. Apparently there was a tiny opening in between the logs they used to block the road; it could barely accommodate a car.
He lost control after he sped through, I just told him to relax, and take his foot off the accelerator, and he managed to regain control of the car after that. He is a very strong guy, that was how we escaped," he added.
They finally arrived in Abuja at about 9pm. This isolated incident did not deter his love for Nigeria; he however took the decision to always set out for his road travels in the morning hours.
The decision to move to Nigeria in 2007 was an easy one for Ahmed. He grew up in Senegal and often visited Nigeria with his father who was exporter of beads.
He therefore considers himself more African than Lebanese even though he also lived and worked in Lebanon for a while. He was running a successful decoration business in London when his brother, Mohamed (already featured on Expats) started Zen Construction and invited him to join the business; he took up the offer without any second thoughts.
Since he arrived Nigeria, Ahmed has lived in Jos, Kano, Abuja and Kaduna, overseeing projects being executed by the firm. "There were already bad press about Nigeria by then, but that did not bother me at all. Since 2007, I have not even gone to England.
During my holidays, I go to Lebanon or some other countries. I have many friends in England, but I do not regret that I moved here, I do enjoy my life here much more than there," he said.
He explained further that living in Africa is by choice. "If you like something, you would enjoy it, being inside it or being with it. If you like Africa and are in Africa, that means it is what you want, it is your choice.
I am not forced to be in Africa, I choose to come here, I choose to live here, most of my life, I was living in Africa, so I am used to the life and I like it," he added philosophically.
He has his fluency in Wolof (the official language of Senegal and Gambia) to prove his love for the continent. He has also started to speak Hausa which he started to pick up after moving to Katsina a year ago.
"Due to the cosmopolitan nature of Jos, Kaduna and Abuja, people do not speak Hausa to each other, so it was difficult to learn the language in those places.
I did not stay in Kano for very long. But here in Katsina, lots of people do not speak English, including my driver. So I am forced to learn Hausa, and I like that."
The security situation in the country has howver slightly reduced the rate of hangouts for Ahmed and his friends. Now, they mostly hang out in each others' houses especially after the kidnap of a French man in Katsina.
He still loves the city and its residents though describing them as some of the kindest people he has ever met.
"The people of Katsina are some of the kindest people I have very met in the world. I am not saying they are the best, but if you can find so much kind people in such a small city, then it means it is a good place."
Adapting to the Nigerian society therefore has been very easy. He still visits Jos for the fun of it, and has also visited the popular Yankari Games reserve. Obudu Cattle Ranch has been pencilled down on his 'to do' list.
Unlike many expatriates, Ahmed professes a love for what he described as the charm of Lagos. He spent one and half weeks in the city just for the experience.
"In all of my earlier visits to Nigeria, I never went to Lagos, and I heard a lot about it. My father did not have anything to do there so he never took us there.
It is a very busy city, but I liked it. The traffic jam is horrible of course, and I do not like traffic jams, but it has its own charm, it is a likeable city.
His favourite Nigerian food is the roasted yam and sauce; the roasted yam sold at the roadside because he believes the women make it better than his cook would do in the hygienic comfort of his kitchen.
Amidst bouts of laughter, he said "I buy it by the roadside; they make it better than we make it in the house. These people are professionals, the make it the exact taste that I like. In the house, my cook roasts it too much."
He also likes Suya which he recalls is called dibin hausa in Senegal, and pounded yam with fish soup. Ahmed Kochen was born into a family where integrity matters a lot.
In business, he learnt from his father to stay away from people with dubious characters, he has applied that lesson in all his dealings.
He studied construction in College and also had lessons in Gemmology, the study of gemstones. This writer underwent a crash course in Gemmology.
"There are a lot of stones rarer than the diamond; the ruby for example is more expensive and rarer. In diamonds, some colours are more expensive; red, green, blue and the pink. But ruby is more expensive than all these. Diamond is easy, coloured gem stones are more complicated," he explained.
But construction remains his first love. For him, it is like artwork, and the taste of the designer must be reflected in his unique designs. " If you want to do something, you should do it properly, to do it properly, you need knowledge," he added. Ahmed Kochen does not see himself moving from Nigeria.
he has visited several countries and while he liked many of them, there was no place he wanted to settle down in. He has merged easily into the Nigerian society, and as far as he is concerned, he has arrived home.

Original_Search_Title: Okonjo-Iweala's Father Denies Murder Allegation
Headline: Nigeria: Okonjo-Iweala's Father Denies Murder Allegation
Body_Text:
The Obi of Ogwashi-Uku in Delta State, Professor Chukuka Okonjo, has denied the allegations that he was the mastermind of the violence, which led to the death of a teenager, one Prince Pentty Chukwu, in his community.
The monarch, who incidentally is the father of the Coordinating Minister for the Economy and Minister of Finance, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, was reacting to a media report which allegedly fingered his palace as being the mastermind of the violence, that lead to the death of Chukwu.
He said such comment was not only ridiculous, but a calculated attempt aimed at bringing the image and reputation of his palace into disrepute.
Okonjo, in a statement issued yesterday by the spokesman of the palace, Mr. Okolobi Lawrence, also distanced the minister and the palace from the unfortunate incident, stating that the minister was in New York at the time of the incident.
The statement entitled: 'The Real Facts about the Recent Violence in Ogwashi-Uku: A Statement by the Palace', described the allegations against the minister and her father as ridiculous.
"Our attention has been drawn to a report published by an online medium in which a member of the usurper Izediunor family in Ogwashi-Uku made several reckless allegations, including that of murder against the esteemed Obi of Ogwashi-Uku, Professor Okonjo and his daughter, our Princess, Dr. Okonjo-Iweala. The allegations are as false as they are ridiculous," the statement noted.
The statement further added: "From information available to us from the State Security Service (SSS) and the Police Force, this tiny section (Izedunor) attempted to disrupt the last Ihaaji traditional festival (Yam Roasting Festival).
"They mobilised some youths and unleashed violence on helpless market women and others in the vicinity in a bid to stop sons and daughters of Ogwashi-Uku and their guests from exercising their free right to attend and partake in the traditional festival. They attacked them with firearms and injured several of them.
"The Joint Task Force (JTF) in the state responded to the distress calls and had no choice than to intervene to avoid a deterioration of the situation. The police and the JTF were able to dislodge these criminals after a brief gun duel, arresting several of them.
"Unfortunately, a youth involved in the violence planned and executed by these agents of destabilisation lost his life. It is indeed sad that a young life would be snuffed out because of the actions of conscienceless people.
"But we cannot allow these elements to have their way. We welcome all peace-loving sons and daughters of Ogwashi-Uku and their friends to attend the upcoming Ine Festival starting on October 9 to observe first hand, the rich tradition of the kingdom and its peace-loving citizens as arrangements have been made to ensure a more secure environment during the celebrations."
It was also alleged that those injured during the fracas included Princes Kingsley Udeh, 22, and Bollus Izedunor ,23, as they were said to be sitting at their father's house (palace), when the soldiers came in two trucks, and in an attempt to run for safety, were shot.

Original_Search_Title: Letter to Nuhu Ribadu, or Reflections On the Destiny of a Mosaic Generation
Headline: Nigeria: Letter to Nuhu Ribadu, or Reflections On the Destiny of a Mosaic Generation
Body_Text:
Mallam Nuhu Ribadu, although you are a Moslem, your cosmopolitanism is such that you surely are familiar with one of the most famous biblical aphorisms about the truth. "Ye shall know the truth, and the truth shall set you free," declares our Galilean friend and apostle of Jesus Christ, John Zebedee, in the biblical book which bears his first name. Freedom, that which is actuated by the truth, came, for me, in the form of a reluctant admission of one grim, sobering, and solemn fact about the destiny of my generation (40 - 60 years-old) in the Nigerian equation.
I was set free the day I decided to accept a friend's Facebook advice that some countries are not meant to be good, fair, and just in the lifetime of particular generations of their citizenry. I was set free the day I decided to live with the fact that, as that friend of mine put it, some countries are meant to serve a higher and nobler purpose by being bad, corrupt, unfair, unjust, self-destructive, and permanently dysfunctional as a lesson to others who may point at them and learn how not to be a country. Another bosom friend of mine, one of Cameroon's most prolific novelists, recently told me, also on Facebook, that Paul Biya's favourite refrain is: "tout, sauf le Nigeria!" Anything but Nigeria! And that from the mouth of one of Africa's open sores! Paul Biya can tolerate the idea of Cameroon being as bad as anything except Nigeria! When you serve as an example of how not to be a country for a buffoon serving as life president of one of Africa's most wretched banana republics, you can hardly sink lower but I don't trust Nigeria.
I was set free the day I decided to accept the fact that I am unlikely to see in my lifetime the Nigeria that my pen and intellect have been fighting relentlessly for all these years. I was set free the day I decided to accept the fact that, although many of the intellectual warriors fighting for and envisioning a new Nigeria, and rejecting Nigeria as defined by Goodluck Jonathan and the corrupt brood of vipers he leads in the political class, are members of my generation, ours is a generation that should look in the mirror and see Moses starring back at us. I am talking about that lonely, forlorn figure on the heights of Mount Nebo, the location from whence his eyes beheld the Promised Land whose earth his feet would never kiss. I was set free the day I accepted the fate of Moses: I may be capable of deploying the power of my imagination to "see" the Nigeria that I am fighting for, it is highly unlikely that the real thing will happen in my lifetime. Given Nigeria's life expectancy, I'm in fact already in the ranks of lucky citizens functioning in injury time.
So, why I am still fighting, having accepted these liberating truths about my fate as a Nigerian? The answer is simple. I have to die fighting to ensure that the generation after me will not hand over the Mosaic fate to those coming after them. I have to die fighting to ensure that the Nigerian babies we are making as write will not hit their forties, fifties, and sixties writing Mosaic epitaphs for posterity. I have to die fighting to ensure that those coming after me will fight a much shorter battle and stand a much brighter chance of seeing a Nigeria that is fair, just, humane, functional, and developed in their own lifetime. This battle is, of course, in the long run, to be fought in the domain of the bigger picture.
Today, we have to contend with the short run and the smaller picture of the everyday mess that is Nigeria. That is where the problem lies. The everyday mess of Nigeria comes with certain realities that you and I had better wake up to, Mallam Ribadu. The smell of the coffee is so strong that I'm surprised you're not choking on it. I have watched with considerable admiration this past week your continued efforts to rid our land of corruption. You took the battle to James Ibori in a London court room. And we were reminded of that unbelievable episode of the $15 million bribe you rejected from the Homo Corruptus of Oghara. That, alone, makes you the Nigerian of the century. It may take another century before we encounter a Nigerian who would throw $15 million back in the face of a corrupt bribe giver.
You have, of course, been very talkative. You have said a lot of things about James Ibori. You have said a lot of things about Mike Okiro. Ibori and Okiro are of course two corrupt jackasses and there is nothing you are saying about both men that Nigerians don't already know. What you seem to have forgotten is that Nigeria is the political paradise of corrupt jackasses. Nigeria is where a jackass Presidency makes it a point of duty to rehabilitate, recycle, and reward corrupt jackasses; where the system awards nine lives of political relevance to corrupt jackasses and they are recycled back into the system as kingmakers, elder statesmen, and shapers of public opinion. Think of Diepreye Alamieyeseigha. Think of Bode George, the ex-convict now running his filthy mouth all over our newspapers.
Going to 2015 and even 2019, this is the Nigeria that we've got. I am saying in essence that 2015 and 2019 are part of the short run and smaller picture of Nigeria's everyday mess that we must deal with. With the kind of money they have in a place like Nigeria, we could very well wake up tomorrow and see a political landscape with Mike Okiro and James Ibori featuring as Godfathers and shapers of political destinies. Nobody with the amount of money that Ibori and Okiro have stolen is ever irrelevant in Nigeria. We are hearing stories of companies still making remittances to Ibori. He is still in charge of the treasury of Delta state via his cousin, Emmanuel Uduaghuan. That is a lot of corrupt money, the sort of corrupt money that the jackass Nigerian state and the jackass Nigerian system reward with political relevance, prison or no prison.
Perhaps you are running your mouth against these two corrupt jackasses because, unlike me, you are yet to make your peace with the possibility that ours is such a hopeless system that both men could become political players in 2015 and 2019 irrespective of their current stations in life. This is why I must ask you a question I want you to think about very seriously: if things happened the Nigerian way and Okiro and Ibori (from prison) suddenly became very serious players in the 2015 and 2019 chess game, would you stand your ground and maintain everything you have said about both men this past week? We've been down this road before with Asiwaju Bola Tinubu and Patience Jonathan. I don't want to awaken the ghosts of recantation. I don't want to relive the sordid spectacle of a man of honour and integrity suddenly swallowing previously uttered words like balls of pounded yam red carpeted through the throat by egusi soup with orisirisi.
I was part of the Nigerian Village Square (www.nigeriavillagesquare.com) editorial board team that interviewed you in the build up to 2011. We had some very interesting interview sessions with a good number of presidential aspirants. We interviewed General Buhari, Dele Momodu, and Governor Bukola Saraki. Then came your turn. We in the panel had all your statements about Patience Jonathan and Tinubu in google printouts right there in front of us. It was very painful listening to you as you tried to heehaw your way out of statements you made to the nation. You screamed and swore and denied and denied and denied again. No, you never said any such thing about Tinubu's corruption. Patience Jonathan is corrupt ke? E gba mi o. When and where did I say it? You attributed everything to the handiwork of mischievous and disgruntled elements.
Now you are back in the public square screaming that two seriously moneyed figures who, thanks to our jackass political system, could be back as serious players and makers of political destinies, are corrupt thieves. Yes, Okiro and Ibori are corrupt jackasses and irredeemable thieves - two essential requirements for political relevance and leadership in Nigeria. But you do understand that you have considerable followership in Nigeria and many look up to you as an essential player in the Nigeria of our dreams.
Personally, I do not see a path to Nigeria's future that would exclude somebody like you. However, whether you will be in the position to play the role I believe destiny wants you to play in the life and future of Nigeria depends on you and your mouth. If you can keep that mouth consistent on the track of denunciation of corruption without shameful recantation at the first smell of political opportunity, I think you will last long in the business of working for Nigeria's future and fulfilling the hopes and aspirations of your teeming followers and admirers. If, however, the political destinies of Okiro and Ibori changed in 2015 or 2019 and you hit the airwaves heehawing that you never said the things you are saying about them today, the two men may yet stand on their mountains of corrupt money and point at the ashes of your reputation as warning to those who attempt to rubbish corrupt jackasses in Nigeria, the political paradise of corrupt jackasses.

Original_Search_Title: I Promoted Unity, Peace Through Broadcasting - Yusuf Ladan
Headline: Nigeria: I Promoted Unity, Peace Through Broadcasting - Yusuf Ladan
Body_Text:
Alhaji Yusuf Ladan, who holds the traditional title of Dan-Iya in the Zazzau Emirate Council, is the district head of Kabala in Kaduna State, and son of the first Comptroller of Radio in northern Nigeria, Alhaji Muhammadu Ladan (MON).
Yusuf followed the footsteps of his father and became a broadcaster. As if the profession runs in their blood, his son, Abubakar yusuf Ladan, is now a staff member of the Federal Radio Corporation of Nigeria (FRCN), Kaduna. Yusuf Ladan is the eldest out of 27 children, including the late Group Managing Director (GMD) of the Nigerian National Petroleum Corporation (NNPC) and Cigarin Zazzau, Alhaji Shehu Ladan. The Dan Iya spoke to Sunday Trust on the twists and turns of broadcasting, as well as other issues.
Tell us more about Alhaji Yusuf Ladan
I was born in 1935; I am 78 years old. I was admitted into the primary school in 1943 in Zazzau, otherwise called Zaria. I was given admission to Middle School in 1947. From there I went to Kaduna Trade Centre with the intention to train as a builder. But I became sick and was hospitalised for a long time, so the principal advised me to go back to the Middle School. I went back; and after graduation I was employed by the government of northern Nigeria. I started working in the Health Department but later transferred to the Ministry of Education in Kaduna. After a year, I was transferred to Ilorin Training College as clerk-in-charge.
I later went to read Administration in the Institute of Administration, now Ahmadu Bello Universtity, Zaria.
When there was a circular that workers were to be posted near their places of birth I seized the opportunity to request for transfer to Kaduna. I was then ordered to be auditioned for a programme in education by the Radio section of the Ministry of Education. The white men gave me a role of moderating the programme. And there was the plan to establish a Radio/TV station in Kaduna, known as the Broadcasting Company of Northern Nigeria (BCNN). I showed interest.
I started as a librarian in charge of gramophone and cartridges in the technical college, now Government College, Kaduna. I was there for one year. I was the one who established the library at Independence Way in the present FRCN, Kaduna. My deputy was Ladan Kontagora.
I was later transferred to the studio as announcer and promoted to the rank of junior producer. Before then I was producing "Yara Manyan Gobe." I also produced programmes on religion, culture and music. My boss, Alhaji Adamu Gumel, took me to Sultan Bello Mosque to record tafsir, conducted during Ramadan by the late Sheik Abubakar Mahmud Gumi. I sought for his permission to start recording his sermon, which he agreed. That was between 1963 and 1964.
When we realised that the Quran is the leading Islamic religious book we substituted the "Wazin Musulunci" with "Tafsir." The programme was for 30 minutes but later increased to an hour. When Sheik Gumi realised that the limit for the tafsir was one hour he adjusted his time to the same one hour.
Christians programmes are: "Waazin Kirista" or Christian sermon was also running. We also brought "Wakokin addinin kirista" and "Wazin tsakiyan mako."
On culture, I started writing drama and invited musicians to Radio and TV for recording. I was sensitive to educate, entertain and enlighten the audience. I fostered unity and peace.
May we know more about your father?
My father's name is Alhaji Muhammadu Ladan, the late Dan Iyan Zazzau, who I succeeded. He was educated in Provincial School, Zaria and Higher College in Katsina. As clerk and translator in the Northern Regional Government, he went to the Cambridge University to teach Hausa. He was later given a job as a broadcasting organiser in NBC. A white man Mr LesLie was the comptroller of the Northern Region. My father succeeded him.
He was given the national honour of Member of the Order of Niger (MON). My son, Abubakar Yususf Ladan is now a broadcaster.
Who were your classmates?
Justice Yusuf Ibrahim. The late Dangaladiman Zazzau, Alhaji Abdulkadir Balarabe Musa and former Secretary to the Government of the Federation (SGF), Alhaji Gidado Idris.
How did you get to the Kaduna State Broadcasting Corporation (KSBC)?
I was assistant director in the FRCN when Katsina State was created out of the old Kaduna State, and the General Manager (GM) of KSBC, Aminu Abdullahi left for Katsina. I was then asked to take the post by the Kaduna State government on secondment. I never thought I would ever work outside the FRCN.
And after a year, I realised that the people were lacking moral. They were mocked as "the radio house near a yam market." The staff members lacked moral because of lack of welfare. When I joined they were moving to the present permanent site known as Muhammdu Ladan House in Wurno Road, Ung Sarki, Kaduna. I influenced members of staff to cultivate the zeal to work.
What were your achievements?
I tried to douse the tension created as a result of the fact that other languages were not included in the station's schedule. I spoke to Governor Dangiwa Umar on the need to allow the English and Hausa Languages as many people not understand Hausa, he agreed.
Why does the corporation's building bear your father's name, Muhammadu Ladan?
Those that contributed to broadcasting were being forgotten; so the management recommended that people who shaped the broadcasting industry in the North be honoured.
My father was the first comptroller in the Northern Region, so when his name was suggested, Governor Tanko Ayuba agreed. The one in the market was to be called Chindo House, but that was not realised as they now call it Kufena House.
When did you retire?
I retired voluntarily in 1981.
Who were your superiors?
Mr Lesli, Alh Adamu Gumel, Alh Sani Kontagora was the Programme Adviser. Abba zoru was the Head of Radio Section. Sani Kontagora was in charge of Programmes and later TV section. Saka Aleshinlowe was the head, News Department. Alhaji Balarabe Musa was secretary/chief accountant. He was my childhood friend and he later became the executive governor of Kaduna State.
Who were your subordinates?
Ibrahim Ahmad, Yaya Abubakar, Lawal Yusuf Saulawa, Hassan Suleiman, Khalifa Baba Ahmed, Abubakar Abdussalami, Bashir Samaila Ahmed, Halilu Ahmed Getso, Abba Gwadabe among others.
What is your advice to people in the industry now?
Radio is the bridge between those in government and their followers. Government has developmental programmes, so the media should continue to sensitise the populace. And people are free to go to radio stations and air their views in news or programmes. Also, radio journalists should be honest and reliable. They should not practise for the love of money; they should serve the society.

Original_Search_Title: CBN Should Support Micro-Finance Banks to Perform Better - Dr Eche
Headline: Nigeria: CBN Should Support Micro-Finance Banks to Perform Better - Dr Eche
Body_Text:
Micro-finance banks have been renowned for their contribution in growing the nation's economy through strengthening small and medium scale enterprises. In this interview with UCHE UDUMA and CHIBUNMA UKWU, the managing director of Danels Global Micro Finance Bank Limited, Dr Wilson Eche, lays emphasis on the need for micro-finance banks to have where to run to for intervention funds
What is your assessment of the new cashless policy initiated by the CBN governor?
That is a laudable programme; the less cash we handle in the society, the more funds the bank would have in their box. A society that is cashless; what that does to the society is that the bank is made more liquid, in quote. The dangers of carrying cash from one place to another is eliminated. It even becomes faster to transact business, but the basic thing we need to do is to give it infrastructure in terms of electricity. ATM cards should be seen to be working. The government also has to ensure that there are consequences for bounced cheques. That is when we will truly achieve the aim of cashless society. But the bottom-line is that the less cash we handle, the better it is for the economy. But one thing is that, with the way Nigeria is structured, we rely more on cash; we trust cash more. Thus, we need to educate the populace on the benefits of cashless policy before we enforce it, so the government will have to go that extra mile telling people of the need for cashless society. And that comes to making the infrastructure that will make it work very effective. For example, if I wanted to withdraw money from the ATM, I should not have problems with withdrawing money. I also should not have problems with customers accepting my cheques or my Mastercards. Otherwise, we would probably go back to square one.
There have been lots of criticism from the masses following this cashless policy, making one to wonder if Nigeria is actually ripe for this cashless policy?
In advanced countries, I do not think there is a limit to how much one can withdraw from his account. If the transaction is suspicious, fine and questions could be raised about it. For instance, I have $1million in my account and I write a cheque to withdraw $1million; of course, as a banker, you have the right to ask why I would be withdrawing so much. But that is in a society the infrastructure work. A society that is used to cashless society. So, we do not just import somethings and put on ourselves, but one thing that is important is that we must start somewhere. So, the way they are starting is okay, but I will admit that there is an issue to the limit to how much one can withdraw. Since Nigeria is a cash-based society, I do not think that withdrawing N150,000 per transaction is enough for a day's transaction. What if I want to buy a thing of N300,000 or N1million? The way our society is structured, if you buy something for N300,000 and you give someone a cheque for N150,000, the seller would prefer that you pay in cash. He might even decline receiving a cheque, and that is because there is the tendency for the cheque to bounce, and nothing will happen. There are people who even issue cheques when they know they do not have money in the account, and there is no repercussion for such actions. So, unless the government is able to enforce those laws, that is when the idea of cashless society will be successful. But by and large, it's a good thing.
Micro-finance banks are renowned for encouraging financial inclusion of those excluded from financial system. What achievement has your bank made in this area?
We were privileged to be the first generation micro-finance, and there have been challenges. In my experiences so far as a banker in a micro-finance bank, I have come to see that it is easier to assist the less-privileged through the micro-finance banks more than the commercial banks. If the function can be truly streamlined, that is the process in the micro-finance banks. When you come to assessing maybe little loans of N50,000 or N100,000, you will agree with me that it will be cumbersome for a bank like First Bank of Nigeria to package a loan of N100,000 for somebody. But at the micro level, which is the micro-finance bank, it becomes such easier to advance such loans with speed, without taking too much time from the customers. So, we have been doing our best to assist as many customers as we have, but one major target we have here is the graduate entrepreneurship scheme. It's a scheme that we have, through which we try to assist graduates. By this scheme, any graduate in FCT that has a business plan, but lacks the funds to start it, would get assistance from us if his business plans are certified okay by us. We give them what we call a loan, which they pay at the end of one year. But the truth is that, if a loan should be more useful to any graduate, the tenure has to be from three years. But there is a good news that CBN is coming up with; a plan of assisting the micro- finance banks, and I am sure that if they see it through, it will go a long way in assisting the unemployed, the market women, and the petty traders who for long have been our focus.
Can you tell us the contents of the plan by CBN to assist micro-finance banks more. How do they intend to do that?
When they launched it, it was said that some customers would be able to assess some funds from the CBN through the micro-finance banks. But they have not showed us clearly how to do the assessing. So, we are still waiting for the processes to take to do this. But that will go a long way in helping micro-finance banks to serve more customers better. However, the bottom-line is that Nigeria is a country that has intelligent people, most of whom cannot carry out their dream because of N100,000. This could kill somebody's dream, and due to the fact that we raise this money from the market, we are not able to reach out to as many as we would. But I believe that with the arrangement of CBN to assist micro-finance banks, we will be able to extend the services to other customers, especially the graduates.
How much has your bank disbursed to small and medium enterprises in the past two years?
This year alone, we have been able to disburse N25million for the graduate entrepreneurship scheme. These are funds we gathered from our customers, and we are able to extend them to graduates alone that have good business plans. But we are conscious of the fact that when CBN comes to examine our books, they take into accounts how long those funds have stayed without being paid. So, we try to make sure that we do not expose ourselves, because they are doing their job by ensuring that we operate according to the rules. However, the truth is that if you give a graduate a loan of N1million to be paid back in a year, from experience, I have seen that they usually have difficulty paying back in one year. It's not as if they do not want to pay the loan back, but the various processes that go with businesses hinder them. So, if we follow them with that heat, because CBN is coming to balance our books, there will be fraud, as they might just abandon the project; and we, on the other hand, will not get our money, thereby making the aim totally defeated. So, over the years, we have been carrying this provision. For example, I will give you a loan of N100,000 for three months. This, we do in a bid to encourage them, because these are people we come in contact with frequently and we also know the efforts they are making. So, the best we can do for them is to encourage them. And sincerely, some have started paying back gradually. For example, in three months, we have been able to recover N7million from part of that loan. So, if we can have more encouragement from the government, then I will assure you that the micro-finance banks will be able to reach out more to the unemployed and the graduates in particular.
What kind of encouragement do you want from the government?
You see, if we say poverty is a disease, and HIV is a disease, it is obvious that the government will go out to attack HIV more. In same way, if we agree that poverty is a disease, then we will need the government to attack poverty the way they attack HIV by setting up such CBN funds and making them assessable to micro-finance banks. Do not give the money to unemployed for free. Rather, give out the monies as loans, and increase the duration. It will last with them. And also reduce the interest rate, because the micro-finance banks, cannot go out and get money at 10 per cent from a customer, and you also expect us to lend it out to a graduate at 10 per cent as well. So, if government wants the micro-finance banks to truly help the graduates, then they should make these funds available to micro-fiancé banks at a very cheap rate. They can even peg it; that they are giving the money at two per cent. So, it could be lent at four per cent. But not when I go out to borrow money at high cost, they will expect me to give it out to graduates at low interest rate. It is not possible, because for micro-finance banks to remain in business, it must be profitable. If it is not profitable, the business will not grow.
And what happened to the money CBN launched recently for business women?
The money was launched as small and medium skills fund. It was launched by the CBN governor, but till now, the micro-finance banks do not have the modalities of assessing the funds. However, the aim of that fund is targeted to encourage small businesses. Stakeholders will have to apply through micro- finance banks, and the funds will be for three years, which is good. The fund, which was launched last month, is about N220million and it was specified that particular percentage should be to encourage women entrepreneurs.
Despite the presence of micro-finance banks, there have been cases of high interest rates. Why is it so?
Foremost is the cost of fund. High interest rates do not encourage the growth of businesses, and that is why it is very few businessmen that will take loans at a very high rate. That is one of the reasons the loan growth is the way it is. But I am sure that there is a little bit of loan growth, because for a bank to make money, it must give out loans. It's just that because of the cost of funds, they do not have much to give, and they cannot cover much because the government funds are not there. And that was what I meant when I set the issue of HIV as an example. If the government wants to tackle poverty like they would tackle HIV, they should make these funds available, which they will give at a lower cost to micro-finance banks that have the capacity to reach out to small-scale people than the commercial banks.
Has your organisation been able to help people in small-scale businesses?
Yes, we covered the market women whom we grouped into groups of five. These are women who are into egg, yam and orange selling. We have over 3,000 of them, whom we give loans that are minimal. One thing we discovered was that these women do not owe. We do not look for them. Before the 15th of the next month, they have come by themselves to pay. So, if these CBN funds come, that would help us to help them better.
How do you recover your money when a customer does not pay back?
It might interest you to know that when it comes to paying back loans, we do not have issues with small groups of market women. They come on their own to pay. Where we do have problems are with people who come to take loans of N2 million. These are people with suits and ties. They come to the office stressing how they need the loans to execute one contract or the other, and at the end, they have difficulty paying back the loan. So, what we do for this group is that we ask them for collaterals. And we make sure that what they give as collateral is something valuable to them. When we first started, we made mistakes. We just believed it was a micro-finance bank and would give loans of N500,000 with the belief that they would be gentlemen and come and pay back. But if you check our books, you will see that we got our hands burnt. So, rather than taking N1million and giving it to just one person, we prefer to give it to these market women.
