Though what we make from them is minimal, but at least, we will be sure of getting our money back.
What are the other challenges that micro-finance banks face?
Another challenge that micro-finance banks have is where to run to for intervention funds. There is no bank in the world that will be able to withstand it if all their customers should come same time to collect their monies, and this is where micro-finance banks usually have problems. So, there is need for banks to follow their ratios, so as to be able to manage their cash well. But the micro-finance banks, because of their sizes as well as people's apathy towards them, people do not trust micro-finance as such. So, they tend to come collect their monies frequently and usually. When there is that rush, micro- finance banks do not have where they can go to and collect money to cover for that shortfall or demand, unlike the big banks that go to the central bank to make withdrawals when there is rush. Thus, this is a major challenge in micro- finance banks.
How much has your bank received from the CBN as intervention?
We have never received any loan from any bank, or even the CBN. We have been able to manage through our shareholders and deposits regenerated.
There have been lots of competitive repositioning among micro-finance banks. In the past two years, what has become new in your bank?
In the past two years, we have been able to manage the bank to the point that the change in ownership that we had at a point did not affect our operations. We are also getting more repositioned, in the sense that in few months time, we would start using our ATM cards. And in few months too, we would be inviting new investors. Our focus is to concentrate on graduates, because in Nigeria, we have lots of graduates, and by the time we focus on these graduates and youths, it would be profitable, both to the bank and the Nigerian economy.

Original_Search_Title: 'Babies Giving Birth to Babies'
Headline: Nigeria: 'Babies Giving Birth to Babies'
Body_Text:
Bayelsa — A new film vividly draws attention to neglected social issues such as teenage pregnancies and early marriage, and shows that there is much more than oil spills and violence in the narrative emerging from the Niger Delta.
Like an oil spill
In parts of the Niger Delta the rate at which teenage girls become pregnant can be compared to the frequency of an oil spill. In its occurrence and effect it is worse than an oil spill. An oil spill degrades the environment by suffocating all aquatic life in its path,and teenage pregnancies also make huge social ,emotional and economic demands which the young girl may be unable to cope with,and which can also deal a severe blow on society, which is ill equipped to deal with this rising baby boom. How does she fend for the child if she has neither a trade nor a job, and the baby's father is just a schoolboy? Under these circumstances life contracts for the young father and mother, especially in a country where there are either limited or non existent forms of social welfare. Under these conditions grandmothers now come in to take care of the infant, as well as the mother. She, like her baby,is a baby too.
Early marriage is another big issue in the Delta,and it too can be compared to an oil spill in terms of its effect on society, as well as frequency. The plight of widows and the endless traditions that rope her in, and seem to choke her from every angle upon the death of her spouse, is another impossible weight sitting upon the widows mind, intuitions, ambitions and family. It too is worse than an oil spill.There are no laws that swiftly encircle the husband if he loses his partner. He is as free as a bird. The man can marry a wife within a year of losing his spouse. On the other hand a widow waits forever for a partner. Taboos seem to dog every move of hers, as well as her innermost delicate intuitions. Weekly Trust also noticed that the oil spill is blamed by the people for everything occuring in the communities. The oil spill is a spectre in the mind, dominating the consciousness and governing the innermost intuition, so that, even if it sounds ridiculous, teenage pregnancies are blamed on the oil spill, as well as early marriages. Here, poverty seems to have made the people superstitious.
The film 'Daughters of the Niger Delta' is produced by MIND (Media, Information & Narrative Development) It is directed by Ilse van Lamoen, and in just under an hour, it dissects striking moments in the Niger Delta, by exploring the lives of a number of symbolic female characters. It advances the thesis that life in Niger Delta involves much more than a one sided focus on oil spills and violence. Rather, life in the oil rich region embraces wide ranging social issues, discrimination and taboos, which can cripple the society if left unchecked. There are several unforgettable figures here. One is in a complex polygamous home. Another is a widow with a stirring narrative, and the last battles sexual harassment in the University. At present both MIND and FACE (Fostering Achievement Community Empowerment) are working together to have the film screened at a number of locations in the Niger Delta.
'1 girl,3 husbands'
At Abobiri, a community in Bayelsa state visited last week by this reporter, where the film 'Daughters of the Niger Delta 'was screened, a man blurted out during the post screening session 'Some girls in this community have either three husbands or boyfriends. Sometimes, she becomes pregnant, then people begin to debate who among the three is the father.' No woman present contradicted this point when it was made.
Baby boom
Mrs. Daisy Esi, a FACE (Fostering Achievement Community Empowerment) member present at the screening, tells Weekly Trust 'Here childhood is immediately eroded when a fifteen year old becomes a mother. It is a case of babies giving birth to babies. Teenage pregnancies in the Niger Delta are worse than an oil spill. In the communities here over 70 % of the girls end up with teenage pregnancies. In some of the oil producing areas boys are paid 10,000 Naira by some of the oil producing companies, and they use this money to oppress the girls, and the latter are not paid a kobo 'But this is not all. There is an array of laws confronting the woman from all sides in the Niger Delta, and these combine to freeze her mind, shackle her life and weaken her resolve to grow. She is battered, bent and mocked by the impossible weight of traditions, and these are worse in effect than an oil spill,or even a gas flare. It may take 30 years to clean up the totality of the oil spills in the Niger Delta, we were recently told, but logic shows that taboos and stigma formed over the centuries may need an equal period of time to be overthrown, not to talk of the near impossible task of freeing the people's minds from these deeply rooted beliefs.
'You can't marry if he dies'
During the same post screening session another respondent showed how a woman who has lost her husband is not allowed to remarry. Her words 'If you are married and your husband dies,then you must remain like that. But if a man loses his wife, he doesn't have to wait for a year before he remarries. This practice is widespread among many communities in the Niger Delta. She added that teenage pregnanacies are rampant in the community, while a young lady showed that teenage pregnanacies are on the rise because parents do not discipline their children and wards. She added that after school hours, the young ones roam about, and do not respect their parents. There were hints that a culture of idleness has crept into the community, thus 'youths don't want to do anything, and girls are already sufficiently idle before getting married'. There is the stunning fact mentioned that' only few girls are able to finish secondary school before getting married'.There were some who showed that teenage pregnancies also occur in disciplined homes, just as it occurs in homes where discipline is a bit relaxed. The respondents showed that their only source of water is the nearby river, which is very dirty, they said. Quite a number also argued for some level of capacity building in the community, so that some of the people could start a trade. On the whole the response to the film on that wet morning, was quite good, and showed that the people know very well what the problems are.
Widow's plight
Hannah Tende, a widow and one of the characters in the film tells Weekly Trust in PortHarcourt that in most of the communities in the Niger Delta,a widow 's life is a difficult one. Her words 'If your husband dies in our place, you will not remarry. But if a man's wife dies, he can remarry. This culture is unacceptable for widow's. If your husband dies,then you will begin to suffer 'She says that in order to fend for herself and her four children, she has had to go to the river side to pick Periwinkles, which are rare to find today, on account of the effect of oil pollution. Now, she speaks of the list of traditions the widow is made to conform with. Her words 'If your husband dies, you are asked to do a sacrifice. You are asked to buy a fowl, a yam and drinks. Every morning you will buy drink, and go and give the elders in your fathers compound for one week. Also, you have to go and wash the clothes he last wore at a river. It is not good for a Christian to do these things. 'She adds that if these cultures do not change, then she won't allow her two daughters to marry from Budo.
Single for life
Bogofanyo Inengibo, who was one of those who shot the film, also comments on the plight of widows in the area. Her words 'In one community in Rivers state, we came across a situation where women were not being allowed to marry once they lost their husbands.' But the situation was slightly different and even more complex at Kiama in Bayelsa state. According to her 'In Kiama, women are allowed to remarry, but they would have to marry a relation of her late husband's family.' Naomi Ofoni, who also shot the film and featured as one of the characters, speaking on the Niger Delta woman, says 'You pity them, but they don't pity themselves. The pregnant woman we met, has had several children, but she is still going round fishing. She spoke as though she is meant to give birth as long as the children will keep coming. Also she was not really convinced about family planning. Another woman said that once you stop a woman in the village from giving birth, she falls sick often'. She explains that she discovered that at Budo, if a woman is married to a man, she remains his forever. Her words 'If you get married to another man, it is a taboo. The women believe that if your husband dies, it is your fate, and if you try to free yourself from the tradition, you will die.' She also found that in some communities if you are the only daughter or the only child of your father, then you must get married from your community. If no man comes from the community seeking marriage, then you will remain single'.
Bogofanyo also recalls the intriguing habit of people who blame everything on the oil spill. Her words 'What's most annoying is that the people blame their customs on the oil spill. People say the spill is responsible for the boys impregnating the girls. That it is responsible for the lady's refusal to marry a man she has been given to marry. If anything has to change, it has come from the mind set of the people. The people have to stop looking for who to blame. They have done a lot of blaming. They need a total reorientation of their mindsets. Almost everywhere we went we saw young girls with babies, all of them with no future plan. We went to Rivers state, where students complained of their classmates dropping out of school, because they suddenly got pregnant.'
'Ijaw culture allows for polygamy'
Inatimi Odio is the Executive Director of FACE (Fostering Achievement Community Empowerment), an NGO that is partnering with MIND. He comments on the plight of widows in the Niger Delta, as well as the matter of teenage pregnancy. His words 'We saw the situation of widows in Rivers state, as well as Bayelsa state. To us it is not as critical as the teenage pregnancy issue, because you see e the girls everywhere. It is a case of babies giving birth to babies.' He showed that in Ijaw culture a woman who doesn't allow her husband to take another wife, is termed wicked.' Customarily, it is a plus for a woman to have her husband bringing in another woman as a wife.This is a plus customarily, whether they have food to eat or otherwise.'He says that education is lopsided in favour of the boy.He says 'A boy impregnates a girl, completes his secondary education, and goes to the University, but the girl stops at JS 111.' Mr. Odio says that the screening of the film in the communities has seen a variety of issues emerging as reactions to its content. His words 'Teenage pregnancy is a cross cutting issue across all communities in the Niger Delta. Teenage pregnancy and unemployment are like a chain, for unemployment leads to a situation that may result in teenage pregnancy and early marriage.' He adds that the people perceive the oil spill to be a major problem that verges on the psychic. According to him 'The people believe that oil is their problem, even if it is not directly related, they find a way to connect it as a cause of the problem. It has become a stereotype that oil is the problem of the Niger Delta.'

Original_Search_Title: No Local Herbs Can Heal Sickle Cell Disease
Headline: Ghana: No Local Herbs Can Heal Sickle Cell Disease
Body_Text:
There is no traditional medicine that can cure the sickle cell disease and therefore people who have it should be wary in order not to be deceived by self-styled healers who might claim to have solution to the ailment.
Mr. Peter Mensah, National President of the Sickle Cell Association of Ghana (SCAG), who said this, expressed worry that there were many misconceptions about the disease which the Association was trying to correct to protect its members.
Speaking at the launch of the Central Region branch of the Association he said the stigma attached to the disease is very worrying.
Among the misconceptions are that persons with the disease must not eat certain types of food including egg, gari and yam, and they that they could also not marry whilst the women cannot bear children.
They are also perceived to die early and so some parents refuse to spend money to educate their children who are sickle cell positive with the notion that they would not live long but Mr. Mensah debunked all these, saying when well-managed a person with the disease could live normal life and live long.
Some people with the disease who are above 60 years attended the inauguration and shared their experiences with the other members.
Sickle cell is an inherited disorder of the red blood cells (RBCs) caused by an abnormality of the red pigment in RBCs called haemoglobin and if both parents of an individual have the abnormality, he or she could inherit the combination of the two genes and, therefore, get the disease. Research had established that the disease is prevalent among people of the black race.
He said the Association had been formed to give patients the platform to share their experiences and also help correct all the wrong impressions the public had about the disease by educating its members to have more information to be able to manage the disease to prevent serious crisis.
Mr. Mensah said a proposal in draft had been sent to parliament by the Suckle Cell Foundation and when approved, the Foundation would coordinate all activities in relation to Sickle Cell in the country.
He urged people with the disease and those with traits of the disease to join the Association for them to draw lessons from each other and also have the best of care whilst the Association helps in the maintenance of the disease.
Dr. Daniel Asare, the Medical Director in charge of the CRH, praised the formation of the Association in the Region and said knowing one's status would help facilitate better management of the disease and appealed to the Public Health Department of the Hospital to intensify education to allay the fears of people with the disease.
He pledged the Hospital's support to the Association to aid the proper coordination and education of sickle cell activities in the Region and called on all with the disease to join.
Mr. Joseph Sarfo Antwi, National Secretary of the Association, called for a national policy to screen new-born babies for early detection. He said the Association's objective was to foster good interaction between members as well as patients and doctors.
Some patients during an open forum expressed dismay that there was no special unit to care for them in times of crisis and called on all health facilities to establish such units for prompt attention when in crisis.
They also appealed to the Association to intensify its educational campaigns to help the public to have in-depth knowledge of the disease to help minimize the stigma attached to it because there are instances where people declare their status only to lose their jobs.GNA

Original_Search_Title: The Thrills and Pains of Nightlife in Motorparks
Headline: Nigeria: The Thrills and Pains of Nightlife in Motorparks
Body_Text:
When the sun goes down is when things really heat up at Motor parks in most parts of the country. The mixture of music coming from different stalls where cassettes, CDs DVD of different artistes are sold, the intermittent noise from loudspeakers calling passengers to board buses to different parts of the country, the blaring of horns by luxurious buses and articulated vehicles around including shouts and arguments in the area are enough cacophony of hoots and cackles that define night life in most part of the country.
From Iddo, Jibowu, Maza-maza in Lagos to Sagamu, in Ogun, Iyaro in Benin, EdoState, Umunede in Delta, Upper Iweka in Onitsha, Sabon Gari in Kano are parks where night life thrives with a lot of bubbles.
Apart from the transportation of people and goods to other parts of the country, some travelers use motor parks as stop over, laze off the stress of long hours on the road, eat, drink and hang out with female partners while it also provides shelter for motor park workers and miscreants who do not have homes.
Saturday Vanguard investigation showed that many of the inhabitants of motor parks across these areas are young men who find shelter in abandoned vehicles, thatched stalls where they lay their heads at night. Most of these people according to investigation are not bus conductors at the park but young lads who do menial jobs such as cart-pushing during the day and find a resting place at motor parks.
Although, some of them refused to talk to us, a few of them were seen hanging out in some abandoned vehicles while others laid on benches, with their wheel-barrows for a night rest. Others are miscreants who rather than offer assistance to distressed people at nights, find pleasure in extorting money from individuals and adding more to the anguish of an individual spending a night in the park.
There were a number of distressed people waiting to get a lift to their destinations while others choose to stay through the night fixing their broken down vehicles.
It is not difficult to find food vendors around with sizeable patronage at every point in time especially before the wee hours. It was also gathered that different parks are famous for different delicacies. For instance, Umunede was famous for bush-meat delicacy.
A lorry driver, Aremu Oluaye shared his experience in Umunede, saying: "When I was driving a tanker, my route mostly was the Eastern part of the country: Onitsha, Enugu and Aba sometimes. Umunuede was a must stop for me for bush meat. I enjoyed spending some resting hours like two or three hours there before proceeding on the journey. I stop only at Ore if I want pounded yam and dog meat delicacy."
When asked about hanging out with girlfriend, Oluaye said: "It is possible as you always see men clutching to their women and at that odd hour of the night, what would you expect. But don't forget they are all mature."
In most of the parks visited, there are make shift bathrooms and places of conveniences. Every darkened and obscure corner becomes the bathrooms and places of convenience for the inhabitants who are not hindered by the stench that oozes out from such areas.
Oyingbo/Iddo
The motor park meters away from the train terminus is full of local bars, even if only a few of them had patrons. What particularly draws attention of liveliness in the area is the assemblage of luxurious buses blaring their horns for passengers traveling to different parts of the country.
Fairly lit with a few bus lamps and other rays from stalls, kiosks within the environment, a few of the popular local gin, paraga joints were noticed, but food vendors outshone, attending to customers. Officials of the park could not speak with Saturday Vanguard on what goes on in the park at nights, saying they were at their peak when we visited at about 9pm. Not until an hour later when the reporter left the area, it was a beehive of activities ready to offer all services as it may be required in the day.
Ojota
Even as popular as it is for the volume of travelers that throng the park in the day, night activities is somewhat sleepy. Passengers desirous of boarding vehicle to other parts of the country will be disappointed even before 7pm. Reasons adduced for this by nearby dwellers are that the areas is notorious for miscreants with regular reports of assaults and extortion, passengers were discouraged from boarding vehicles there at nights. Others noted that other wing of the park operate till 8pm only with commercial mini buses as different with other known areas like Mazamaza, Iddo and Mile 2 area of the state.
Umunede, Delta State
Umunede is a great spot for particularly drivers of lorries and articulated vehicles en route the Eastern part of the country. The cluster of lorries at most time of the night would revealed 'welcome to Umunede' as it is a must stop for drivers of these vehicles.
Here, you will find bars that attract both drivers and passengers on transit and fairly local crowd that find enjoyment in motor parks at nights. The area though not well illuminated, serves as advantage to those who clutch to their partners in the milieu of the dark. It is known for bush-meat delicacy. One of the best places to have some drinks according to a lorry driver who is acquainted with the place is Umunede. "For me, I get satisfaction with whatever I need there, both food and drink and at the time I visited, the security of your vehicles and people was guaranteed." People who really stop there are lorry drivers and it was representative of typical lorries' park.

Original_Search_Title: Peculiar Faces of Nigerian Motor Parks
Headline: Nigeria: Peculiar Faces of Nigerian Motor Parks
Body_Text:
The motor park in today's modern society is arguably the most common 'first contact' for people travelling from one destination to another within Nigeria. For African neighbors such as Benin Republic, Ghana, Niger and other bordering countries coming into Nigeria, the motor park too probably is the first contact and thus gives the first impression. Over times, these parks are but something else.
Commute, pause, eat...
In Abuja, incessant rains appear to be revealing the poor state of the Utako Motor Park, popularly called 'Jabi Park.' The entrance to the park is usually a nasty sight to behold as buses splash in and out every now and then especially it has become permanently wet due to the rains. "We wish the government will assist in giving it a facelift so we can enjoy it better," Mr. Emmanuel Onche, a revenue collector, said. But this is just an aspect of the problem. Apart from this, Weekly Trust discovered the notoriety of hawkers and some stalls for selling controlled drugs despite the existence of a police outpost within the environment. However, the Area 3 Junction motor park is a moderate breather from the condition of other parks. With an environment that is fairly devoid of slippery mud, the grounded gravels still hold their shine and protect the environment. From afar the set tarpaulins behind the parked buses catch the eye with their clientele of hungry commuters who pause to fill their bellies before embarking on their journeies or short trip to work. Just like it happens in most competitive markets; here young girls quickly draw the attention of any commuter who approaches their make-shift restaurant with the quick words, "Oga come, food is ready." Alongside the restaurants are women roasting yam under wide umbrellas. Again, behind the restaurants is a haven for all sorts of petty businesses. Shoe cobblers, hawkers and local barbers, known in the Hausa language as 'wanzamai' with their nail cutter counterparts.
Congestion, criminals; pick-pockets...
When Abali Motor Park was established in the late 1980s, the original idea of the administrators of Port Harcourt City Council was to use it as the City Council Motor Park that will serve as a link to travelers that transverse from Port Harcourt to northern and western parts of Nigeria, but today the once bubbling Motor Park located at the busy Aba/Port Harcourt road wears an obsolete look that has become a hideout to notorious armed robbers and pick pockets, Weekly Trust learnt.
At Abali, a first time visitor to the park is confronted with deafening shouts of; "Aba road, Aba road!" from touts canvassing for passengers. At times they push passengers around in an unorthodox way of convincing them to join a particular vehicle. A few steps forward, one is again confronted with the main park meant to accommodate not less than 500 vehicles. A section accommodates vehicles which convey passengers from Port Harcourt to Akwa Ibom, Cross River, Enugu, Bayelsa and Benue States.
Another section of the park serves as a mini market where young ladies plait their hair; sell all kinds of clothing and cell phones. The mini market, according to Mr. Bethel Dappa, Chairman of Abali Park branch of National Union of Road Transport Workers, operates illegally in the market. Mr. Dappa told Weekly Trust that the market was allocated to a relation of the Council Chairman who collects tolls from traders in the market. Thus, the locating of the market in the park constitutes a nuisance. It threatens the security of commuters and passengers at the park because the management of the Park finds it difficult to control movement. "The park is divided into two. One section was given to somebody as a market. The operation of the market inside the park has contributed to its congestion.The park is supposed to accommodate more than 1,000 vehicles, but now only about 500 vehicles can have psace at once," Dappa said.
"Some of the problems we are having in this park were caused by the government. They allow proliferation of arks in all the nooks and crannies of Port Harcourt and this made it difficult for commuters to patronize the major park. Before we used load vehicles to Uyo, Lagos, Markudi and even Kano, but since the government allowed mushroom parks to spring up all over the place, we hardly load two vehicles a day. This situation is really affecting the smooth running of the park," he said.
Another problem confronting the park, according to Dappa, is the activities of criminals in and around it. He said all the adjoining roads and the premises of the park is a 'no-go' area the moment it is dawn and as early as 6.30am. "You see all manner of criminals and pickpockets harassing innocent passengers. This is the reason why people do not patronize this park always," he pointed out and called on the state Commissioner of Police to urgently deploy police officers to beef up security in and around the park.
The Secretary of the National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW), Abali Park, Mr. Eugene Ogu said the union has limited power to control movement of vehicles within the park. He said the responsibility is that of the City Council to ensure that all commercial vehicles load from designated parks. He pointed out that the proliferation of mushroom parks across Port Harcourt has made many parks to remain dormant. "If you go round Port Harcourt, you will see all manner of illegal motor parks with touts scouting for passengers. The law enforcement agents should go after them. Our power is limited in this direction," he said.
Mr. John Akpan, a bus driver who operates from the park, told Weekly Trust that drivers experience low patronage, said passengers prefer to board vehicles outside the park instead of coming into the park. He pointed out that boarding vehicles outside the park is very dangerous, arguing that "when one boards vehicle from the roadside, the person stands the risk of boarding vehicles owned by armed robbers and other kind of criminals. The reverse is the case when one uses the motor park. Our vehicles are documented at the park and are given tally numbers. This is to monitor the movement of vehicles in case any of our members are involved in any act of criminal activities. It is very important that one boards from the park."
Some of the passengers at the park expressed worry over the activities of criminals and touts operating there. A lady who simply gave her name as Joy said the activities of criminals scare most of the passengers away from the park. She said "I have been robbed at the Abali Park once. I was on my way to the park as early as 6.30am. I was stopped on the way by two boys who accosted me. They ordered me to hand over my handset to them and I complied. They collected two of my handsets as well as my wallet which contained N10, 000. Since then I stopped using the park to travel."
A playground for touts...
At Akure Bus Terminus, drivers complained that passengers always refuse to give their details, which is aimed at helping in times of emergencies, such as accidents. According to an inter-state driver, Mr. Mojeed Taiwo, "we drivers have no problem than to get passengers on board and start the journey. Getting passengers details in case of emergencies is a difficult thing for us, but the introduction of the passengers manifest by the Federal Road Safety Corps (FRSC) for the National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW) is helping to address that problem now."
He, however, said "some passengers still refuse to give their details on the manifest claiming that they do not wish for emergency situations, whereas anything can happen at anytime, though nobody wishes for bad things to happen."
Taiwo identified the lack of parking space as another major challenge facing them saying " every where is jam-packed and that is why sometimes we don't park all our vehicles in the park at the same time so as to allow for space."
Mr. Ojuolape Olaiya who travels through Ketu to Ikeja said part of the problem facing the drivers is the dues they have to pay. "It is almost as if we are just working for others, because when you pay all sorts of unnecessary dues and at the end of the day you pay the police, LASTMA, you fuel the bus, you deliver to your owner, then you as the driver what else do you go home with?"
A passenger who simply identified himself as Olaolu said the situation in Nigerian motor parks is not generally good, adding that "it only shows how uncoordinated we are as a people and a nation. He said the motor parks are usually porous and passengers going to a destination for the first time find it difficult to know where to board the appropriate vehicls unless they ask people around."
Buttressing Olaolu's position, another passenger, Mrs. Comfort Akerele said the level of buying and selling at various motor parks confuse passengers as many people who are ordinarily not supposed to be at parks are found there.
This, she said, is why some touts see motor parks as hide outs where they perpetrate all sort of evils against passengers especially night travellers such stealing their belongings.
According to her, government should do more in ensuring security and a well arranged motor parks in Nigeria, saying "some motor parks are eyesore and as passengers you want to quickly get out of the there in other countries is not like that in fact passengers can even decide to wait and catch some fun before departing because there is a provision for that and the environment is conducive."
The NURTW branch chairman for Ketu Bus Terminus, Alhaji Gunwaola Sunday Ogundipe said everything is being put in place to ensure hitch-free departures and arrival at motor parks.
According to him, the NURTW meets with all branches to ensure proper monitoring of activities at bus terminus in Lagos State. He said the union also conducts inter-states meetings to ensure that buses from one state to another safeguarded.
Ogundipe explained that Ketu bus terminus, for example, has a police station there to guarantee security of passengers. "As far as Ketu Bus Terminus is concerned, everything is in order, because we are able to control the drivers and bus conductors and we respond to emergencies promptly. We are working with relevance government agencies to guarantee security; that is why pick pocketings, robberies or anything of such had beenreduced to the barest minium here," he said.
Ogundipe said there is a special task force that examines the status of each vehicle in Ketu terminus, particularly the inter-state buses; and any vehicle found unworthy is not allowed to take off. "It could be tyre that is not good, we ask the driver to fix it and if it has to do with engine, the task force can issue an ultimatum to the driver to fix the problem," he said.

Original_Search_Title: Anambra Guber - Court Upholds Andy Uba As PDP Candidate
Headline: Nigeria: Anambra Guber - Court Upholds Andy Uba As PDP Candidate
Body_Text:
THE Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) still has a high hurdle to scale ahead the November 16, 2013 Anambra gubernatorial election. A Federal High Court sitting in Port Harcourt, Rivers State yesterday ruled that Senator Andy Uba remains the PDP candidate for the Anambra gubernatorial race.
It ordered the Ken Emeakayi-led PDP in Anambra state to maintain the status quo pending the final decision of the court. It also ordered that the PDP should recognize the Ejike Oguebego-led faction of the party - as the authentic faction.
In the ruling handed by Justice H.A Nganjiwa, the court also directed the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to identify with the Ejike group that produced Uba as the authentic governorship candidate of the PDP.
It's season of endorsements for candidates
SIXTY-FOUR days to the November 16 governorship election, the Anambra polity is awash with endorsements for the various candidates running the race. It is a season of endorsements, as it were, from hordes of groups cutting across all strata of the polity.
Physically challenged endorse Ifeanyi Ubah: THE Association for Comprehensive Empowerment of Nigerians with Disability, ASCEND, in Anambra State has endorsed Mr. Ifeanyi Ubah, the candidate of the Labour Party, LP, for the governorship of the state. The 100,000-member ASCEND took the decision in Awka yesterday during a meeting attended by physically challenged persons from all parts of the state.
President of the association, Chief Cosmas Okoli, who announced the endorsement, said Ifeanyi Ubah was the only candidate that has spelt out in clear terms what he would do for physically challenged people and the entire people of the state if he wins the November 16 election.
He said: "Today marks the beginning of a new chapter in the lives of persons with disability in Nigeria as we take the first decisive step in the politics of Nigeria, with the endorsement of Dr. Patrick Ifeanyi Ubah for governor of Anambra State come 2014.
"We started this journey by announcing our intention to endorse a candidate for the gubernatorial election and we thus opened our doors to all the aspirants to engage with us in the process of endorsing a candidate for the election.
"We make bold to say that no aspirant responded to this overture, except Dr. Ifeanyi Ubah, who promptly reached out to us to find out what the issues are and to tell us that even before we announced our intentions, he had sought after our members and invited them to join his campaign organization. "He sought to know the needs of persons with disability and he promptly included them in his manifesto and also pledged to run a transparent and inclusive government that will take Anambra State to the next level.
He came across to us as someone who could be trusted, but because we want to make sure that we endorsed the best candidate, we went ahead to assess all the gubernatorial aspirants to know those who have the capacity to deliver good governance to the people of Anambra State and can ensure that no group or section of the society is left out."
Anambra technocrats thumbs up Ngige: IN like manner, Shipping, freight-forwarding and allied logistics operators under the auspices of Anambra Maritime Practitioners Association (AMPA) have endorsed Senator Chris Ngige for the position. AMPA National President, Dominic Obi alongside other executives in Lagos, voiced their zeal and financial commitment to the return of the Ngige construction and rehabilitation era, during the official presentation of campaign flag-off materials to the senator.
Obi disclosed that though the group is non-partisan, AMPA's support in politics is "earned by individuals whose records, antecedents and pedigree show that they will serve the people and perform well," more so with the state's slogan change from "Home for All" to "Light of the Nation."
The AMPA boss decried the deteriorating state of insecurity, infrastructural and institutional decay, which he said puts the people at risk of losing their identity and roots as more people stay away from new yam festivals and other cultural/religious programmes including conducting traditional marriages for their children in Lagos and other secured cities.
"The government that did not give its people security has lost it all. We must not get used to this imprisonment of locking ourselves inside prison-like houses, moving with troops of armed policemen and military, like prisoners being escorted to court for trial or those condemned to death being escorted for execution.
"Dr Chris Ngige had it in his blueprint to commence developing freight and transport infrastructure to stimulate the economy of Anambra state and also create employment.
We know that not only will he restore all sectors that have gone to the doldrums since he left office, but he will move fast to continue implementing his people-oriented plan," he concluded. Senator Chris, who was represented by Messrs Emmanuel Ojukwu and Valentine Uzokwe, stated that the richest man is not found in the graveyard but among the living and pledged to fight insecurity through provision of jobs.
Moghalu tasks INEC on credible polls
A chieftain of the All Progressives Congress (APC), Chief George Moghalu, has urged the Independent National Electoral Commission (INEC) to ensure a credible, free and fair election on November 16.
Speaking with Vanguard yesterday, the former All Nigeria Peoples Party national secretary also boasted that the APC is the party to beat in the governorship election because it has the best candidate that will deliver dividends of democracy to Anambra citizens.
Moghalu said, Senator Chris Ngige, the candidate of the party parades the best credentials out of all the candidates running for the office.
His words: "I believe strongly and I am very confident that we are going to win the forthcoming gubernatorial race in Anambra State because our candidate, Senator Chris Ngige is widely accepted. He is tested, experienced and can be trusted".
He also cautioned the Independent Electoral Commission (INEC) to be up and doing at the forthcoming polls.
"INEC should give Anambrians a free, fair and credible election in November because anything below standard will be unacceptable" he added.
He however maintained that the party is battle ready to confront the other parties at the polls saying "I can tell you precisely that we in the APC are ready for this election and we are not going for anything less than to win the elections".

Original_Search_Title: Hajj - Lagos to Airlift 3,000 Pilgrims Saturday
Headline: Nigeria: Hajj - Lagos to Airlift 3,000 Pilgrims Saturday
Body_Text:
The Lagos State government will begin the airlifting of over 3,000 Muslim pilgrims to Mecca on Saturday this week.
The state Commissioner For Home Affairs and Culture Alhaji Oyinlomo Muhammed Danmole, who disclosed this at a one-day seminar held for intending pilgrims, said the airlifting of the pilgrims will end on Friday, September 20, 2013.
He said the airlifting had been contracted to NAS Airline, the official airline for the state's pilgrims and the first airline that will commence airlifting of pilgrims to Mecca.
The commissioner pleaded with the pilgrims to ensure that they meet up with their flight arrangements and schedules in order to avoid unnecessary delay in the hajj operation.
Danmole also revealed that the state government had barred its 2013 Muslim pilgrims from carrying food items to Mecca and Medina in the upcoming Hajj, stressing that the government has provided five kilogrammes each of garri, rice, beans and yam flour for each of the pilgrims.
He added that the state government had inaugurated the Year 2013 Hajj Committee to ensure a hitch-free hajj for the state, headed by Dr. Abdul Kabir Paramole, a senior lecturer in Islamic Studies at the Lagos State University, Ojo.
Meanwhile, the Lagos State Government has commenced medical screening of 2,500 intending pilgrims to this year's pilgrimage to Jerusalem, Israel.
The screening currently taking place at the Chapel of Christ the Light, Alausa, Ikeja in Lagos has government officials, the Police, Immigration and the National Drug Law Enforcement Agency, NDLEA are involved in the screening process.

Original_Search_Title: 50 Insurgents, 24 Civilians Killed in Borno
Headline: Nigeria: 50 Insurgents, 24 Civilians Killed in Borno
Body_Text:
Maiduguri — Troops using fighter jets have killed 50 suspected members of the Boko Haram sect who had earlier murdered many civilians in villages in Nganzai and Konduga Local Government Areas of Borno State, spokesman of the newly established 7 Division of the Nigerian Army, Maiduguri, Lt Col Sagir Musa, said yesterday.
When asked the number of civilians killed by the Boko Haram fighters on Wednesday and Thursday, Sagir said, "the exact number is not certain at the moment, but I am sure local officials should have an idea of the casualty figure."
But latest information from witnesses indicate that the Boko Haram insurgents had killed 24 civilians, nine in Bulabulin Ngabura of Konduga Local Government Area and 15 others in Gajiran, the headquarters of Nganzai Local Government Area.
It was also gathered that in Bulabulin Ngabura, the insurgents have kidnapped a man, his wife and three children, a development which sources said prompted the military to deploy fighter jets to arrest the insurgents.
Col Musa in Maiduguri yesterday, said the insurgents were trailed to their operational camps by ground troops and military fighter jets.
According to him, "troops of the 5 Brigade of the 7 Division of the Nigerian Army have effectively repelled Boko Haram terrorists attack on Gajiran and Bulabulin Ngabura communities in Nganzai and Konduga Local Government Areas of Borno State.
"Troops pursued the terrorists to their camps with air support and about 50 insurgents were killed in a shootout.
"Subsequently, the villages have been rescued from the insurgents. At the moment, the Brigade troops are pursuing the remnants of the fleeing terrorists by blocking all possible escape routes," Sagir said.
Weekly Trust gathered from locals that the Boko Haram terrorists, numbering over 40 reportedly attacked Gajiran, 75 kilometres from Maiduguri and killed 15 locals around noon on Wednesday. They also burnt the council secretariat, market, police station and hospital.
A local trader who came to Maiduguri from the area yesterday said, "the insurgents came in the afternoon, some of them in military uniforms and others with hooded face and opened fire in the market, after accusing our people of conniving with security forces.
"They (insurgents) were well armed with heavy guns. When they successfully attacked the people, they fled to the bush, but the military came after about an hour and pursued them," he said.
Another source said insurgents had tried to bring down a military plane with a rocket launcher. "The camp of the insurgents which was recently established was not more than five kilometers away from Gajiram. We saw when the military aircraft came and we saw when it retreated back for a while when the insurgents targeted it. It (the plane) went back again and again but left the place after some time," he said.
And in Bulabulin Ngabura which is 50 kilometres from the state capital, the insurgents, numbering about 30 reportedly killed nine people and abducted a family of five.
Musa Lado, a taxi driver of the National Union of Road Transport Workers (NURTW) who arrived at the scene of the attack minutes after the insurgents left, told journalists in Maiduguri that the insurgents killed nine people, including Alhaji Mohammed Sani (A Banje) an aide to Borno State Governor, Kashim Shettima.
"A man, his wife and three children were also abducted by the insurgents who dressed in military uniforms and mounted a huge roadblock around 10am on Thursday," he said.
The driver said he had confirmed that the abducted man was from Miringa district of Biu Local Government Area in southern part of Borno State.
"We assisted in putting the 406 car of the abducted man into a truck. The attackers did not take it away, they only forced him and his family into a different vehicle and whisked them away," he said.
Other sources who came to Maiduguri said some of the victims were slaughtered while others were shot by the insurgents.
"The insurgents are angry with the people of Borno State who have turned their backs against them. The coming of the civilian JTF is a big minus to the insurgents," Bakura Bamusa, a yam dealer said.
Weekly Trust reports that in the last two months, the Boko Haram insurgents have moved their activities to villages outside Maiduguri and have killed many people even as the locals have kept on cooperating with security forces.

Original_Search_Title: PDP's Stormy Tea Cup
Headline: Nigeria: PDP's Stormy Tea Cup
Body_Text:
WHEN the Nigerian Governors Forum, NGF, election politics was raging and some commentators said Their Excellencies were wasting time on an irrelevant issue that had no bearing on the average Nigerian, I laughed.
It reminded me of what Mohandas Gandhi said about religion and politics: "Those who say religion has nothing to do with politics don't know what religion is". They did not remember that a governor is first of all a politician, and a politician's first reflex is to play the power game.
The NGF election of May 2013 was a dress rehearsal for the shape of things to come, especially in the Peoples Democratic Party, PDP. The PDP knew it was going to expose the treachery, disloyalty and rebellion of the governors bent on ensuring that President Goodluck Jonathan does not win his second term.
The Governor of Rivers State, Chibuike Amaechi, was advised to forgo that election and avoid being used by Northern governors to divide the solidarity of the South-South. He refused to listen. Egged on by the opposition All Progressives Congress, APC, governors, the rebellious Northern governors as well as their vocal supporters in the media and open forums, Amaechi plunged into the contest.
The recently concluded national convention of the PDP ended with the rebellious Northern governors - Rabiu Kwankwaso of Kano, Magatakarda Wamakko of Sokoto, Aliyu Babangida of Niger, Sule Lamido of Jigawa, Murtala Nyako of Adamawa, Amaechi of Rivers and Abdulfatah Ahmed of Kwara (who was then dancing on the sidelines) - pulling out to form what they termed "new" PDP.
A familiar newcomer, former Vice President, Alhaji Atiku Abubakar and his handy political peripherals were there.Within the space of one week, we saw the registration of Peoples Democratic Movement, PDM, as a political party, the announcement of the intention of the rebellious Northern governors to register a new party to be called Voice of the People, VOP and the "secession" to form the "new" PDP.
They do not quite seem to have made up their minds exactly what to do, except that they are all united in their determination to pull the rug from under the feet of the President come 2015. The central question trailing this development is what effect this will have on the ruling party.
The question is best answered by looking at every mid-term to a presidential election year since the return of democracy in 1999. In 2001/2002, there was turmoil in PDP because Atiku's PDM faction wanted former President Olusegun Obasanjo to "do a Nelson Mandela" and hand over to Atiku after only one term in 2003.
They failed because Obasanjo not only ran for second term but also forced Atiku to run with him as Vice President.In the 2005/2006 midterm toward the 2007 presidential election, another series of crises erupted in the party.
The first was over the determination of Obasanjo's camp to de-register those big stakeholders in the party opposed to his third term ambition as members of PDP. That was how Atiku and many others were pushed out. This eventually resulted in Atiku becoming the presidential candidate of the opposition Action Congress, AC, for the 2007 election.
The second was the attempt by Obasanjo to use the then ongoing constitution amendment to give himself a third term. Atiku vehemently opposed this bid, along with majority of Nigerians. A crisis-riddled PDP was very instrumental to the abortion of its leader's unholy ambition.Come the year 2010, the nation was in a heated transition from one president to the other when President Umaru Yar'Adua passed away.
Some Northern leaders did not want then Vice President Goodluck Jonathan to succeed Yar'Adua because they said it was the turn of the North to rule for eight years after Obasanjo's eight. Later, they grudgingly caved in to pressure, only on the condition that he would not seek an election of his own.
Therefore, the PDP crisis in 2010/2011 was basically a section of the Northern leadership versus President Jonathan. Jonathan successfully defeated the regional gang-up against him, and some say the Boko Haram terrorism is a fulfillment of their threat to make Nigeria ungovernable.Against this background, I tend to see this PDP crisis as a teacup storm.
The party is used to them. The party always comes out of these storms intact and strong enough to win the next election. The PDP is a veteran of many civil wars. These wars will never end because they are normal jostling and positioning to hijack the party and use it to win. As it happens, the incumbent President, being the leader of the Party and the man who holds the proverbial knife and yam, he is always best placed to hijack the party for his own political ends.
Much is being made over the "splitting" of the PDP after the convention. It pays to ruminate more on the possible long term survival of the "split". Ordinarily, the pullout of seven out of 23 governors of the Party to form a "new" PDP is considerably earth-shaking.
In 2010 when the Adamu Ciroma-led Northern Political Leaders Forum, NPLF, queued behind Atiku to drag the PDP presidential ticket with President Jonathan, the governors had kept their distances, obviously because they were looking for second term tickets. Now that all of them are on their ways out, they can afford to join the effort against the President.
The question is: Who will step down for whom? Lamido, Atiku, Kwankwaso and Aliyu are all known to have presidential ambition. Lamido, an ardent Obasanjo supporter, is several times on record pouring vitriol on Atiku as a political leader.
Kwankwaso is also an Obasanjo lackey. Would they agree to step down for Atiku when the time comes? Will they go with Atiku to PDM? It does not seem likely. Some of them may reconcile with Jonathan any time, especially when Obasanjo gives the President one hundred per cent support. Nigeria politicians are not known to voluntarily jump down from the gravy train.

Original_Search_Title: The Filthy, Muddy Faces of Nigeria Markets
Headline: Nigeria: The Filthy, Muddy Faces of Nigeria Markets
Body_Text:
Port Harcourt, Lagos, Kaduna, Makurdi — In this report Weekly Trust x-rays the proverbial 'the good, the bad and ugly' sides of some major markets across the country, revealing pathetic and dangerous conditions that pose present and potential dangers to both sellers and buyers, much as it affects smooth transaction of business.
Rainy season is the period traders in most Nigeria markets dread. The muddy state of these markets and roads leading to them brings both traders and customers pains within the period.
These markets, most of which are unplanned coupled with the filthy environment are always an eyesore.
Because of lack of proper drainage system and un-tarred surroundings water hardly flow and therefore form ponds around most of the markets making movement difficult for traders and customers. The situation, Weekly Trust learnt, resulted in low sales as customers avoided such markets during the season.
Weekly Trust observed low turnout of customers in virtually all the markets visited within the suburb of the Federal Capital Territory (FCT).
The access road leading to the popular Nyanya market has become a nightmare to customers, traders and even residents in the area.
Vehicles get stuck in the gapping portholes and muddy surface resulting in gridlock. The market vicinity was muddy. Traders selling items such as yams and vegetables sit on stools with their feet and goods in the muddy ground, creating an unattractive scenario that makes it impossible for customers afraid of falling into the filthy arms of the mud to patronize the market.
A tomato seller, Mrs. Roseline Audu painted the picture this way: "There is nowhere for us to put our legs. Sometimes when the rain falls , we do not see customers"
A customer attested to this when she said, "I avoid this market when it rains. I usually wait until the place gets dry. However, today I had no choice because I have no meat in the house."
"The market is in a terrible state, yet they keep collecting taxes. It is just those sweeping the market and packing the dirt that we can say, are doing a good job," a trader lamented. According to Mr. Daniel Bright, a butcher, "Our market is in a deplorable state. The government has promised to develop it into a modern one with the required facilities. Until that is done, we will continue to lose our esteem customers. We want customers coming from Asokoro and AYA to be able to patronize us."
The situation is the same in Mararaba, where the entire market environment was muddy and filthy. Traders seemingly oblivious of the unhealthy surroundings called out to the few customers around for patronage. The roads within the market are hardly passable but some customers brave it to buy their household needs. A trader simply told Weekly Trust in response to a question, "they repaired the market but the government came and demolished it."
Weekly Trust's visit to Karu community market in Abuja was not any different from many of the major markets in the FCT. The market was muddy and made worse by the blood flowing from the tables of butchers and meat sellers in the market. A trader, who sells dried stock fish and simply gave his name as Philip, said it was always difficult to sell his goods during rainy season. "What I sell requires a dry place and so during rainy season, it is always difficult to sell my stock fish. On a good day I spread them out on the ground so customers can sample and pick whichever they like. This is not possible now because of the unhygienic state of the market. It would really be nice if something is done to help reduce the loss we suffer during the rainy season due to the market's unhygienic state," he added.
Another trader, Yakub Musa, who sells fish, also expressed similar displeasure.
His words, "The rainy season for me is supposed to be a thing of joy because we should naturally sell more at a period when people would want to take fish pepper soup. But most days, we experience low patronage. We would appreciate if the condition of the market is generally looked into at least for the sake of people like us who do not have a shop of our own," he said.
Customers who trooped to the market also had their share of complains regarding the market: Mrs . Bisola Jayesimi says, "I detest coming to this market during rainy days but when I think of the money I can save, I will have no choice than to come. Most of the sections where I get majority of my items are the areas that are really muddy and filthy during rainy season. Last year was even better because there was someone who was renting out boots to customers who wanted to enter the market. With the condition of the market during rainy season one can easily slip and fall and this could be very embarrassing. Asides that the whole market is not hygienic during this season, how can one be sure that the goods are safe for consumption?" she asks.
Wearing rain boots in the market
In Port Hacourt, Rivers state, the railway market is located at Sangana Street in Diobu area. It is a regular market that is situated beside a rail track and dotted in a filthy and muddy environment.
Weekly Trust observed that despite the nature of the market, traders and customers alike converge on a daily basis to transact their business. Whenever it rains the market becomes an eyesore. From the entry point to the different sections of the market is a pitiable sight. One is confronted with an offensive odour emanating from a nearby drainage filled with debris and all kinds of refuse. Coupled with this is the risk of standing beside a rail track to transact business, but the traders remain unperturbed about this.
The market mainly accommodates food stuff sellers who are mostly women. A fish seller in the market, Mrs. Beatrice Nmadu told Weekly Trust that they are used to the poor state of the environment. She said she has been in the market for more than five years after she lost her shop in a fire incident at Mile One market in Port Harcourt. "I lost my shop in a fire incident, since I have no money to rent a shop in the reconstructed market, I had no option than to come here and start a small business because I am a widow and have a family to take care of. So I am not worried about the nature of the market because I have to fend for my family".
A butcher who simply gave his name as Okon complained bitterly about the state of the market. He said "the market is flooded anytime it rains. You cannot enter here the moment it rains. Everywhere is flooded and covered with blackish mud. We are here because we do not have another place to transact our business. The environment is nothing to write home about. Look at the whole place, very dirty and muddy. We use rain boots everyday to enable us move in and around the market. We want the government to construct a befitting market for us so that we can move out of this place."
A fruit seller revealed that the market has no access road to bring in goods. He said wholesalers who bring goods in trucks to the market from the North park area had to engage labourers to off-load and move the goods to the market several kilometers away at a very high rate. He called on the Rivers State Government to construct a befitting market and relocate the traders.
According to George Chindah, a customer in the market who said he comes to the market every day to buy meat for his restaurant, "the market has become a house- hold name in food stuff that Port Harcourt residents cannot do without it. He told our correspondent that he puts on rain boots anytime he visits the market to enable him move freely.
Crossing muddy boundaries to reach customers
When Weekly Trust visited the Sheikh Gumi central market in Kaduna, traders and customers were seen going about their normal business in the mud even though they were all wearing bathroom slippers and both the women and men raised their trousers, skirts or wrappers to knee length so that their clothes do not get soiled.
A trader who gave his name as Shehu Tanimu said he has been in the business of selling tomatoes for the past 15 years. According to him "the market has not always been as muddy as it is today."
"We are calling on the government to come to our aid and repair the access roads for us so that we can do our trading effectively for the overall benefit of the state. If the road is repaired, the traders and the customers will have a better business environment.
"When our customers come and the place is too muddy, we take our goods to them instead of them crossing over the mud, so we do not really lose customers due to the muddy nature of the market," he said
On her part, Jennet Salawo has been in the business of selling bananas for the past 10 years. She said, when she started her business, the market was not as muddy as it is today.
"Like some six years ago, this market was well taken care of by the government, but I don't know what happened along the line. We are pleading with the government to help work on this side of the market so that we can transact our business without any hindrance because some customers will not come to your shade if the area is muddy," she said. Odour in the market
"Odour is no barrier to trading", the traders in Lagos' popular Merishable Mile 12 market told Weekly Trust, adding that even customers are used to it.
While some people canvas for the need to maintain proper hygiene within the market environment, Mile 12 traders are of the opinion that there is no way the market will not be dirty due to the volume of buying and selling and the variety of perishable goods that flood the market.
Mrs. Comfort Ejiofor, a condiments seller said she has taken the odour as part of her routine experience in as much she has nowhere else to make money. According to her, the traders who are making sales on a daily basis from the market do not perceive any odour, rather "we perceive it as scent or aroma which we have learned to live with. So, if you are in my shoes you will not use odour as an excuse not to make money," she said.
Another trader who simply identified herself as Iya-Ibeji Elelubo said mile 12 market smells because of the salad and pepper segments in the market. She explained that salad ingredients are brought into the market raw and they are perishable items that get rotten easily, adding that the situation is better because it hasn't rained for a while.
However, Mrs. Badejoka Salam, a customer, called on the government to constitute a monitoring team that will make sure that markets are well kept at all times. According to her, being a market does not mean it should be dirty or muddy, "after all, we go to other countries and see how they are able to keep their markets clean for healthy living and business transactions. So we must always ensure that the market environment is hygienic."
In Makurdi, the sorry state of the chicken abattoir at the modern market has continued to give customers serious concern.
Weekly Trust which visited the area observed that the chickens are prepared for customers in a rather dirty and foul smelling environment.
A customer, Ngozi Chukwu expressed disgust at the way the chickens are prepared for customers by young boys within the ages of 10 and 20 as they dump them in their numbers on the untidy floor.
Chukwu who has patronized the chicken dealers for over five years now explained that the place used to be clean in the past, but wondered why it is left unkempt these days.
On his part, a trader who simply gave his name as Baba Aminu argued that the place is washed and swept on daily basis before the commencement of trading activities and so whatever product is prepared at the slaughter is safer for consumption.
Corroborating him, Dogara Sule, a butcher opined that the section is always washed before the commencement of activities everyday, adding that the rainy season contributes to the poor outlook of the area.
Nathaniel S. Bivan, Amina Alhassan; Victor Edozie, Nahimah A. Nurudeen, Maryam A. Suka, and Hope Abah

Original_Search_Title: Nine-Month-Old Baby Burnt to Death in Sapele
Headline: Nigeria: Nine-Month-Old Baby Burnt to Death in Sapele
Body_Text:
Sapele — A nine-month-old baby locked up in a house by her parents has been reported burnt to death.
The girl's mother was said to have gone to the market to earn a living by plaiting peoples' hairs at Sapele yam garage while the father was also at work.
The incident occurred at about 7.00pm, Wednesday. The whole building was brought to ruins as neighbours could not put out the fire while men of Sapele Fire Fighting Service arrived late.
It was reported that the grand-father of the infant who was alerted of the incident was later involved in salvaging other parts of the building without knowing that his grand child had been consumed by the fire.
When Vanguard visited the scene, neighbours and sympathizers where seen wailing over the loss of the baby just as household items littered the place.
At press time, the cause of the fire was yet to be ascertained, but source at the Sapele Fire Fighting department, who preferred anonymity, confirmed that it was due to electrical spark from power fluctuation.

Original_Search_Title: Osun Osogbo, Odun Oranmiyan, Udiroko - Mediums for Celebrating Culture
Headline: Nigeria: Osun Osogbo, Odun Oranmiyan, Udiroko - Mediums for Celebrating Culture
Body_Text:
Promotion of cultural festivals is one of the surest ways of attracting tourists to Nigeria. Demola Ojo writes on a few taking place this month...
Another edition of the Osun Osogbo festival has come and gone with the finale of this year's edition taking place two days ago. The festival is another reminder of where Nigeria's comparative advantage lies when it comes maximising its tourism potentials. Despite different national parks across the country, Nigeria is way behind Kenya and other countries in the eastern and southern parts of Africa in the area of wildlife tourism. There are also many other countries in Africa to compete with when it comes to natural beauty and physical attractions.
However, Nigeria - which as an agglomeration of nationalities - is replete with a variety of cultures, many of them around for hundreds of years. The celebration of different festivals around the country helps in showcasing these cultures to the world, as well as shedding light on the rich history many of the individual nationalities that make up Nigeria possess. And very importantly, these festivals are instrumental in attracting tourists from far and wide.
Osun Osogbo One of the more famous festivals is the Osun Osogbo festival held in honour of the river goddess Osun, a famous Yoruba deity. It includes ceremonies where priests seek protection for their local communities through gifts and sacrifices to the goddess.
The Osun river goddess is credited with the founding and establishment of Osogbo town. The festival, which has been celebrated for about six centuries, was built around the relationship between the river goddess Osun and the first Monarch of Osogbo kingdom. Despite the gradual fading away of many celebrated African cultural traditions the Osun Osogbo festival can be traced back to 1370.
The earliest settlement seems to have been in the Osogbo Grove and included palaces and a market. When the population expanded the community moved outside the Grove and created a new town, which reflected spatially the arrangements within the Grove.
In the last century though, political and religious changes had a detrimental effect on the Grove. It was being neglected and traditional priests began to disappear. All this was exacerbated by a rise in the looting of statues and movable sculptures. Hunting and fishing began to be recorded, a previously forbidden act in the sacred Grove.
Luckily though, Austrian-born Suzanne Wenger moved to Osogbo and, with the encouragement of the Oba and the support from local people, challenged land speculators, repelled poachers, protected shrines and helped to bring the Grove back to life.
Odun Oranmiyan, Ife The celebration of Odun Oranmiyan festival kicks off tomorrow in Ile-Ife, regarded as the cradle of the Yoruba race. Oranmiyan was one of the seven sons of Okanbi, the only child of Oduduwa and reigned as the sixth Ooni of Ife. He was famed as a strong leader who led his people into a path of greatness that lasted several centuries, leaving a heritage that lasts till today. Among many feats, he is credited with establishing the Benin and Oyo kingdoms. His cenotaph popularly known as Opa Oranmiyan (Oranmiyan staff) is in Ife, and attracts its own share of tourists.
These tourists include Yorubas in the diaspora. They come from places like Brazil, Trinidad and Tobago and Cuba. For them, the festival is an opportunity to connect with their roots. Organisers of the Oranmiyan festival are pushing to it is enlisted in the calendar of the Nigerian Tourism Development Corporation and the World Tourism Calendar, and thus use it as a platform to drive investment to the ancient town.
Udiroko Festival, Ado-Ekiti Also taking place this week, the annual Udiroko festival is celebrated in Ado-Ekiti in the month of August. It is the only festival apart from Ogun or Oitado that brings all Ado-Ekiti citizens both at home and in the diaspora together.
Udiroko brings the Ewi, his chiefs, and subjects together once a year at the palace of the paramount ruler of Ado-Ekiti to celebrate.
Udiroko was established at about 1310 during the reign of Oba Awamaro, the first Ewi in modern Ado-Ekiti. The word "Udiroko" translates to "under the Iroko tree." The Iroko tree used to shield the people from the sun and its effect in those days and served as a meeting venue for the populace.
According to the Akogun of Ado-Ekiti, Chief Pius Ojo, "Udiroko is the first day of the year according to native law and custom. It is the day that commemorates the arrival of Ado-Ekiti people." The festival is celebrated with the people treating themselves to pounded-yam with egusi soup and undiluted palm wine and heading for the palace to catch a glimpse of their Oba in their best attires.
There they would be treated with cultural displays, Warlords from various towns and villages under Ewi's territorial domain would come with gifts.
"In those days, the Ewi appeared to the people only once a year. He would be dressed in full royal regalia and beads which kept most of his face hidden," the Akogun explained.
During the festival, the Ewi would present his address after the warlords had displayed their prowess and demonstrated their war exploits. The address usually contains the aspirations, developmental activities and budget for the New Year as well as a review of past activities and events.
Traditionally, the Akogun is one of the chiefs that fought wars to earn the right to the land. The Akogun was a general in the army. Normally, the Akogun and other warlords were rarely seen by the people. "It is during Udiroko that the war chiefs came out, also in full regalia for the people to see and celebrate. In essence, while Udiroko is a festival for everyone, it is especially important for the chiefs who come to dance at the palace," Chief Ojo said.
The fact that these three festivals are held in different places by different peoples for different reasons within days of each other, highlights the glut of festivals that have the potential of drawing visitors to a country with so many rich and diverse cultures.

Original_Search_Title: Playing the Ostrich Would Not Help the Peace Process!
Headline: Ghana: Playing the Ostrich Would Not Help the Peace Process!
Body_Text:
The news that dominated the airwaves yesterday was that a New Patriotic Party activist has been sentenced to death by hanging. Yahuza Yakubu was found guilty by a High Court in Tamale, presided over by Justice Lawrence Mensah, for murdering Rashid Mohammed, an activist of the ruling National Democratic Congress in February 2009.
Four others, described as accomplices, were given prison terms of 36 years each. They are Majeed Alhassan, Habib Mohammed, Sayibu Alhassa and Imoro Gondana. A fifth person, Habib Mohammed, was given a prison term of 10 years.
The sentences provoked various re-actions. A number of followers of the opposition New Patriotic Party were not happy with what they called selective justice creeping into the body politic. To be fair to the protestors, the complaint was not about the sentences per se, though Nana Obiri Boahen, the defence counsel, complained about what he called a 'miscarriage of justice.'
The source of concern is people's perceptions that this society is slipping into a situation where selective justice appears to be holding sway in a country which motto is Freedom and Justice. While applauding the sentences passed on those who committed the heinous crime of murder in Tamale, a number of people, particularly followers of the opposition NPP, fear that the party's supporters are punished for the least offence, while followers of the ruling NDC get away with murder.
Yesterday, the airwaves were filled with many NPP sympathisers complaining about the way the five NPP party activists have been severely dealt with by a Tamale High Court, while NDC activists who slaughtered five of their supporters at the Agobloshie Market in Accra in January 2009, one month before the incident in Tamale, for which an activist of the party has been sentenced to death and four others given very stiff custodian sentences.
I will like to submit here that these concerns should be treated with serious concern. In January 2009, while Prof. John Evans Atta Mills was swearing the oath to treat all Ghanaians equally before the law, three activists of the NPP were murdered in cold blood.
Alhassan Fuseini, M. Suala and J. Y. Sule were butchered to death when they were attacked by people suspected to be followers of the NDC. The situation created panic among traders at the yam market at Agbobloshie. Most sympathisers of the NPP at the yam market fled and camped at the party's campaign office at Asylum Down, also in Accra. They were what the United Nations High Commissioner on Refugees would describe as internally displaced people.
Instead of pursuing the perpetrators for justice, the police invited then party General Secretary, Nana Ohene Ntow, for questioning, when he complained about police inaction on the matter. Apparently, Nana Ntow had expressed misgivings about the situation.
At the burial of the three victims at the Madina Cemetery, Alhaji Aliu Alidu, father of deceased complained about the lukewarm attitude with which the police had responded to the crime. He said the "perpetrators would do it again. They claim their government is in power. They are jubilating over the issue."
The murder squad had apparently continued to issue threats. They were alleged have planned to disrupt the burial of their victims, prompting the burial grounds to be shifted from the Awudome Cemetery in down town Accra, to Madina, at the outskirts of the national capital.
The bereaved families held a joint press conference after the burial, and called on the government of President Mills to ensure that that the perpetrators were brought to book. Apart from the three deaths, many others sustained various degrees of injuries.
Members of the Minority in Parliament, led by Mr. Osei Kyei Mensah Bonsu, held a press conference and urged the police to interest themselves in the matter. At this confab, seven suspects were named. They are Mohammed Ayatu, Salana, Awal Voulina Naa, Sule Nabiya, Abdallah Rasta, Abdulla Sey and Sulley.
Following the public outcry, Parliament dispatched members of the Select Committee on Defence, Interior and National Security to Agbobloshie to acquaint themselves with the circumstances leading to the murder of the three persons. On that day, armed policemen and women put up a show of strength.
The MPs were met by a host of police officers, led by then Greater Accra Police Commander, DCOP Rose Atinga Bio (now DOP), James Town District Police Commander Superintendent Oklu Gyamera, and DSP Samuel Kwasi Ofori, then Director of the Public Affairs Directorate of the Ghana Police.
The police leadership informed the parliamentarians that the situation was so bad, and that "but for the intervention of the police, the casualties would have been higher." On his part, the Chairman of the Select Committee, Alhaji Abukari Sumani, MP for Tamale North, asked the traders not to extend the Dagbon crisis to Accra.
His ranking member, Mr. William Boafo, MP for Akropong, explained the mission of the committee. "Our presence here signifies our collective aspirations to work together. We want our supporters to realise that we are together in Parliament," according to a report in the Daily Guide. He appealed to the feuding factions to co-operate with the police in their quest to bring peace to Agbobloshie and other troubled spots in the country.
"Ghana has one of the most efficient police systems in the world," he stated. "All of us should help the Police Service to keep the peace in this country and to live up to expectations, so that their enviable international image will be protected," Mr. Boafo was quoted to have said.
Sadly, the police completely went to sleep on the Agbobloshie murders. Up till now, not a single member of the seven-member gang, identified as the perpetrators of the heinous crime, has been apprehended. Yesterday, many Ghanaians were wondering on various radio and television networks why the police was being selective in its delivery of justice.
Some recall the virtual commando raid enacted by the Ghana Police when Mr. Kennedy Agyapong, NPP Member of Parliament for Assin Central, voluntarily reported himself, in the infamous case of his outburst on a private radio station. Even when a court of competent jurisdiction has pronounced him not guilty and thus discharged him, the Attorney-General has given indications that Mr. Agyapong could be re-arrested and charged again over the same matter.
The notion here is that the police want to get at Mr. Agyapong at all cost, on the basis of his utterances as a member of the opposition.
Yesterday's committal of the four NPP activists has rekindled the case of the Agbobloshie murders. Five and a half years after the murders so foul, the perpetrators are still walking on the streets of Accra and other parts of the country.
Members of the NPP, especially, have pointed to similar treatments of their supporters in Chereponi, Atiwa, Akwatia and many other parts of the country. There is no indication that the police have shown any interest in these atrocities.
It is interesting that with the Supreme Court verdict on the Electoral Petition awaited on August 29, the citizenry is being bombarded with messages calling for peace. It is strange that none of the many religious leaders calling for peace has demanded justice, which alone, could promote peace.
On this occasion, I salute the National Chief Imam. Sheikh Ousman Nuhu Sharabutu is the only religious leader linking peace with the need for justice.
