"Our women are worst affected by this onslaught because of the manner their fundamental rights are being abused with impunity. They are constantly being harassed, especially on market days whenever they take their wares to the market," he said.
Audi further said apart from the constant harassment they face from security operatives, the local Banana and Musku tribes with whom they have cohabited for decades are threatening them with violence.
According to him, the attack by the locals, which started in 2004, made the locals to bar them from fishing.
"For close to three months, our economy has been relegated to the background following the directive given by the local tribes that we should fish no more, this has never happened to us," he lamented.
He added that following the renewed violence, in which they were the targets, they were left with no option than to flee the area for fear of being killed.
He explained that their plight was worsened by the fact that security operatives who should have come to their rescue when they were being attacked were treating their complaints with levity.
"We have been living in that area for the past 70 years when our grandfathers settled in the area but we never had it so bad following the discrimination from local residents," he added.
He said they were forced to bring their case to the public after they had reported their alleged maltreatment to the Nigerian ambassador to Cameroun and no action was taken to address the situation.
Audi said while they were fleeing, they were attacked during which one person was severely wounded.
He appealed to both the Nigerian and Cameroonian authorities to come to their rescue so that they could go back to fishing.

Original_Search_Title: CNPP Demands Orubebe, Madueke, Ogiadomhe's Sack
Headline: Nigeria: CNPP Demands Orubebe, Madueke, Ogiadomhe's Sack
Body_Text:
Group says Nigeria is sliding into a Banana Republic.
The Conference of Nigeria Political Parties, CNPP, has asked President Goodluck Jonathan to immediately relieve the Minister of Niger Delta Affairs, Godsday Orubebe, and his Petroleum Resource counterpart, Diezani Alison-Madueke, respectively, of their duties.
CNPP, in a statement by its Secretary General, Willy Ezeugwu, on Thursday, accused Mr. Orubebe of overheating the polity in his (Orubebe) native Delta State over his governorship ambition by defying a presidential directive on 2015 campaign.
It said the minister had continued to carry on with a larger than life image.
The group of opposition parties said unless Mr. Jonathan was not serious with his avowed war against corruption and political slandering and was only plating with the senses of Nigerians, should such public officers continue in office.
CNPP also said that what was happening in the oil sector was most damning as it shouldn't be that oil companies indicted in the fuel subsidy fraud were still doing business with the federal government.
"Due to vested interest, public officers in the oil sector led by the petroleum minister, Allison Madueke and other PPPRA officials have re-established business with fraudulent oil companies indicted in the oil subsidy probe," it said.
"This is most unacceptable and clearly a slap on the Nigerian people and seems to vindicate the saying that the Nigerian economy is ran on corruption. This we must rise against as a nation and force the President to do what is right and correct - sacking the minister and her cohorts."
The group also asked the President to sack his Chief of Staff, Mike Ogiadomhe, over his role in misleading the administration and fostering corruption.
It launched attack on the director general of the nation's satellite commission, Ahmad Rufai, noting that the latest embarrassment over the failure of the counting machine installed by the agency in the House of Representatives was just one of the many incompetence and fraud associated with the satellite agency since his (Rufai) era.
"If steps are not taken to relieve these public officers of their job immediately, we in union with the civil society will have no choice than to mobilise Nigerians on the street till this change is effected.
"We are gradually sliding into a Banana Republic where impunity continues to override our senses of responsibilities," CNPP warned.

Original_Search_Title: 2,000 Nigerian Fishermen Flee Cameroun Over Violence
Headline: Nigeria: 2,000 Nigerian Fishermen Flee Cameroun Over Violence
Body_Text:
No fewer than 2,000 Nigerian fishermen flee Cameroun as a result of violence and extortion by gendarmes and local tribes, LEADERSHIP reports.
The leader of the fishermen, Mr James Audi, who led a team of the fleeing fishermen made the revelation at the Correspondents' Chapel in Yola. He said that despite being in possession of residents permits, they were subjected to constant harassment, incarceration and extortion by gendarmes who seems not to accord recognition to the residency permit in their possession.
"We are always inundated by constant harassment, unjustifiable incarceration, intimidation and extortion in the hands of gendarmes. Our women are worst affected by this onslaught because of the manner their fundamental rights are being abused with impunity. They are constantly being harassed especially on market days whenever they take their wares to the market".
Audi added that apart from the constant harassment they face from security operatives, the local Banana and Musku tribes with whom they have cohabited for decades are threatening them with unfathomable level of discrimination and violence.
He said the attack by the locals started in 2004 when the local tribes launched an attack on them and killed one of their men called Bambur which led his pregnant wife to have a miscarriage.

Original_Search_Title: For Senator Pius Ewherido, A Tribute
Headline: Nigeria: For Senator Pius Ewherido, A Tribute
Body_Text:
OUR paths crossed. We were friends but our bond was brotherly. He was a senior and ever counselling brother. He was always ready to make the age argument. Hence, it was his entitlement at some point when we shared a room in Fajuyi Hall, OAU, Ife, to sleep below while I slept atop our double bunk bed.
We entered the University of Ife together, did all our 101s, 102s and all 100 level courses together. We attended all the great Jingo (Dr. Dipo Fasina) lectures, the Dr. Geoff Tangwa lectures, etc, in philosophy. Pius left the university earlier than me, graduating in B.A. Philosophy; I followed shortly with LL.B. We graduated from Obafemi Awolowo University, even though we were admitted into the University of Ife by JAMB.
Our times together, which continued after Ife, were great and memorable. We were Catholics and did share many other bonds beyond books, politics and academics. He was highly cerebral, articulate, focused, organised. A role model; a great strategist and a futurist.
A philosopher, indeed and in truth. We did "practise" and certainly had some stint in campus journalism as freelancers. We "anchored" at The Bee (a campus newspaper), did our pranks, stung, bugged and buzzed in accordance with the best ethics of campus journalism.
This was during the anti-intellectual Buhari-Idiagbon military dictatorship when our teachers were hounded as those who were "teaching what they were not paid to teach". At that time, even our rival and perhaps more known publication, The Cobra, was catapulted into national limelight by the military junta which described it as "a subversive publication" in a nationwide broadcast.
Pius went ahead and won election into the Students Representative Council, SRC, where he distinguished himself as a great debater. He cut his political teeth in the Great Ife tradition. Activism was in his blood; responsible and purposeful activism, that is. He took time to equip himself for service. He obtained a law degree at Uniben after graduating from Ife while cutting his teeth as an entrepreneur.
He made his entrepreneurial debut modestly in entertainment, catering, rental and hospitality services. Plying his trade in business and services his contacts spread quickly, fuelled by his public-spirited disposition to clients and patrons. He lived with his people in the Warri axis and got to know them extremely well while serving them. Whenever he visited me in Little Road, Yaba for Lagos weekends, he was the same big brother, humble, full of vision and passion for service. We reminisced over our days in Ife. We were most pained and challenged by the injustice of June 12. He admired my occasional newspaper commentaries on the raging subject of June 12. We said "never again" together.
It was hardly surprising that when the opportunity beckoned for him to run for the Delta House of Assembly to represent his native Effurun, no one else stood a better chance. He had warmed himself to his peoples' hearts. He knew them, they knew him. He was genuine and generous, caring from the heart of service and not out of opportunism.
A politician with non-severable bond with his constituents was born. It is rare in Nigeria, but Senator Ewherido demonstrated that such a genuine connection was possible. That was the very secret of his strength as a legendary politician. That was why he was able to "kill" giants and became a cat with nine lives. But it is a lesson that those who kept courting and who are presently mourning him would not learn.
His infectious personality, charm and charisma had no hiding place in Delta State House of Assembly. Rather, they came to public affirmation from that Chamber, where he served for eight years, and spread across the state. While officially the Deputy Speaker, he was the Acting Speaker for most of his tenure, owing to the ill-health of the substantive speaker and to all the impeachment dramas that marked his tenure. As a speaker, he steered the Delta State House of Assembly into a forum for constructive debate.
He did not miss the opportunity to draw from the wisdom of Nigeria's genuine patriots (even our own WS) whom he occasionally invited to address the House. I had opportunities of personal visits to him in Asaba at the height of his provincial legislative career. As a confident strategist and pragmatist, he already had a clear vision of his political career after the House of Assembly.
I was struck through one of the nights we spent together on how he talked with genuine concern and sympathy about the ill-health of his boss, the then speaker. After the speaker's death, he ably survived all the intrigues and banana pills of his office and did not hesitate to play the game of survival whenever that was inevitable. This was the time when James Ibori called the shots in Delta State.
Ewherido was truly a Deltan, a true Urhobo son and lover of Urhobo language, an Isoko grandson, and a worthy Ijaw in-law; at home with the Itsekiri and a good friend of the Anioma peoples. He was always conscious of his broad support base.
His desire to rule Delta State was motivated less by ambition than by a sense of duty and a commitment to right wrongs; to empower the weak. But such motivation was a wrong one in the eyes of PDP. A sense of duty, service, merit, justice and competence proved too ideal to thrive in the party Senator Ewherido then belonged. Those were the reason that the PDP could not trust him with the governorship of Delta State. Having been denied the opportunity to serve from the State House in Asaba, Ewherido bided his time. He was not manifestly bitter and was hardly malicious.
He spoke no ill of his adversaries. At the right time, he mobilized his people and got their consent to serve them in Abuja as a Senator. But he kept his eyes on the State House. He believed in the power of the executive to make tremendous and lasting impact on the people.
Prof. CHIDI OGUAMANAM wrote from Ottawa, Canada.

Original_Search_Title: Babe of the Week - Charitable Julian Crazy About Goodlyfe, Piercings
Headline: Uganda: Babe of the Week - Charitable Julian Crazy About Goodlyfe, Piercings
Body_Text:
Julian Mutoni is the peer learning co-coordinator and education publicist of the Violence Against Children programme in schools, under the children NGO Raising Voices.
The sassy Ugandan-bred Rwandan beauty is a social scientist with many passions but romance. She has a big heart for charity and will stop at nothing in her fight for violent-free childhoods, writes Prisca Baike.
What do you enjoy about your work?
I love spending time with children. They touch my heart in a special way and my work allows me to interact with so many of them. I can rightly say I am living my passion.
What do you find challenging about your work?
Not being in position to help people the way I want to. Sometimes you find children with pretty touching stories and you feel like changing their situation. You do all you can to help but at some point, you realise your hands are tied and you cannot go beyond that point. It is heartbreaking.
What are your favourite pastimes?
I love spending time with my nephew, reading novels, watching movies, especially the old non-romantic ones. I also like listening to music and trying out new places, especially those with loud music and fun- loving people.
What do many people find weird about you?
I don't watch romantic movies but I don't find that weird. I hate them because they give a skewed impression of love. They act and make it so flawless yet it is not like that in real life.
What is your biggest dream in life?
Having a home or place where I can help abandoned, abused and violated children. It will be a place where children can feel free, spirited to be themselves and do what they cannot do comfortably at home.
They will be able to explore their talents, share their problems, get advice and solutions, generally a place filled with love and care so they can smoothly transition from childhood to adulthood. I would also want to be a fashion designer in future, as a charity avenue.
So, who is the lucky guy?
I would rather keep him out of the public (blushes). He is a cool guy who, like me, does not believe in romance but reality. He once surprised me with flowers though we are not the romantic fairy tale-kind of lovers. We like to keep it real and simple with no exaggerations.
Every girl has a dream car. What is yours?
Sorry to disappoint you, I am not into cars. I, instead, love bikes. I get blown away by big nice bikes. They are outstanding.
And your favourite meal is...
(Laughs) It's posho and beans. My friends keep asking whether I did not have enough of it at school, making fun of it but I just like that meal. Even when there is other food on the menu, I ensure that I have a little posho and beans too. I also love home-made banana muffins.
Talking about schools, which ones did you attend?
I attended SDA schools because of my religion. I went to Lakeside College, Katikamu SDA and Midland high school before joining Uganda Christian University for a bachelor's degree in Social Work and Social Administration.
You cannot get enough of...
Jeans and nice shoes. I love flat shoes because they are so comfortable and so are jeans.
Favourite colour?
Green!
What are you crazy about?
I was hoping you would ask this. I am crazy about Goodlyfe music. Radio and Weasel are my best artistes. I have all their songs and I have not missed a single concert of theirs. Those two are very talented and their music is good.
What don't people know about you?
I love piercings on my body though I don't have many of them yet. I have five piercings and I hope to add more. Just like some people fancy tattoos, I like having studs on my body, but I take care not to get too many that will look weird.
Lessons learnt from your work.
Working with vulnerable women and children has taught me that there is too much violence and unfairness in the world. Even behind a smiling face, there could be a sad story. People are not as privileged and as happy as they look.
Your philosophy?
Do good to others, regardless of what has been done to you.

Original_Search_Title: Power Outages Hurt Rwandan Enterprises
Headline: Rwanda: Power Outages Hurt Rwandan Enterprises
Body_Text:
Kigali — Unreliable power supply is affecting the profit margins of many enterprises here experts have revealed.
Ernest Mazimpaka, a consultant in energy development, said last week with power outages most businesses which rely on electricity to operate are likely to register losses accruing from unreliable power supply.
"Frequent power outages result in forgone sales and damaged equipment for businesses, which result in significant losses," he said
"These losses are equivalent to six percent of turnover on average for firms in the formal sector and as much as 16% of turnover for informal sector enterprises that lack a back-up generator," he said.
Rwanda experiences the same power outage levels as many African countries.
This is one problem affecting Rwanda's competitiveness with the country's power tariffs at around $0.20kWh, which exceeds the already high regional averages of $ 0.10-0.12/kWh.
At the same time, Rwanda is facing a challenge of low investment in the energy sector, ageing energy assets and logistical constraints which worsen the supply-side crisis, among others.
Mazimpaka said scaling up energy investment infrastructure is a key priority in bringing down transport costs which are at $165/tonne/km almost twice higher than the region's average of $95/tonne/km.
"We need to see how we address these challenges if we are to make our private sector competitive," Antoine Manzi, Director of Employment at Private Sector Federation - (PSF) told East African Business Week.
Experts add that the country's electricity cost stand out to be highest in the East African region which in turn lowers competitiveness of the locally manufactured goods. For example, the average production cost of electricity is 210RFW/kWh while it is on average purchased at 155.76RFW with government footing the 54RFW difference.
" We are not getting much profits from selling our products at a high price, it is always the costs of production that put the prices higher. This is why we cannot compete in the regional markets," Christen Murebwayire, a banana wine producer said.
Murebwayire said Rwandan products like wine are able to beat other products from the region on international market due to quality and standards, an advantage coupled with lowering costs of production like electricity would give a good competitive edge.

Original_Search_Title: Youth Minister Unveils Motorized Boat
Headline: Sierra Leone: Youth Minister Unveils Motorized Boat
Body_Text:
Minister of Youth Affairs, Alimamy A. Kamara, over the weekend launched the newly motorized fishing boat of Harmony Organization, which awarded scholarship to 6 school-going children of the Banana Water (Oshoro) community at Murray Town in the west-end of the city.
Addressing residents of the community, the minister said President Koroma was impressed with Harmony Organization's positive action towards the youths and that they have discovered their place in destiny. He added that every youth has the dynamic ability to effect change, noting that based on what the organization has done, he is willing to work with them to ensure the Agenda for Prosperity impact the lives of youths across the country.
"Harmony Organization has launched the Prosperity Agenda in practical form to support the president's initiative. Harmony is worth supporting and I can stand as guarantor for the organization," he said, while admonishing the youthsto maintain the equipment which has been given them.
He informed his audience about plans being put in place by President Ernest Bai Koroma for youths who are ready to work.
Chairman of Harmony Organization, James Bangura, said the organization was formed in 1996 with the sole aim of assisting the community in developing activities such as fighting drug abuse and violenceamong young people, and feeding the needy.
He said the amount used to build the boat was fifty million Leones (Le50,000,000) which, he said, was raised by members and from other partners. The organization, he said, has employed eight people to start work.
Bangura stated that the scholarship funds were donated to them by the Syria Scholarship Fund in order to support the less privileged children from primary to secondary school in the 2013/2014 academic year in that part of the city.
He assured the community that by 2015 the organization will be transformed into Harmony Group of Companies to champion the development cause of the Banana Water community. "During the past years we have gone through a lot of challenges but we have been able to prove to the country and community that yes we can do it," Bangura stated.
Deputy Bank Governor and Board Chairman of the organization, Ms. Adrian R. Conteh on her part said she was representing the organization and not the Bank of Sierra Leone, noting that the organization has a vision which is unlike that of most NGOs and CBOs,which she said always look for donor funds.
She encouraged the youths to have role models which will help them become useful members of society and help others change for the better.

Original_Search_Title: President Jonathan and a Militarized Polity
Headline: Nigeria: President Jonathan and a Militarized Polity
Body_Text:
Long years of military rule have affected the psyche of Nigerians and converted it to a polity of 'immediate effect' and 'kill and go' mentality. Obasanjo's long years in democratic governance changed nothing, as he stoutly resisted all efforts of the legislature and even the judiciary to pave way to a true democratic rule.
The coming into office of the late Umaru Yar'adua in 2007 actually began the real process of democratic rule. He introduced and insisted on the principles of Rule of Law and Due Process. It was not easy for the quintessential gentle man, as Nigerians adjudged him the slowest President on earth because of the militarized life they have come to unconsciously accept. When Yar'adua died and President Goodluck Ebele Jonathan took over, he too refused to run the country as jackboot emperor and maintained democratic principles to demilitarize the polity. In fact, at a point, following the undue pressure to become 'tough', he had to tell the nation that he was neither an army despot nor a pharaoh of Egypt whose words were law and disobedience meant death. He wanted to be a civilian president under the law, not above it.
Despite the presidential clarification, those Nigerians who attacked Yar'adua as being irredeemably slow and uninspiring, still descended on President Jonathan in a very heavy manner with such unprintable names, the least of all calling him 'clueless'. What such Nigerians fail to understand is that Nigeria is a nation of multidimensional parts, diversities and ethnicity. The ethnic nationalities alone are over 250, mostly illiterate and yet to be meshed into the necessary unity that is found in advanced countries and thriving economies.
Beyond the nation's diversity, the military left behind a bitterly divided country; since the civil war and the annulment of June 12, the Nigerian nation has not been the same again: it has been the north versus the south, Muslims versus Christians and so on.
The successive military governments took their turn to revert the country's federalism to a unitary system; to the point that fiscal federalism is now extinct. The 1999 Constitution, which the military bequeathed, bears an eloquent testimony of being more unitary than federal, up to the point that states cannot as much as generate electricity and distribute to its citizens. What the Constitution allows is that States can only generate power for the national grid, where from who gets what can be decided.
The military endured since its first incursion in coming 1966 and remained so until the Shagari interregnum interrupted it in 1979. Even at that, the military never allowed the young nation to face up to the real issues of nationhood , as General Muhammadu Buhari soon found an excuse to boot out the democratically elected government and replaced it with his self-righteous and self-serving one.
So, if one really looks at it, it is only now that the country has had the breathing space to work on democracy and undo what the military did against federalism. To truly unite the country will not happen overnight.
In a military system, the head of state can announce any decision, no matter how far-reaching, from his bed, without any approval of the military Council. In the military system, the executive and legislative arms are fused in one arm in theory but in practice, all the three arms are fused in a single man called the head of state.
In a democracy, particularly in a Presidential system, the three arms are separate (though coordinate as they say) yet, jealously guarded, guided and defended by their leaders. So, even if a President wishes to operate at the speed of lightning, the other arms must operate at the same speed for that aim to be achieved. The legislature makes laws, the executive implements while the judiciary interprets. One major law is the Appropriation Act, which up till now remains very slow in coming into force in Nigeria and this cannot be determined by the President alone or the executive arm. The National Assembly will have to do its job, which is constitutionally assigned and cannot be circumvented.
Where there are violations of the law, it is the judiciary that must say so. The executive arm can only take the suspect/matter to court and wait for the colonial judicial process that runs slowly into months, sometimes, years to take its course.
The Judicial system inherited by the Jonathan administration is weak and obsolete. For example, both the Penal Code and Criminal Code are colonial enactments and carry sentences that are now laughable against contemporary realities. The procedural act allows injunctions that can keep a substantive matter in abeyance for years also. Such is the justice system met by President Jonathan. That explains why John Yusuf Yakubu of the pensions scam fame got away with the misappropriation of up to twenty billion naira by merely paying a paltry seven hundred and fifty thousand naira in a so-called plea bargain.
Many have written off the anti-corruption fight of the present administration as a charade and blamed President Jonathan for such judgments as if he made those laws or asked the culprits to be charged under them. Whereas the fact is: it is the duty of the legislature to change these laws if they are obsolete and unproductive. Yet, it is the President such Nigerians blame.
There is no doubt that the President is the head of government, but in a presidential system, there is an extent he can meddle in the affairs of the other arms and it will become interference. The quintessence of the doctrine of separation of powers, which is a hallmark of the presidential system of government, is that the three arms must maintain and exert considerable autonomy, if not outright independence. In Nigeria, since the return of the country to democratic/civil rule, Obasanjo who started it, attempted to muscle the other arms and this led to perennial fight between the executive and the legislature and precipitated the fabled banana peels which brought three senate presidents down in heaps in two years or so.
Yar'adua and Jonathan refused to follow the Obasanjo ugly examples and the polity is the better for it. Both Jonathan and Yar'adua insisted on due process and rule of law and shunned the allure of arbitrariness and impunity. Yet, such Nigerians, rather than give these democrats par excellence credit for being on the side of the masses, called both men names and questioned their claim to purposeful leadership.
Nigerians must change their fascist attitude while interrogating democratic ethos, in order to benefit from the fruits of democracy. President Jonathan, like Yar'adua, is a good man and more importantly, a visionary democrat.
Nigerians have to be the change they want to see. To make a democracy, you must first make democrats. The polity is suffering from the overhang of such military mentality, which has led many to expecting omelets without first breaking eggs or expecting the delivery of a baby in 9 weeks instead of 9 months.
Things don't work that way. Presidential democracy is slow, cumbersome and expensive. It is good for Nigeria though. If we don't like it we can change it and stop blaming President Jonathan.
Dr Kaigama is a medical practitioner based in Port Harcourt

Original_Search_Title: Black Tunisians Suffer Discrimination
Headline: Tunisia: Black Tunisians Suffer Discrimination
Body_Text:
Tunis — Racial discrimination was rarely discussed in Tunisia. And then a publicity photo for supermarket giant Carrefour brought the issue to the fore.
The Carthage Eagles were in Freetown for their 2014 World Cup qualifier against Sierra Leone on June 8th. Carrefour, which sponsors the national team, posted a picture on its Facebook page of Tunisian footballers giving bananas to black children.
The picture outraged rights organisations for its alleged "racist" intimations.
Despite the withdrawal of the ad, activists Blamassi Touré and Yamina Thabet insisted on pursuing the agency to demand it finance a project that combats racism.
According to Touré, who heads the Association of African Students and Trainees in Tunisia (AESAT), there are some 8,000 African students in Tunisia.
"None of them are happy," he told La Presse last month.
Black Tunisians face the same discrimination as the Africans, activists say.
Tunisia was the first Arab or Muslim country to abolish slavery.
But 167 years after Ahmad Bey bin Mustafa ended the practice of slavery, the after-effects are still visible.
In the Medenine town of Sidi Makhlouf, separate school buses are used for black children. The slur "oussif" (slave) is still used against blacks, and there is no legislative protection for victims of racial discrimination.
During the celebration of the International Day for the Elimination of Racial Discrimination on March 21st, Constituent Assembly President Mustapha Ben Jaafar called for "the need to empower black citizens to have access to senior positions in the country".
But more than promises are needed, according to Yamina Thabet. She heads the Tunisian Association for the Support of Minorities, which joined in the protest against the Carrefour ad.
"Many reports confirmed the marginalisation faced by this group for more than half a century, despite the fact that Tunisia was a frontrunner in eliminating slavery in the nineteenth century," she tells Magharebia.
The absence of black citizens from state censuses has been going on since independence, she says.
"They weren't awarded real opportunities to participate in public life and to access posts of responsibility in management, government jobs and the diplomatic corps," Thabet adds.
"The situation is no different in the media, despite the presence of highly qualified people from this group already successful in several areas," she says.
The new attention to the issue of racism in Tunisia did not just begin with the banana commercial.
On May 1st, the M'Nemty ("My Dream") Association led a march in the streets of the capital to denounce racism and promote co-existence and equality. The Tunis rally was organised to coincide with Labour Day.
"As suggested by its name, M'Nemty is a dream: the dream of justice and equality in a beautiful and diverse Tunisia," President Saadia Mosbah tells Magharebia.
In order to end discrimination, racism and hatred, she says, "The culture of citizenship is important."
The activist explains: "One of our goals is to sensitise young people through their families, which are their first school. We also urge institutions to carry out their responsibilities through the promotion of an education that encourages the rejection of racism and hatred."
"We are switching to high gear to say that racism lives within our walls and is going beyond all reasonable limits. For example, taxis refuse to stop to dark-skinned Africans and sub-Saharan Africans are exposed to all forms of violence," Mosbah says.
"We are all the fruits of one tree," she adds.
The head of another rights group is focused on change through legislation.
"ADAM for Equality and Development" chief Taoufik Chairi is working to realise a judicial system that preserves the rights of minorities.
"The judicial system in our country does not do justice to blacks and there is no law criminalising racist abuses," Chairi says.
"Many black-skinned people who are exposed to abuses prefer not to file suits, since no brown-skinned Tunisian citizen has ever succeeded in winning a case or punishing those who commit racist attacks against them," the activist adds.
Lawyer Emna Zahrouni confirms Chairi's claim that there is an absence of clear legal text on penalising racism.
According to Zahrouni, the only jurisprudence that comes anywhere near the issue is the anti-terrorism law, which speaks about "criminalising advocacy of hatred on religious or ethnic grounds".
The Tunisian Criminal Code criminalises hatred between races, religions or ethnicities. Violations are punishable by a fine and a prison sentence of up to three years.
Still, equality is an uphill battle for black Tunisians. Black-skinned people in Tunisia complain about being excluded from top positions in the country.
"In the judiciary, for example, we find only ten judges out of a total of 1,600," Zahrouni notes.
The media show a similar lack of racial diversity. Najiba Hamrouni, the president of the National Union of Tunisian Journalists, suffered a relentless harassment campaign because of her positions regarding media freedom in the country.
She was even described as a "slave" because of her skin colour.
There are no official figures on the number of blacks in Tunisia, but some speak of a range between 10 to 15 per cent of the population (11.5 million).
Hamza Ben Bshir, a black Tunisian citizen and holder of a university degree, says: "There is an exclusion of black-skinned people from all government positions and senior positions in the public service, despite the availability of qualified people."
"They've reached the point of not considering us Tunisians," he adds.

Original_Search_Title: Ramadan - High Demands Spark Up Fruit Prices At Zuba
Headline: Nigeria: Ramadan - High Demands Spark Up Fruit Prices At Zuba
Body_Text:
With the commencement of the Muslims' Holy month of Ramadan, demands for fruits have gone up in Abuja which has also led to increase in the prices of the items in the markets.
Fruits of various forms are recommended to be consumed by Muslim faithful during the break of fast.
A survey carried out by City News yesterday revealed other reasons for the increase in the prices of fruits during the period to include allegations of greed and exploitation on the part of suppliers and sellers of the products.
At the FCT wholesale fruit market in Zuba, many traders interviewed said they now get the supply from far away states like Kastina and Sokoto, adding that the cost of transporting them to Zuba keeps increasing on daily basis. According to the traders, the supply from neighbouring states like Benue and Nasarawa could no longer meet the demands of the people because of the fasting period, hence they have to be taking deliveries from far away states and as such their prices need to be re-adjusted. An orange supplier, Mustafa Salihu, said a bag of orange which sold for between N3,500 and N4,000 depending on the brand, before the fasting month, now goes for between N5,000 and N6,000 respectively.
A trader at the market, Rotimi Taiwo, who was busy arranging his banana when our reporter approached him, said a banana of 'three head' which sold for N1,500, now goes for N2,500.
A water melon wholesaler, Salihu Abdulrashid, said a dozen of water melon, previously sold for between N1,500 and N2,000, now goes for between N2,500 and N3,000.
According to the traders at the market, a dozen of big pineapples, which previously sold for N2,000 now goes for N3,000.
However, there are some fruits whose prices still remain steady. These include Sour sop, sweet melon, and compeer among other items.
A trader, Mr. James Otabo, said the reason for the stability of their prices may not be unconnected to the fact that the level of their demand is rather declining instead of rising like fruits such as orange, banana, mango and pineapple.

Original_Search_Title: Standards Body Closes Down Fake Wine Factory
Headline: Rwanda: Standards Body Closes Down Fake Wine Factory
Body_Text:
A factory that has been making substandard banana wine in Murama cell, Kinyinya sector in Gasabo district has been closed by the Rwanda Bureau of Standards (RBS).
About 10,000 litres of Dukeshinkera Banana Wine worth millions of francs were also impounded.
Philip Nzaire, the RBS quality assurance director, said the wine produced by Dukeshinkera and Dushime Banana Wines did not meet standards.
He added that the managing director of the firm, Beatrice Mukashyaka, was using fake documents she claimed to have obtained from the standards body.
"The wine is full of molasses, which makes it unfit for human consumption," Nzaire said, adding that Mukashyaha would be prosecuted for endangering people's lives.
He warned manufacturers to always observe standards as prescribed by RBS or risk losing their businesses.
However, Mukashyaka insisted that she was producing according to standards, arguing that she got the idea of starting up the business from RBS.
Selemani Semana, the Murama cell chairman, advised RBS to always engage local authorities if the problem of selling or producing fake goods is to be stopped.
"We knew she was selling genuine wine because whenever we came to inspect the factory for hygiene, she would present these documents... since we cannot tell the difference between the fake and genuine ones, there was nothing we could do about it. RBS should sensitise local authorities to understand these issues," Semana said.
He said the factory had been operating for months now.
Last month, Mark Cyubahiro Bagabe, the standards body director general, warned importers and makers of fake goods, saying RBS would not allow Rwanda to be turned into a dumping ground of counterfeit products.

Original_Search_Title: Street Vendors - Convenient for People, Bad for Business
Headline: Rwanda: Street Vendors - Convenient for People, Bad for Business
Body_Text:
While Kigali City Council has declared war on street vendors a long time ago, along the Nyabugogo road, Jacques Ndabaruzi goes about his daily businesses of selling jeans trousers. He says it earns him enough to get food and a roof over his head, but not nearly enough to start a formal business.
"On the street, you can start business even if you have peanuts, but if you want a place in the market you need serious money," he explains.
The fact that his street business implies that he can get jailed and his jeans confiscated, is a risk Ndabaruzi is willing to take.
It is the same for Mariette Yabaragiye, who sells fruit and vegetables in Nyamirambo. "For 5000 francs I can buy enough to sell in different houses here, but at the market you cannot even get a stall for that price," she says.
Those who work at the market, on the other hand, accuse the street vendors of ruining their business. "There is very little business here; why would someone come to the market while they can buy everything on the steet?" asks Salim Mukandekezi, a vendor of vegetables at Biryogo market, who says it is high time the authorities take measures to protect their business. "We pay taxes but still earn nothing while others are selling outside for free!"
If you can't beat them, join them, seems to be the reasoning of some of the market vendors. One of Mukandekezi's colleagues admits that she and others at the market also venture in the street after hours to increase their income. "I have a stall here but I often go out with some bananas to see whether I can catch customers at home as we are not getting them here," she explains.
And for most customers, what matters is convenience. "When I need something, I buy it where I can get it. Many street vendors have things that I need, so why should I go to a market when I can get it brought to me?" asks Emmanuel Nshimiyimana, who lives in Nyamirambo.
That is also the opinion of his neighbor Vedaste Ngiruwonsanga, at least when it comes to foodstuff which he mostly buys from street vendors in the neighborhood. Yet for more pricey goods, he goes to the market. "When I want to buy clothes for instance I go to the market where they have a lot of choice; I am not going to take whatever the street vendors bring to me," he says.
But while consumers, street merchants and market vendors have established some kind of modus vivendi, KCC PR man Bruno Rangira is adamant that street vending is illegal and has a negative impact on legal trade, thus slowing down development of the city and reducing its income.
"Street vending kills business and competition as it is difficult to know where they are working for their recognition," he observes.
Yet he adds that measures are taken to eradicate the illegal trade, such as fines and confiscating their products. In addition, KCC tries to help them to adopt formal trade by helping them through a basket fund and building their capacity on how to do business by training. He stresses that eradication of street vending is among the major priorities of the city, and that a general census is planned in the new strategic plan to find a lasting solution.
According to Rangira, Kigali city has currently more than six thousand street vendors. That is the same number KCC has been giving for years now, and the measures cited by Rangira are the same that the city has always claimed to be implementing.
Maybe it is time for KCC to take some real action.

Original_Search_Title: Why Tambuwal Is 'Untouchable' in PDP - BOT Member
Headline: Nigeria: Why Tambuwal Is 'Untouchable' in PDP - BOT Member
Body_Text:
Both President Goodluck Jonathan and his party, Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) are fed up with the Speaker, House of Representatives, Hon Aminu Tambuwal over his alleged romance with the opposition but there is nothing they could do about it but waiting for when he would step on the proverbial banana peels, a member of the PDP Board of Trustees (BoT) confided this in LEADERSHIP.
Since his emergence as the number four citizen in the country, Tambuwal, has not ceased having what his party described 'unholy romance' with the opposition that brought him to power at the expense of the PDP discredited zoning arrangement.
Hosting the leadership of the Rivers State chapter of the party, President Goodluck Jonathan took the likes of the speaker and Governor Rotimi Amaechi to cleaners accusing them of flirting with the opposition at the expense of the party that brought them into office.
But the PDP chieftain said, suspend Tambuwal from the PDP is to kick their members in the House out because they would follow him.
The speaker, in a tactical response told the President that party supremacy should not be at the expense of democracy. And speaking in Benin, Edo State capital at the birthday and silver jubilee wedding anniversary of the Deputy Governor of Edo State, Dr Pius Odubu, Tambuwal agains told his cynics that in the House, it is Nigeria before politics.
But speaking with our correspondent in Abuja yesterday, the chieftain of PDP expressed concern of the party for the flirting of the Tambuwals, Nyakos and Amaechis but regretted that his party lacks what it takes to call them to order.
He said, "No one is happy with the public appearance of the speaker with the opposition. Remember how he got to the office, it was the same opposition that brought him to power all because of indiscipline of some of our members. We have zoning arrangement but he and a few others sabotaged the zoning arrangement. This is why we are unable to stop him and we may not be able to do so. One of the greatest fears of the PDP is that if Tambuwal is suspended or expelled, majority of our members in the House have threatened to follow him to whichever party he is going. And that will make the PDP a minority party in the House.
"If we asked him to resign as the Speaker, he would not do that, and we have no power to remove him despite the fact that we have the majority of the members but we have no control over them. This is our dilemma. When last did you see Tambuwal with our governors the way he is doing with the opposition? We even heard that he might defect to contest the presidential poll come 2015 yet he remains on that exalted seat." The former national officer of the party urged the interim leadership of the party "to work hard and wield those rebels within our party before the next convention in order to move forward. Look at what Amaechi is saying, the party ordered him to reinstate the leadership of the local government he teleguided the House to sack, but is he saying the House is stronger than him? The likes of Murtala Nyako would not take a directive from the National Chairman of the party, is that good? Some of them would be attacking the party that brought them to office on the pages of newspapers. Is that good? The earlier we sanitise the party before election time the better for us." 2015 Presidency: North will negotiate properly - Governor Aliyu
Meanwhile, the Chairman of Northern states Governors' Forum NGSF and Niger State Governor Babangida Aliyu has said that as the politics towards 2015 is gathering momentum the north would negotiate properly with whoever want to be president.
Aliyu, who spoke in Edati local government area of the state while commissioning some project, also stated that the people should not listen to politicians in Abuja who would want to distort the state Zoning arrangements of political offices.
He said, "Some people, very few of them are holding nocturnal meetings, that they are telling Jonathan that they are the only supporters of Jonathan. In our own case, we are supporters of constitution of Nigeria. Whoever got this power we are in support of him.
"But what we are saying this time around in 2015 is we will negotiate, we will follow whoever gets this power, and we will support whoever gets this power. But we will ask for fulfilment of promises and redemption for the work that we insist for our people, that is all we are saying."
The outspoken Governor stated that the negotiation has become imperative in view of the need the safeguard the interest of the north even as he urged the people to always asked elected leaders to deliver on their campaign promises
Aliyu, who commissioned workers Village constructed at N215 million by Edati local government council, said that the North was ready and prepared to follow whoever is duly elected in 2015 as president.
He stated that where ever the next president comes from, either north or anywhere in the country, what he is after is a leader that will make people to feel the impact of leadership.
Aliyu said that the state would continue to maintain its present zoning arrangements and would no tolerate introduction of tribal and religious sentiment into the polity, "here in Niger, we do not play the politic of religion as such attitude would negate the development drive of the state and the country at large" he added.
Meanwhile, Aliyu has stated that he does not hold unto the funds of local government councils as exemplified by Edati local government council saying that "Edati Local Government Chairman, Alhaji Isah Kantigi, has proved right by the projects executed today. Kantigi said he had slashed the council over head by 50 per cent to enable the council funds the N260 million projects, which included offices for federal staff deployed to the area, magistrate court and 15 kilometres road project.

Original_Search_Title: Osun Lawmaker Faults PDP's Attack On ACN
Headline: Nigeria: Osun Lawmaker Faults PDP's Attack On ACN
Body_Text:
Sequel to attacks unleashed on seven members of the Action Congress of Nigeria (ACN) at Yekemi Abata Egba in Ife East Local Government Area of Osun State on Wednesday, by thugs suspected to be members of the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP), the lawmaker representing Ife Federal Constituency, Hon. Rotimi Makinde, has condemned the assault, urging the police to bring the culprits to book.
In a statement made available to THISDAY Saturday, Makinde lamented the spate of violence from the PDP in recent times in Ife, stressing that the PDP has invented a scheme to disrupt the serenity of Ife and its environs ahead of the 2014 governorship election in the state.
Members of the ACN were on Wednesday attacked during a rally organised by the party in the area. It was reported that thugs armed with guns, cutlasses and cudgels attacked the rally, thereby bringing the political gathering to a halt.
It was gathered that seven members of the ACN, namely: Oyesiji Michael, Rebbeca Omole, Femi Adesoji, Posi Adewoyin, Taofeek Oke, Sunday Omowaiye and a yet-to-be identified member were attacked and are currently receiving medical treatment.
"PDP is gradually positioning Ife and by extension, the state of Osun towards anarchy thus enabling them declare state of emergency. To the PDP, this is the only option left for them to terminate the appreciable transformation of Aregbesola that we are witnessing," he added.
He pointed out that the 'attack on Ife East ACN chairman and six others is barbaric and condemnable, saying 'the elements involved in the act must be brought to book, harping that the peace and tranquillity that the state has been known with is about being thwarted.
While citing similar attacks aimed at him, Makinde noted that five innocent souls including his brothers, were killed by hired assassins who came to his residence looking for him on the eve of the National Assembly election in 2011.
"Violence and killings are the stock in trade of the PDP. However, we are calling on security agencies to bring these violence tides under control before the 2014 gubernatorial election in the state because we are not in a banana republic," he said.

Original_Search_Title: President Dares People in Same-Sex Unions
Headline: Zimbabwe: President Dares People in Same-Sex Unions
Body_Text:
PRESIDENT Mugabe yesterday challenged people involved in same sex relationships to fall pregnant and produce children, failing which they should face jail.
Speaking during the launch of the Zanu-PF election manifesto and election campaign at a star rally at the Zimbabwe Grounds in Highfield, Harare yesterday, the President said it
was shameful that US President Barack Obama who is of African parentage insisted on promoting gays and lesbians.
He lamented that the problem had spilled into some churches that were also solemnising same sex marriages, a taboo in many African communities.
"Iyezvino kwauya nyaya yevanozviti maKristu. Zvino varikuti murume nemurume ngavadanane. Hameno kuti bhaibheri ravava kuverenga nderipi rinoti dananai mukadzi nemukadzi kana murume nemurume. Zvino vanga chata?
"Asi zvichinzi ivava vachata, vachaita vana, vaani vacho? Asi pane paungavazaririre mumba kwegore tigoona mimba yacho inobuda ipapo. Mukatadza kuburitsa mimba ipapo mose mujere nokuti munenge matika muchaita vana," he said to loud cheers.
President Mugabe said he was shocked by US president Obama who was saying African countries should embrace same sex marriages, adding such practices were not tolerated in Africa and in Zimbabwe in particular.
Some Western countries were forcing African countries to embrace same sex marriages as a pre-condition for aid, but President Mugabe has remained steadfast on his position, describing sexual deviants as "worse than pigs and dogs".
"Zvino anokuzvarirai vana ndiani. Saka huwori ihwohwo tinoti kwete. Huwori ihwohwo hwapinda nekuChechi. Archbishop of Canterbury kutochatisa varume. Hameno kuti anoti mukadzi ndiani kana murume ndiani.
"Vakadzi nekwavo vane huwori hwavo. Kubva kwabuda mumwe achiti ndini murume ipapo. Chauri kuti uri murume chii? Tiratidze. Ndo zvataramba izvozvo. Kana pane vane vana vavo vari kuDiaspora vaudzei kuti kuno zvinoera hazvidiwe. Takatonga vanwe vakuru tikavaendesa kujere nenyaya iyoyi," he said in apparent reference to Professor Canaan Sodindo Banana.
The MDC formations tried to smuggle homosexuality into the new Constitution and were only stopped by Zanu-PF vigilance.
However, Zanu-PF triumphed and the new Constitution is clear that marriage shall be between people of opposite sex and of legally acceptable age.

Original_Search_Title: Lawmaker's Ambulance Leads Maternal Mortality Fight in Needy Bong County
Headline: Liberia: Lawmaker's Ambulance Leads Maternal Mortality Fight in Needy Bong County
Body_Text:
Panta District, Bong County- Accessing health care in isolated villages in rural Liberia can be difficult especially for pregnant mothers who are about to give birth.
Mothers who are in these areas cannot be accessed to the maternity centers to give birth due to deplorable roads across the country.
If it is an emergency case, the mother risks losing her baby or even die. Some stay home to be nursed by elderly women or traditional birth attendants, who may not have enough skills to help a woman deliver.
The purchase of a 12-seat ambulance two months ago by Rep. Lester Paye (Independent district 4) for citizens of Panta district has helped curtailed maternal death during child delivery, according to health officials.
They told FrontPageAfrica Thursday because of the ambulance more mothers are delivering their babies. They also said the ambulance can navigate difficult roads in isolated villages, especially when there is heavy rain fall.
In Panta, the ambulance is situated in Foequelleh, the district's headquarters, to serve nearby towns and villages.
Barbara Brown, a health worker at the Foequelleh clinic told FPA in an interview that the region being a mountainous area, the ambulance is assigned to far-to-reach towns and villages.
"The ambulance helps convey people who live far distance to make things easier. And that has helped reduce the number of maternal deaths," she said.
Brown said unlike in the past where most pregnant mothers in the rural areas either delivered at home with the help of elderly women or traditional birth attendants, more mothers in the district where the ambulance has been allocated now deliver in health units.
She said, "In Foequellehtown a total of 10 mothers delivered at the clinic May, an increment of 15 per cent in June. There are areas in the district that become inaccessible especially when rain falls. The ambulance cannot everywhere."
Brown further told FrontPageAfrica statistics at the health center indicate that a total of 18 mothers delivered across the district in May and 21 in June.
She also said that many towns and villages often deliver mothers in unhygienic conditions, for instance, trying the umbilical cord of the newborn baby with a banana fiber, which she said increases the rate of infection.
According to Rep. Lester Paye, one of the main reasons of procuring the ambulance in April was to reduce the current rate of maternal mortality.
Panta district's current maternal mortality is at 20 per cent, according to the Foequelleh clinic statistic.

Original_Search_Title: Is Our Country a Banana Republic?
Headline: Kenya: Is Our Country a Banana Republic?
Body_Text:
I know you are puzzled and wondering but that is what the recently released Failed States Index 2013 published by the Fund for Peace in the United States silently says.
Kenya is sandwiched between Nigeria and Niger at position 17. The term, banana republic was coined by American writer William Sydney Porter (he wrote under the pen name O Henry) to refer to countries that are politically unstable and have limited resources in terms of exports.
The term has evolved since the early 20th century to refer to countries that are undergoing political turmoil or conflicts or other problems that weaken its governance and economy. Kenya has had its share of nightmares with the ethnic related post-election violence of 2007-08. And now it has al Shabaab to worry about since the Somalia invasion in 2011.
So, is Kenya a banana republic? According to factors outlined in the FSI 2013 like security apparatus, violation of human rights and rule of law, progressive deterioration of public services among other factors, Kenya performed poorly. It was position 16 in 2011 and 2012, 13 in 2010, 14 in 2009, 26 in 2008 and 31 in 2007. The FSI data is normally collected from the previous year and analysed then published in June every year. That explains the 2007-08 ranking.
The FSI ranks countries based on 12 factors on a scale of 1-10 with 10 being the worst and 1 being the best. The overall score for Kenya in FSI 2013 is 99.6/120 with the worst variable being the mounting demographic pressures. There is also a rise of factionalised elites and groups seeking vengeance for their grievances.
South Sudan--Africa's newest republic--made its debut in the FSI in 2012 but was not ranked since the data available did not constitute a whole year. In 2013, it was ranked court while sandwiched between Chad and South Sudan.
Uganda is ranked 22 which is far better than Kenya. In 2007-08, it was ranked 15 and 16 respectively. As Uganda progresses, Tanzania, which was ranked 75 and 76 in 2007-08 respectively, is now 65 in 2013. Like Kenya, it has regressed. In 2007, Somalia was ranked third after Iraq and Sudan (before the birth of South Sudan) but from 2008 it has been ranked first, making it the most fragile place to live in out of the 178 countries in the FSI.
Niger, Syria, North Korea, Egypt, Iran, Libya and Colombia are far better places to live in than Kenya if we go by the data. This is despite the challenges they face at the moment. Finland has remained the least fragile place to live in at position 178/178 since 2011.

Original_Search_Title: When Patients Would Rather Die Than Endure Life On ARVs
Headline: Uganda: When Patients Would Rather Die Than Endure Life On ARVs
Body_Text:
Who can forget the days when the possibility of catching HIV was so real and fearsome, and casual sex was frowned upon?
Then, hallelujah, the life-saving antiretroviral drugs (ARVs) came. Today, Ugandans have developed a laissez faire attitude towards sex and HIV. They have unprotected sex and you may have heard one say, "Eddagala lyajja" - we now have medicine - as they go about living carelessly.
Unknown to them is that though ARVs changed HIV from "a death sentence to a chronic disease," as Dr Alex Muganzi who treats HIV says, the side effects of ARVs are so bad in some people that some prefer death to them. Here, we bring you one woman's 22-year HIV journey and the side effects she endured from taking ARVs in the hope that one reader's laissez faire attitude changes.
Cotilda Mawa* believes she was among the first people in Uganda to use ARVs.
"I started in 2000 and at that time, I believe there was only one other person taking them," she says. Before she started using ARVs, Mawa had been prepared and was waiting for death.
"When you tested HIV-positive during that time, you were sent to Nsambya Homecare where you were prepared for death. They brought us lawyers from Fida and taught us how to write wills," Mawa recalls. She tested HIV-positive in 1991.
She recalls that in preparation for her death (which she thought was imminent); she constructed a house within 37 days so that her children could have a home to live in.
"My father told me: 'You build your house here near me so that when you pass away, I can look after your children'." Mawa had two children then, a four-year-old and a three-month-old baby.
While she awaited death, she prayed.
"I would tell God, God give me life until my children reach P.7." Today, she laughs at the modesty of her plans and prayers.
"I make big and long-term plans today. I look forward to seeing my children get married. I look forward to things like grandchildren and I've undertaken postgraduate studies [something she couldn't think of wasting money on when she thought death was imminent]," Mawa says.
In 2000, a Swiss doctor told Mawa he would not let her die. "We have to keep you alive," the Swiss doctor reportedly resolved and told Mawa after seeing the positive impact her activism was having.
"I know in two years, these drugs [ARVs] will be available in Africa but for now, we have to find a way of keeping you alive," Mawa recalls the doctor telling her. She says that doctor started sending her the ARVs in 2000 via FedEx and he would monitor her on fax. As earlier mentioned, Mawa believes that at this point, there were only two Ugandans in Uganda using ARVs as they were expensive.
So expensive were they that the doctor could only afford to send her his patients' leftovers.
"I could see that they [had been picked from this and that patient]," Mawa says.
More to ARVs than meets the eye:
But oh, the side effects to Aids drugs!
"The first three months were bad. I fell sick for the first time after starting to use them. I would get nausea and I was dizzy, weak and anaemic," Mawa says. At that time, she was taking twenty pills - ten in the morning and ten in the evening. "I would take some and throw up. Then I would take others." This makes today's single-pill cocktail sound like heaven, which it definitely is not, though.
So bad were the side effects that Mawa contemplated giving them up.
"But I had my children in mind. I would [hear] my daughter's voice as she told me I had to take my medicine. Someone had told her I was HIV-positive and she was so worried I was going to die".
Muganzi says that HIV treatment in Uganda was started in the early 2000s after a 1996 international Aids conference in Canada showed that HIV was treatable, but not curable, with antiviral treatment. "[When Highly Active Antiretroviral Therapy -HAART] was incorporated into clinical practice, [it] rapidly showed impressive benefit with a 60 per cent to 80 per cent decline in rates of Aids, death, and hospitalisation."
But the price to pay for use of ARVs is still in the form of the side effects. Some of the side effects Muganzi records from patients include nausea, vomiting, dizziness, abdominal pains, nightmares, sleep disorders, skin reactions, yellow eyes and anaemia, among others. He says these resolve within four to twelve weeks of treatment.
But Mawa's did not go away soon: "Crixivan made me vomit too much. I devised ways to take it but it was hard to take."
She recalls that attempts to pour it in her porridge or take it while inserted in a banana did not make it any less bitter. While Mawa considered her side effects bad, she tells a story of a young boy she met in New York, whose sleep patterns had been disrupted by Efeverance, one of the drugs used in HIV treatment.
"He had put his mattress on the floor and would wake up in the night and start running around screaming." Efeverance is responsible for nightmares. Other side effects are diarrhoea, increased or reduced appetite, loss of libido in men and numbness, tingling and tightening of feet.
Mawa insisted that her medication be changed and finally in 2002, she was put on Combivir - where she had to take eight pills: four in the morning and four in the evening. She was on this therapy until 2011 when she travelled abroad and the doctor noticing she was suffering from lipodystrophy (fat redistribution -fat is either lost or it is built up around the cheeks, stomach or the whole body), changed her regimen.
"The country I was in was advanced and they had better treatments than we do; so, the doctor changed my treatment. In Uganda, it might not be so easy to change treatments as ARVs are expensive but in advanced countries, lipodystrophy is watched for and as soon as it is noticed, change in treatment occurs," Mawa says. Today, she takes a single pill for treatment of HIV.
Ugandans letting Aids fight down:
While Mawa appreciates the quantity and quality of life ARVs have afforded her, she is critical of the lax attitude Ugandans have adopted towards HIV.
"It scares me that people are lax towards having protected sex. Don't they know that they can get a strain of HIV whose ARV resistance is high?"
She says that prevention of HIV is best because much as there is treatment, "HIV is not like malaria where the parasite is killed by medication". HIV is still incurable, she says, and just because treatment exists, people should not take the disease for granted.
Uganda, once the world's success story for its work in curbing the pandemic in the 1990s and early 2000s, is currently experiencing an increase in both incidence and prevalence. The reversal of the so-called success story has been blamed in part on complacency as those who are HIV-negative think Aids is no longer scary since it is treatable. How wrong!
Muganzi advises individuals on treatment to report side effects to health workers so that adverse ones like lipodystrophy, nerve changes and kidney failure are arrested early.
*Not real name. Though an HIV/Aids activist, Mawa requested for anonymity because she has not yet disclosed her status to her youngest, HIV-negative child.

Original_Search_Title: Ncube Defends Misihairabwi Mushonga's Inclusion
Headline: Zimbabwe: Ncube Defends Misihairabwi Mushonga's Inclusion
Body_Text:
MDC leader Professor Welshman Ncube has defended the inclusion of the party's secretary-general Mrs Priscilla Misihairabwi-Mushonga on the list of women's quota under proportional representation in Matabeleland South province.
In an interview after the MDC Gwanda central district assembly in Gwanda town on Sunday, Prof Ncube said Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga had every right to contest in Matabeleland South as she originated from the province. He said Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga's name was also brought forward by the provincial assembly.
"The leadership decides who to deploy and where. For your own information, we don't just deploy. We held a meeting with the provincial assembly and the secretary-general's name was among that list contrary to all the rumour-mongering that you got," said Prof Ncube.
When pressed for further comment on the issue versus the party's lobby for devolution of power, Prof Ncube fired back with questions expecting to get responses from this reporter.
"What makes you think that she is not from Matabeleland South? I am asking you. How do you say that she is not from this province when her entire family is in Umzingwane? You know her mother comes from Matabeleland South and that even Canaan Banana was her uncle. So how do you say she is not from Matabeleland South?" asked Prof Ncube.
Most MDC party members in Matabeleland South are bitter over the inclusion of Mrs Misihairabwi- Mushonga whom they said was not from the province insisting that only her mother was from the area.
Also present during the interview was the treasurer-general and the candidate for Gwanda North, Mr Paul Themba Nyathi, who is reported to have included Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga's name. Mr Nyathi also showed displeasure over the issue and only said "one person" whom he did not disclose, was against the inclusion of Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga.
"How does a single person who goes to Chronicle become people who are not happy?" he asked.
The Nomination Court on Friday announced Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga's name under Umzingwane as the first on the list of the women's quota under proportional representation together with Sibekithemba Njani (also under Umzingwane), Elizabeth Ndlovu, Thokozile Sibanda, Hellenic Ncube and Thokozile Moyo.
However, MDC party members who spoke to this reporter after the Saturday meeting denied having included Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga on the list.
The party members said the move was just a strategy by the party leadership to prolong Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga's "gloomy" political life after she suffered a dismal defeat in her constituency in the 2008 elections.
An official at the party's provincial offices also confirmed that Mrs Misihairabwi-Mushonga's name was not on the list and that they were shocked when the name was announced by the Nomination Court.

Original_Search_Title: No Elections Are Perfect - Journalist Pius Nyamora
Headline: Kenya: No Elections Are Perfect - Journalist Pius Nyamora
Body_Text:
Journalist Pius Nyamora was at the forefront in the clamour for democratic freedoms in Kenya before he was forced to flee to the US. He was recently in the country after 20 years in exile. Kiss TV's John Sibi-Okumu caught up with him last week. The interview was transcribed by Star writer Dickson Soire.
I describe you as a journalist because from 1977-1988 you worked for the Nation newspaper. In 1988, you launched Society, a political news magazine to fight for democracy in Kenya. In 1994, political threats forced you and your family to flee to the US, a country which gave you political asylum. We are referring to a dark chapter in our history. Just how dark?
It was extremely dark because in 1988 when I left the Nation newspaper after 11 years with them, my idea was to start a newspaper to allow Kenyans the freedom to express themselves. At that time the opposition was not allowed to speak. A situation that had gone on for quite some time. And it got worse after the attempted coup in 1982. So, my idea was to have people who did not have any other forum an opportunity to express their opinions about how things were going on in the country through Society.
My first story was an interview with Jaramogi Oginga Odinga,which was really a profile of him. Later, people started coming up with stories which we never really expected. For instance, people were being tortured at the Nyayo chambers. People like Mr Kamangara and Mr Miano who came to give us their stories. Then, we started getting exclusives like corruption in government and we published those stories as well.
By the way,we were the first Kenyan newspaper to publish the memorandum of association of the first opposition party to be launched. In those days, as it had not been registered by government , it was supposed to be termed a pressure group. But, because I knew the real situation, I went ahead and termed it an opposition party and published its whole memorandum of association. We had not been invited to the press conference but a foreign correspondent gave us a copy and I decided to publish it.
If my memory serves me right, your printing press was either shut down or burnt down? What were the risks to being a beacon of freedom of expression?
There were many dangers, actually. For instance, we were being followed by Special Branch police officers. My home at Mountain View was monitored almost seven days a week. We would find two police officers at the gate. Then, at one point, we were rounded up. I was supposed to go to Zambia to address journalists on the freedom of the press.
The government took my passport on that day. They arrested me, and they arrested my wife and three other senior officers of Society and put us in different police stations in Nairobi. Our lawyers kept going round, particularly Paul Muite, but they kept on telling them that I wasn't where in fact I was. Later, they took us to Mombasa and kept us in different police stations.
Were you treated with civility?
The problem was, when they took us to Shimo la Tewa, it was embarrassing when I was stripped naked in front of my junior staff before being taken into the cells.
How about your wife?
Yes, my wife was also stripped naked but in the female cells. It was worse than eating ugali that felt like it had been mixed with sand. We had to endure that for about 10 days until the international community, local organisations like Release Political Prisoners, and people like Smith Hempstone, the American ambassador, and the German ambassador made noise and we were released 10 days later.
What was the trigger to leave the country?
You see, my idea was to enable Kenyans to get opposition parties and in 1992 we were able to do that. Although the opposition did not win the election, I knew that now Kenyans could speak. However, a fire bomb was placed in our office on the exit side. We were lucky to have a burglar proof backdoor which the bombers were not aware of. We were able to find the key and escape.
So, the idea was to burn you alive in your office?
Yes, I was there with my wife and a member of staff. At 8pm.
We hear much about whistleblowers like Julian Assange nowadays. So many years back, how difficult was it for you to get out of the country?
Well, I did not have a passport. The US embassy helped me to get out. Hempstone demanded that I be given my passport back from the government since I was allegedly going for a two-week training programme organised by the United States Information Service. We left our house in Runda without telling anyone and we left the office the way it was because we did not want people to know we were escaping.
We, that is to mean you, your wife and your children as well?
The children had actually left already. We had sent our second kid earlier. When we were arrested, he was left alone in the house at night without knowing where we were.
Let's switch our attention to your life in the US. A lot of people imagine that living in the US is a bit like Dick Whittington in the streets of London, paved with gold. Did you feel that way when you arrived?
It is not what Kenyans imagine. Actually we went to stay in Michigan, where our kids lived and went to school. We had to share a room with them. Within a month they were told to leave the house.
So, we went to a place you could call a slum. I would prefer living in Mathare Valley but we had to because I had had no money. And even if I had, the Americans want you to have a credit history. We tried to get a better house but if it was going to cost $400 then we couldn't. Looking for a job was difficult. I went there as a proud Kenyan and I wanted to continue being a journalist but I could not even get a photocopying job. For one thing, I had an accent. And according to most Americans, when you have an accent you are not intelligent enough.
After a year, I realised that I had to accept anything. So, I got into a photo lab and I was taught how to process pictures. As a journalist I already knew how to size them. This was thanks to a young American employee who was sympathetic after he heard my story. I was earning $6 and 75 cents, which was nothing. Then, my wife started running a kiosk in a mall to sell the gifts we took with us from Kenya. Just to survive.
Every evening I would ask my wife how much she had made since the kids had to eat and we didn't have any other source of income. One of my sons had to work in the college cafeteria and the other one did painting in the same cafeteria to earn a little money for us to survive. So, it was really difficult.
Was this unique to you? We know that there are a lot of Kenyans in the US. Are they having a whale of a time?
To survive in the US, you must have led a difficult life here in Kenya. For me, I never had a comfortable upbringing. My father and mother did not know where I went to school. I had to sell bananas, eggs and sugarcane to do that. I had lived in Mathare Valley once when I started working. So, it was nothing out of the ordinary and, therefore, I knew I could survive.
I want to draw you further on this because we are reading a lot in our papers about unions in this alien society being very difficult to sustain. You are of a certain generation; how did your experience affect your relationship with your wife?
My wife has always been with me. When I was arrested, she was with me. When I started the newspaper, she was working with me and financing the newspaper.
Why do you think that young couples are finding it difficult to sustain that kind of solidarity? The young people suffer because they are not honest with each other. You have to be honest about what you have and what you want to do and you have to discuss things. If you do not have similar thinking or a similar target, you are not going to survive and you will separate very easily.
Let's fast forward. That was 1994, now it is 2013 and you are in Kenya. Would you be so bold as to comment on the Kenya you find?
Actually I came here in May last year and I was surprised but very happy because I found many newspapers and radio stations. And the media was really free. I thought that if I still had Society I couldn't do what the current media was doing. I felt very proud. And then I found Kenyans working very hard and they were ahead of the government. The government has to keep up with them.
So, you have nothing to criticise?
In fact, Kenyans must be very happy about how far we have come. For example, I find business people are more polite. You won't find a matatu tout telling you off and taxi drivers actually give you their telephone numbers, for next time.
With regard to human relationships, do you think that human interaction in Kenya is somewhat superior to that in the US?
It is far superior in Kenya. You cannot compare it to the US. In the US you do not go to a place because you will be given more attention since you are older. In Kenya, people tend to even leave their business for 10 minutes to take you somewhere. In the US even a friend whom you fed when you last met in Kenya or who stayed in your home in Kenya, will not invite you to his house for a cup of coffee.
Politically speaking do you feel , again, that everything is on the right track, if not wonderful?
Actually I'm excited and delighted to see what has been going on...
So you did not sit there and say: "Botched elections; BVR machines broke down..." As a journalist, you did not see this as the worst thing that could happen?
I was mad in 2007. But, this time, I was following it on facebook and I followed the presidential debates. I thought Kenyans ought to know that there are no elections that are perfect.
I myself feel that we have become more tribal than I have ever known it in my lifetime. Would you agree with me?
That is what I felt when I left. But, I have changed my mind. Because politically, the support you get is from the people around you. And they may happen to come from your own tribe. The only way to eliminate tribalism is for the current leaders to distribute the national wealth to everybody whether they belong to their own tribes or not. And soon Kenyans will not even care who becomes president.
What about the implementation of our new constitution? Again, nothing shocks you?
No, I was shocked about the story in Nakuru where some officials were not approved but they were sworn in anyway. I expect Kenyans to reject such people. Kenyans are so free now that I'm really not afraid of this constitution being thrown away or being messed up. Because, Kenyans are awake and we have younger people who are educated and who are going to deal with the political leaders and straighten them up.
So, what do you think we should prioritise in order to achieve vision 2030, from a journalistic viewpoint? Do you think we should give a bigger focus to culture and the arts, or do you think that they are also thriving?
I have been thinking that Kenyans should try very hard to give the Swahili language a priority because when you go out there, people speak in their own languages. If you go to America and you do not know English but you know Kiswahili, you will not be given an interpreter. So, Kenyans should be proud of Kiswahili. And, right now, the media is trying to promote it, which I like.

Original_Search_Title: 2015 - Opposition Parties On Final Journey to APC Registration
Headline: Nigeria: 2015 - Opposition Parties On Final Journey to APC Registration
Body_Text:
