Papers containing the possible editorial topics would have been selected and typed with all the issues pertaining to them duly set out, mapping out the trend which the discussions would take. The members are granted the indulgence of refreshment while deliberating and so the tea things and the condiments including banana, biscuits, bottled water etc, would also have been ready by the time the meeting kicks off. Suleiman Muhammed makes sure everything is in place to oil the board's deliberations.
Suleiman Mohammed cut his journalistic teeth with the defunct Democrat newspaper straight after earning a diploma from the Kaduna Polytechnic in Kaduna and when the Democrat went down under, as many newspapers in the north did before the advent of the Trust, he joined the Media Trust as one of its foundation staff. He also took a Bachelors degree in Public Administration from the University of Abuja. He worked variously as a reporter on several beats, serving at one time as its Aso Rock correspondent which afforded him the opportunity to travel extensively in Africa and the world. He was also an Associate Editor in charge of Lagos and the entire South West, in its news gathering and other operations. It was a delight to listen to him as he regaled you with the story of the Media Trust in its formative years when it was struggling to break into the Nigerian newspaper market, particularly the vigil everyone kept at the premises of the company on production days. I came to know Suleiman intimately in the one year I spent sharing the same office with him. In 2005 I had joined the company as Company Secretary and Legal Adviser but in 2009 moved to the editorial department as deputy editorial board chairman. The company had just completed installing a printing press at its Chida Hotel premises that contained the press and a number of offices. Shortage of space meant staff would have to share offices and so Suleiman and I shared one. It was a time that I got to know how meticulous he was as he hit away at his Toshiba computer preparing editorial topics or writing an allotted one. Every once in a while he would ask what I thought about the appropriateness of a turn of phrase, or some other bothersome linguistic expression, to which I replied as I thought fit.
As managing editor, part of his job was to ensure reporters were on their toes and this entailed copious compilation of everyone's record of performance including the number of stories filed in at specific periods, making a note of whom to call in for censure and whom to praise and recommend to the authorities for commendation and reward. If he had to upbraid a reporter, he did so in the calmest of voices that betrayed no emotion at all, as an uncle seeking the best for his ward would do. At a point the company was enforcing the idea that every staff must be computer literate and backed it up with handing every staff a computer almost free. While I struggled with the rudiment of the word processor, he was already using the intricate coral draw application to tabulate the performance of his reporters in graphs and sending them on line to higher levels of decision making. A few times I have had to seek his help to get out of a computer quagmire, to which he had obliged readily, sometimes with a wry smile on his face as if to say Malam Murtala (as he liked to call me as a mark of respect) is still in the Stone Age on computer literacy! When I was bitten by the journalistic urge to move on in 2009, I noticed he was unusually quiet that day and so I asked what the problem was. He shot back immediately with a query about my resignation and where I was headed. But before I could answer he pressed on with "a rolling stone gathers no moss but that is a saying that is only true when you are young". Not only was I taken aback about the brutal frankness of his comment but the underlying empathy and fellow feeling was not lost on me either. I thanked him for his concern for my wellbeing. The virtue of fellow feeling was well ingrained in him, as he made a point of asking after members who couldn't attend meetings because of ill-health and he paid get well visits to such persons. Last Thursday, I arrived at the meeting venue to see the few members who had arrived early wearing sombre faces. One of them intoned that Suleiman was no more. After the initial confusion, the cold brutal fact hit me like a sledge hammer, followed by images of him, only last week making a point about the necessity of editorialising on an issue. The detail of his death by slumping while trying to open his car door became a mere and unnecessary footnote to the finality of one so boisterously alive one moment and dead in the next, it is beyond comprehension. In his passing the Media Trust has lost a gem of a committed and dedicated staff. May Allah grant him rest in Aljanna Firdausi. Amen.

Original_Search_Title: Baboons Kill Hens in Kitengela
Headline: Kenya: Baboons Kill Hens in Kitengela
Body_Text:
HUNDREDS of baboons from the Nairobi National Park destroyed property after raiding homes in Kitengela town on Thursday night.
The animals sneaked into homesteads that border the Export Processing Zones and killed chicken, ate eggs, bananas and maize before owners arrived from work in the evening.
Residents said the baboons turned on the home owners with sticks and stones, forcing the residents to seek assistance from the local police station.
Francis Mutea Katiku who lost more than 2000 eggs and 10 hens said on spotting police officers, the animals moved to rooftops and refused to leave.
Katiku said police and KWS rangers insisted that the animals are "harmless" and asked residents to guard their property. When the Star visited the homes yesterday morning, the animals had left. Residents said buffaloes were spotted grazing near the homes last week. Elder Peter Saruni said when baboons are hungry, they feed on sheep tails.

Original_Search_Title: Banana War On Illicit Brews
Headline: Kenya: Banana War On Illicit Brews
Body_Text:
Kiambaa residents yesterday made a resolution dubbed 'Banana Declaration' not to allow illicit brews and drugs destroy the youth.
Two years ago we buried more than 10 people in Banana and Muchatha who died after drinking illicit brews. Today we make a declaration not to allow illicit brews destroy us, said the residents.
The residents were speaking at a public baraza in Banana town which was attended by area MP Paul Koinange, DO Gerald Ratemo and security personnel. "Those selling the illicit brews are being protected by the law because they continue to sell illicit brews after their arrest. We want our MP to address all legal bottlenecks that are hindering the protection of our people from cheap liqour," said Kikuyu Council of Elders Kiambaa constituency chairman John Mwangi.

Original_Search_Title: Re - Clarifying Nigeria's Debt Position
Headline: Nigeria: Re - Clarifying Nigeria's Debt Position
Body_Text:
I read with consternation Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala's piece of THISDAY back page titled; 'Clarifying Nigeria's Debt Position' and I was appalled by what I was reading by not just one of the most qualified minister in Nigeria's history, but someone I personally respect so much as to get really excited when I logged on to THISDAY mobile service on my blackberry phone and saw the article written by her.
As soon as I saw the title of the article, I was salivating and licking my lips in anticipation of what I expected would be thought provoking, knowledge gathering and finally the truth about how Nigeria has found itself in this debt conundrum yet again.
And Aunty Ngo (initially) did not disappoint when she started the piece by writing, 'My goal in this article is to shed some light on the public debt, to clarify the real state of Nigeria's debt position, and hopefully, provide a knowledge platform for constructive debate.' By now, my appetite to devour the article was thoroughly wet. But alas, disappointed to the point of boiling was I when Aunty started rolling out the figures and reasons for our debt situation.
As an unemployed Nigerian who has been home for a year now, I think I can speak for a majority of our frustrated youth. A good lawyer will tell you that you don't ever cross-examine an expert witness in his/her area of expertise, because he/she will floor you. So as a non-financial expert but a typical Nigerian that Aunty Ngo went to a great length to write the article in a layman's language for, I will use the figures provided in her article to point out some salient facts, and 'deliberate?' omission by her.
Aunty left more unsaid than said and those are the points I will like to tackle here using her hard data in the article.
While she mentioned our present external debt being low at $6.67 billion, I noticed she cleverly avoided mentioning the present domestic debt standing except for saying in paragraph 12 how well we have been reducing domestic borrowing from 2011 till date. And of course, Nigerians know the real reason why that domestic borrowing has been reducing since especially 2012, which I will later mention here.
Whether domestic or external, debt is debt and that is what Nigerians believe. And by her admission, some of these domestic debts are owed to foreign investors through bond purchase. How do you explain a debt that was $46.2 billion in 2004 prior to the Paris Club debt relief when we were forgiven approximately $32.4 billion (calculating the external debt pre-relief and when she left office in 2006, $35.9 billion minus $3.5 billion) and by 2011 when she returned back to the post, had grown to $47.9 billion? If we add the approximately $32.4 billion relief, then we are talking in the region of $80 billion that would have been owed today without the relief.
And again, notice how Aunty Ngo dodged mentioning the amount we had to pay before this relief was granted, which was rumoured to be in the region of $12 billion. This is part of expenditure that she should have factored in, and its simple 'Finance 101'. So Aunty left office in 2006 and domestic debt was $13.8 billion with total debt at $17.3 billion. Fast forward to five years when she came back in 2011, domestic debt had gone up to $42.23 billion, that's around 210 per cent increase, with the total debt at $47.9 billion. Notice that by 2011, we owed more than we were owing ($46.2 billion) even before the debt relief. In just five years of the Yar'Adua/Jonathan presidency, we had racked up more debt than we incurred throughout our country's years of existence. (Not factoring in inflation trend)
Aunty Ngo also said that 'while the debt grew, our national income also grew'. Is she implying that a country can go ahead and borrow as much as it can so long it has debt 'sustainability'? Because that table she showed after this argument contradicted that fact with all the G7 economies averaging 120 per cent GDP and we know how massively the western world have been borrowing from Germany and China. One of the biggest fights between President Obama and congress is the debt ceiling. She's almost justifying the huge surge in borrowing. The message I'm getting there is 'pile the debt up for the unborn generation since its manageable'. It is manageable now does not mean it will be manageable next year, especially now when even the 'Banana Republics' are discovering oil and the developed nations are breaking through in alternative energy technology. When the national income that Aunty rightly claimed is growing suddenly falls like it happened recently in the western world, our debt will not fall with it, nor will the interest be reduced. Ask Greece, Spain, Italy, etc.
Of course Aunty knows using differences in GDP percentages will sound foreign to ordinary Nigerians, hence the attempt to significantly want to show a GDP difference between the debt prior to relief, after relief and the debt by 2011. That even though by 2011 we had racked up the same amount of debt as we had pre-debt relief, the GDP is now much lower. That's brilliant ! She had already prepared her defence and cushion in case Nigerians point out the fact that the debt had gone even higher than pre-relief period. My problem with this is: Why is she not using percentage as a measure? For example, tell us the amount and percentage of domestic debt five years prior to 2004, i.e. from 2000-2004, and leave us the readers to measure and compare it with five years after you left that low debt for us, i.e. from 2006-2011, instead of using GDP as an 'abracadabra'.
Using GDP, which can be affected by inflation trend as a measure will only allow time to be wasted arguing on financial jargon. But using percentage will solve the issue pronto. Even among economists, using GDP to judge the welfare health of a country's citizens most times does not show a true picture.
And now the main reason why I almost cried when I read aunty's article further down and decided I must reply, even if THISDAY refuse to print it. I was happy when she acknowledged the astronomical and unsustainable growth in domestic debt in five years. But it was a slap in the gut when she mentioned 'the two main reasons which resulted in this outcome'. 'First, the initial growth of the domestic debt stock was because the federal government wanted to deepen the domestic debt markets and generate a yield curve for Nigeria which ultimately could help our corporate bodies to access the capital markets and borrow funds at more affordable rates. The DMO through its work has been successful in doing this'. I was disappointed reading that first one but I had to let that go, it's her turf, I have no chance there because Nigerians were never told this until now. Even if that is true (which I believe is not, but is Aunty's surgical measure to plaster up the cracking debt ceiling when she came back in 2011 and not an initial plan of the government when they started piling up the debt from 2007), it shouldn't be a main reason.
But I kept perusing, expecting the one we all know and thinking, finally, the government is going to admit what we all know. But if the first reason was meant to throw us non-economists out of the argument, the second reason were meant to follow it up with a slap. She claimed rightly that 'domestic debt was raised to finance budget expenditures including consumption'. And of all the examples to give for these consumption? 53 per cent salary increase for civil servants! Haba, Aunty Ngo, fear God, ha ha!!!
Yes, it is part of it, but that is not a 'main reason' as you claimed. I got hugely disappointed she didn't for once mention the bogus fuel subsidy claim by every Jack, Dick and Harry (including a luxury watch maker). Notice the cleverness in just cloaking it into the word 'consumption'. But of all things, it shouldn't be cloaked nor garnished. While Aunty Ngo has spent most of her professional life in the financial and diplomatic circle, with her down-to-earth demeanor, reminding everyone that she was born and bred in Ibadan and never for once bragged about her Harvard credentials like many politicians who only studied there for a few weeks, the issue of the damage the fuel subsidy scam has done to us as a country should have at the very least been mentioned in that article.
With various discrepancies at different government agencies like the CBN, DPR, Ministry of Finance and the Debt Office pertaining the exact amount paid out to fuel importers from 2007 as witnessed during the subsidy probe, I will be safe to say between 2007 and 2011 When aunty Ngo resumed her previous role, and remember, January 1, 2012 when we came out in protest, the government had paid/owed more than N3 trillion, and that's being conservative. That amounts to approximately $20 billion. Let's even assume, 'okay it's an exaggeration', let's halve it, that's still $10 billion. How does aunty justify omitting this humongous amount by brushing through it as 'consumption' while exposing and blackmailing the civil servants taking home $110 compared to the $50 per month that was being paid him/her before as the 'main reason' for our domestic debt increase? It's not as if salaries had not been increased before by previous administration. This didn't over-bloat the domestic debt. At a time when President Obama is slugging it with congress to, on a yearly basis lasting four years, increase the American minimum wage to around $12 hourly, our government is basically telling its workers that it 'will endeavor not to repeat' that increase.
And the subsidy issue brings me back to why the domestic borrowing has been reducing since 2011. It was plainly and simply because of the fuel subsidy protest in January 2012 and the attendant probe which revealed the stinking rot in the system. Even Malam Sanusi Lamido Sanusi admitted that 'any fool could make money from that fuel subsidy system'. We learnt how domestic fuel consumption increased by 200 per cent between 2007 and 2011 despite nothing on ground showing Nigerians have bought more cars and driving it at the same time, we learnt how importers where bringing in empty fuel vessels and claiming several billions of dollars with the connivance of government officials, how import licenses are being given out as political gift and recipient just selling that piece of paper for several millions for onward claim of 'ghost' oil import. Can I go on?
So to portray the salary increase as a major reason for the unprecedented domestic debt increase is, for lack of a better word, cynical.
Thank you Aunty Ngo for 'going to some length to throw light on the real facts and issues'. Please next time, know that majority of us might be unemployed and never worked in the financial sector, but we do know our ABC, 123 and our plus and minus (to an extent), and most importantly, we do love our country. You are doing a wonderful job cleaning up after their mess, and God will guide you to complete the good work. But please, don't cover up for them.
- Adewale writes from Lagos

Original_Search_Title: Ombatse Killings - In Search for Justice
Headline: Nigeria: Ombatse Killings - In Search for Justice
Body_Text:
Since my brother, Christian, was gruesomely killed alongside over 90 other policemen a month ago by Ombatse cultist/ militiamen in Nasarawa, the need for justice - or rather the absence of it - has tormented me like a sore toe in an ill-fitting shoe. Though I've tried to airbrush the torture with a façade of occasional light-heartedness, I've only succeeded in switching from one level of depression to another like a danfo driver switching lanes in Lagos rush hour traffic.
My pillows should be renamed Insomnia. Even when I manage to sleep, it's often in snatches - I'm violently roused to reality by frightful nightmares sweating like I took a dive in the devil's pool. Leaving the house is terribly becoming unexciting these days. Almost everything that thrilled me suddenly tastes bland. I'm badly beaten and bruised all over by dejection.
One might argue that these are signs of intense grieving. True. I haven't known grief this deep before. The pain I felt at the passing of my old man was nothing compared to this. However, I'm traumatised more at my helplessness to bring the perpetrators of this heinous crime to justice; it disturbs me greatly that 10 years from now I might have to tell my two nephews (3 and 1.5 years old) that the savages that killed their dad were never arrested or prosecuted. Worse, the government and police authorities have made only a feeble, or honestly, no attempt whatsoever to make them pay. They haven't even bothered to tell the truth of how these men were led to their death. It breaks my heart that the founder of this cult group still talks freely to the media and brags about how "his gods sent fire from above to kill the policemen."
I'm not saying this because I crave for people's pity or sympathy. No. First, I want to express how brazen we have become at the brutal waste of human lives and the impunity that vultures around it. Further, I hope by sharing the trauma my family endured these past month, we can begin to, at least, imagine the suffering of thousands of families that have lost loved ones in the convulsing madness that has darkened the soul of the country. I also intend that this piece will serve as a wakeup call for those of us in our nice little bubbles outside the north that evil is roaming just down the street. But I doubt if that would be necessary. If anyone don't still realise how dare our situation is at the moment, he/she probably just woke up from a thousand years hibernation.
On a personal level, this article is therapeutic. I hope it would completely purge the emotional clogs I'm still burdened with and free my grief-ravaged mind for the flow of positive forces.
I'm not naïve. Bad things regularly happen to good people. Then again if you work as a security agent the probability of having a bad guy put a bullet in your heart is very high. But Christian didn't deserve to die the way he did. In fact no one deserves to die such way. The details I shall be sharing in the following paragraphs are gory and heartrending. I want to apologise to the families and others affected by this tragedy. I'm not trying to make anyone relive the trauma. Remember, I know where the shoe pinches. But my training as a reporter has taught me the truth has to be told, for the greater good, no matter how unpleasant it may be.
Many of the murdered policemen would still be alive today if the police authorities had done basic checks before hurriedly deploying over a hundred officers to their death. They were not properly briefed; the police had no intelligence or bothered to get any. There was basically no planning whatsoever. To highlight the haphazard manner the team for the operation was put together, many of the officers were told just few hours before the operation. For instance, my brother had already finished his shift for the day and was only summoned back by his bosses. Yet nobody within the police has been made to account for disaster. Small wonder their convoy was easily ambushed and the men butchered in the most coldblooded manner. Many of the bodies recovered were sprayed with bullets; butchered with cutlasses and deliberately burnt beyond recognition. The Assistant Commissioner that led the team was discovered in an abandoned well, bloated, with one of his legs missing.
The pictures of some of the officers suggest they were captured before they were hacked to death. Their legs were bound; hands tied behind their backs. We got the body of my brother, though terribly mangled by bullet wounds and deep machetes cuts, back for proper burial. I hope he died quickly.
In fact my family was fortunate. Over 90% of the bodies recovered were mutilated beyond recognition and were buried en masse. As of today only 12 families got back the bodies of their loved one for proper burial.
The bodies that were recovered by the Red Cross (the police were too scared to retrieve their dead) were treated in the most demeaning manner possible. Charred remains of policemen were stacked together in a heap in plain sight. Decomposing remains of policemen were strewn on the floor of over- capacitated morgues. Families who could recognise their love ones were asked to pay for their embalmment. The police authorities provided no support and didn't bother to treat the corpses with dignity. I know a policeman who resigned after seeing pictures of the slain officers.
I wish no family the trauma we endured the fortnight following this tragedy. Perhaps in the most bloodcurdling heartlessness I've ever heard of, one of my brother's killers called my sister-in-law with my brother's phone and said: "We are those that attacked the police. We have killed all of them."
Then there was the argument over the identity of my brother's body as another family claimed it was the body of their son. This report, detailing what happened cannot even begin to scratch the surface of nightmare. I can't describe myself as a religious person and I think miracles are overrated. But, if the fact that my mom didn't suffer a heart attack or stroke isn't a miracle then nothing is. Just as I typed the last sentence, I peeped at her grief-weary face and can't help but wonder how she could muster the strength to survive those nightmarish 14 days or so.
The shock of my brother's death is beginning to wane. And as I gradually accept the reality of his death I'm beginning to handle it properly. My sun is beginning to rise again. The decapitated banana stem is sprouting fresh shoot. Thanks to the kind and soothing words from friends and colleagues who called or left messages on Facebook and Twitter. Your words have proved to be the most potent balm in curing my grieving mind. I'm forever grateful for your support. Who knew I have some of the most amazing friends ever! I thought my somewhat cocky and nonconformist persona has earned me a busload of haters. I'm humbled and overwhelmed by your show of love. Thank you.

Original_Search_Title: Notes of Shame
Headline: Namibia: Notes of Shame
Body_Text:
It Pains Me to Say I Told You So.
Amid all the stories, I got headaches over this week, the one that stuck with me and surprised me the most was the deterioration of the Nujoma notes. Ja, not even Christina Swart Opperman's N$6 000 regret letter and Jerry Muadinohamba's quarter million dollar phantom head-hunt or the alleged Gobabis apartheid style attack disturbed me as much as the crumbling notes.
And since we all know that nothing happens in Namibia without the permission and knowledge of The Sam, I should be forgiven for thinking "the strongman of Namibian politics is at it again". Sam Nujoma is the only man alive today who can break a diamond ... with his face. Pas tog mal auf.
I always thought diamonds are forever. I was wrong.
The new N$10 and N$20 notes, which were issued on 15 May last year with a portrait of the 'President of the Nation', will be soon be replaced with new notes bearing enhanced security features, The Namibian reported this week.
Bank of Namibia (BoN) noticed that the notes are ageing faster than usual and some features were cracking, BoN Governor Ipumbu Shiimi said this week.
The date for issuing the new notes to the public will be announced at a later stage, the governor said. He said the bank will, in the meantime, issue a limited quantity of the improved banknotes to the public for testing.
In July last year, just six weeks after the introduction of the new N$10 and N$20 notes sporting Nujoma's image, the reflective diamond security feature in the middle of some of the notes were already 'cracking'.
If ever you felt like there was a reason to believe in karma, juju or any of those frilly fringe beliefs the time is now. Remember, BoN decided to unveil these notes that replaced Hendrik Witbooi in his own backyard during last year's Independence celebrations. That's what you get from messing with Hendrikkie in the South.
Etse, Ipumbu, if it's not broke, don't fix it. I say toss the Sammetjies überhaupt and bring back the lower denomination Hendrikkies.
OK, I know the Hendrikkies initially felt strong and valuable but they had the penchant of making so much noise in your pocket that you just wanted to get rid of them.
The Sammetjies, on the other hand, felt rigid and uncompromising at first but they washed out faster than you could say, "I'll deal with you!" They are also considerably much less valuable than they were in 2012.
Legacy? What legacy?
Some people's faces just have that ability to turn anything into akkies as soon as they come close to it.
A sure sign of a banana republic is that your money is not worth much more than a Rizla. We know that our money doesn't really have value on its own but it at least used to look good. They're so useless you can't even make a good zol with the Sammetjies unless you want to die of ink inhalation and bullshit.
What must people think of us now? That our diamonds are as sturdy as Knowledge Katti's ego after only few journalists jumped on the 'we've got oil' bandwagon when they had the greatest oil non-event at Statehouse the other day?
Maybe I mustn't be so hard on my compatriots. Maybe I should also just sit back and accept that Namibia is now finally in the toilet and we are all satisfied with the worst quality goods, services and excuses. Maybe I should accept that it is a bigger desire for some to fall flat on their faces and worship The Deity than it is for them to just do their jobs well and serve the people. Shiimi should have resigned when they launched these excuses for poor quality secondhand toilet paper last year. Did anyone ask him at this week's press conference if he was satisfied with his performance?

Original_Search_Title: Kiambaa MP Wants Commissions Disbanded
Headline: Kenya: Kiambaa MP Wants Commissions Disbanded
Body_Text:
KIAMBAA MP Paul Koinange yesterday said the number of commissions in the country should be reduced as most of them are unnecessary and expensive.
"The man in the finance docket can tell you that the commissions are unnecessary financial burden to Kenyans and they should be cut down for the better of the country ," Koinange said.
Koinange who was speaking in his constituency said the government needs to put its house in order and have a cost effective systems.
"If you look into the commissions most of them have no work now yet the commissioners are earning more than Sh 1 million each and are also using state vehicles and facilities which can be done away as it is not helping the mwanainchi but only fleecing from their pockets,"said Koinange.
However the legislator said Parliament is the superior house from the senate in terms of quality and the senate should know that the national house has the final word.
"I have no problem with the senate but they have a bigger ego and should come down and the two houses should be at par," Koinange said.
Koinange said that the parliament has more work than the senate and the senate house was made up in a hurry and was drawn from competition of Banana and orange.
"The senate was drawn from competition of Banana and Orange which was done in a hurry and we need to redesign how the government should run as we are doing double job and the parliament is the one which has the final say and we should go back to the drawing board and come up with a better system," Koinange said.
Koinange added that the salary issue has been a distraction to the development of the country and it was time to tackle issues that affect the people.
"I rarely respond to falsehoods but I want to clarify something that has been misreported on social media; I have never supported calls to raise MPs pay and my position is that I am fine with whatever is decided. This Parliament has only four years and the issue is a distraction from real agendas. We need to tackle issues that affect our people," Koinange said.

Original_Search_Title: Denzel Back in the Country
Headline: Uganda: Denzel Back in the Country
Body_Text:
Entebbe — Clad in his usual green shorts, a striped shirt, a bow tie and black jacket, Uganda's Big Brother Africa Season 8 representative Denzel, who was evicted from the reality show last Sunday, emerged through the doors of Entebbe airport at about 7:20 pm local time.
That was all the crew that had been anxiously awaiting his return needed, and the ever-smiling Denzel rushed to them with a confidence that only he could exude.
"Oh hi ma, so good to see you," he said to his mother who was waiting for him with a bouquet of flowers, which he gladly accepted and not once erasing the smile off his face.
To his friends from XFM who also had a bouquet of flowers for him, he smiled even more and playfully said, "Hey guys!"
The Denzel that we watched not so long ago on the Big Brother show was the very same one that walked through the doors, talking, talking and talking some more.
Denzel got evicted from the game show prematurely, but his mother was as delighted as everyone else to see him back safe and sound. PHOTO/Abu Mwesigwa
Next was the press conference organized by Multichoice Uganda where waitresses looked on and whispered, "Is that Denzel? Nga [how come] he doesn't change!"
Upon entering the arranged press area, Multichoice Uganda's Public Relations Officer Tina Wamala was all full of praise for the charming Denzel.
"We at Multi-choice Uganda believe that Denzel is a winner. We also believe that he was nominated and eventually evicted because he was a threat," she said.
And before she sat down, she told Denzel that a lot of doors in his career had opened up for him.
When asked to speak of his time in the Big Brother house, located in South Africa, Denzel said that he was glad he had the chance to be a part of such an amazing show and would have done more if his stay wasn't cut short.
"I know many of you expected a lot from me and are somewhat disappointed, but that is the thing about reality TV. It is like swimming, everyone will tell you what to expect but you can't really know until you get in there."
When asked about his cloth-less stunts in the house, he said that it was just him being himself.
Also, he said that upon reaching South Africa, everything seemed different, even the bananas tasted different, so stripping off his clothes was how he dealt with his new environment.
Meanwhile, Uganda's other representative in the same house, Isaac Lugudde a.k.a. LK4, is up for possible eviction this week.
The contestants in the show are battling to stay longest in the house for a prized jackpot of $300,000 after a maximum 90 days.

Original_Search_Title: Arts Industry Poorer Without Godwin Mawuru
Headline: Zimbabwe: Arts Industry Poorer Without Godwin Mawuru
Body_Text:
As I was in the rehearsal room, I got an SMS from Charity Dlodlo (Mai Madziva on Studio 263) informing me that my dear friend of outstanding character, a visionary, a legend, Godwin Mawuru had passed on.
I froze as fleeting images of our last meal of sadza and enlightening conversation at Pamahuswa of three and a half weeks before came immediately into focus.
Without hesitancy, I cancelled the rehearsal, sent SMSes to fellow artists and friends whom I knew would be equally shocked by the passing on of this giant, marshalled colleagues and fellow actors to Mawuru's house.
That was the morning of May 24 at 11am.
A 27-year relationship built on trust, respect and industry-passioned discussions had come to a screeching halt, just like that.
I was still relatively young and he was a bit older when in 1986, I started Rooftop Promotions. at that time it was going by the name Rooftop Promotions and Players.
I had reacted badly to some food allergy which saw me confined to my bed in my parents' house in Marlborough.
Mawuru showed up with a pack of apples and bananas and took his time to keep me strong.
At that time Mawuru was the Chairman of the Peoples Theatre Company, as well as a credit controller for Sales House.
We were to share a house, 80 Scott Road in Hatfield with him as an elder brother full of advice with the late Rudy Katsande for slightly over a year.
This caring aspect of his was to persist through our many years of friendship, even after he had gone to film school in Canada.
Over the years we learned to celebrate each other's achievements and give each other guidance and support in tricky and sticky situations.
His strength in time was to be confined to filmmaking and he did not disappoint at all.
Mawuru was to burst onto the film scene as a camera trainee in Chris Menges' two-time Oscar Award Winner's, his directorial debut A World Apart which went on to clinch a Golden Globe Award for Jodi May and South African, Zimbabwe based architect Linda Mvusi.
The significance of this film for Mawuru lies in that he was given the privilege of shooting the last five minutes of that film.
Not one who was content with one aspect of filmmaking, he was to enter into directing with a training video for Thomas Cook International produced by John and Louise Riber of Media for Development Trust (MFD).
Mawuru's relationship with MFD was to grow stronger in 1989 as they worked to bring to life that story which as per production notes is stated thus... "The issue of women's inheritance rights is one close to Godwin Mawuru's heart.
When he was a child, his family had gone through a very bad time after the death of his father.
Almost everything was taken away from his mother, and his family left almost destitute. They were one of the many families caught between traditional and modern ways of life. In early 1989, Godwin approached John Riber of Media for Development Trust (MFD) to help him realise his dream of making a film on this issue".
That film, with Oliver Mtukudzi's haunting sound track Neria, was born.
Mawuru's creativity and artistry was put on the platter to be consumed by so many people the world over.
His sharp directing skill was to be seen in the captivating performances of Jesesi Mungoshi, Dominic Kanaventi, Emmanuel Mbirimi, Mtukudzi, etc.
Neria won him accolades too numerous to mention.
I am the Future, a film that was totally financed with Zimbabwean corporate money produced by Kubi and John Indi was to follow.
Together we entered into filming a rare programme featuring Zimbabwean musicians, Entertainers Against Aids, a programme which was showcased in all the 10 provinces of Zimbabwe which subsequently gave birth to the Ray of Hope concert.
During the early stages of Studio 263, Mawuru would push himself to the extent of spending nights on a drip in private clinics so that he could ensure that the filming continued.
This dedication is unparalleled.
In between the soap Studio 263, he would immerse himself with other short films from other production houses such as Positive, Face to Face, etc.
When Mtukudzi started his Nzou Nemhuru Mudanga concept with his late son Sam Mtukudzi, it was Mawuru who was behind the shoot.
Without Mawuru, the industry is all the poorer.
He touched many people's hearts.
For the past two to three years, Mawuru's attention was on doing two major films, Nehanda and a film on Pfepferere.
Both films' thrust was on the liberation struggle.
In Mawuru, I personally have lost a friend, a brother and an advisor.
Fambai zvakanaka Samaita! Rest In Peace sahwira wangu.
Immense talent developed on studio 263
Seized with a desire to add value to our TV programming, Mawuru was to push the boundaries of our industry when he directed and produced the evolutionary and ground breaking TV shows; simultaneously This is Life a talk show co-hosted by Derek Bailey and Eyahra Mathazia and the longest running soap Studio 263, which gave birth to most of Zimbabwe's stars in the likes of Anne Nhira, Ben Mahaka, Chipo "Eve" Bizure, Tatenda Mavetera, Tapiwa Mavindidze, Denzel Burutsa, Yvonne Mangunda, Stephen Chigorimbo, Tinopona Katsande, Michael Kudakwashe, Edgar Langeveldt, Arnold Chirisa (Mr Bones 2), Charity Dlodlo, Eunice Tava, Wakanaka the late Nevernay Chinyanga aka Muvengwa, the list is endless.
Many writers like Tawanda Gunda, Samuel Ravengai, Blessing Hungwe did have a dalliance with Studio 263.

Original_Search_Title: Wizkid Third On Top Eight Non-American Summer Songs of 2013
Headline: Nigeria: Wizkid Third On Top Eight Non-American Summer Songs of 2013
Body_Text:
Hit track, 'Azonto' has pushed Nigeria's Ayo Balogun aka Wizkid to the third place on the top eight non-American songs of 2013. The EME boss and singer, according to American entertainment online news site FeedBuzz.com, is the only African artiste who made the list.
Top on the list is United Kingdom's Wretch featuring Shakka in the song 'Blackout' followed by 'Biglang Liko' by Philippines Ron Henley feat. Pow Chavez.
In fourth place is South Korea's Shinhwa for the song 'This Love' with 'Invader Invader' by Japan's Kyary Pamyu Pamyu in fifth position.
'This Is What It Feels Like' by Netherland's Armin van Buuren feat. Trevor Guthrie is sixth while 'Big Banana' by Australia's Havana Brown feat. R3hab and Prophet is seventh.
In eighth position is 'Get Lucky' by France's Daft Punk feat. Pharrell Williams.

Original_Search_Title: What Fewer Newspapers Meant for Everyday Ugandans
Headline: Uganda: What Fewer Newspapers Meant for Everyday Ugandans
Body_Text:
When the Ugandan government closed two independent newspapers and radio stations for ten days this month, their reporters and editors were visibly affected. Before long, international media ran images of Ugandan journalists in protest. But how did the clampdown affect other Ugandans who may not produce the news but still rely on it to earn a living?
Newspaper vendor Kumakech Jimmy expressed frustration, that of his own and his colleagues.
"Because I am in the field, I can sense what is going on in the streets now," he said. "People feel frustrated. For us, we rely financially on the newspaper business, so, to be honest, we feel like crying right now. There have been rumours of a street vendor protest, but people feel it is too risky because we have too much to lose. Because the Red Pepper and Daily Monitor are out of circulation, I miss about 18,700 Ugandan shillings [5.50 euros] every day."
Jude has been an official newspaper vendor in Kampala for 21 years. That's more than half his life, considering he's 38. After the media crackdown, his sales dropped by over 60 percent.
"I get commission over every newspaper I sell. Now, I can't sell the Daily Monitor and Red Pepper anymore, and many Ugandans refuse to buy the government owned New Vision newspaper," he said. "I have four kids. If this continues, I can't pay their school fees. I pray this situation will soon improve, not only for me but also for my country".
The closure of the Daily Monitor and the Red Pepper compelled some Ugandans to get their news elsewhere.
"At the moment we are reading Bukedde, a newspaper that is still in circulation", said Sadam, a butcher in Kamwokya, a low-income area in Kampala. "This Bukedde newspaper is in Luganda, our local language, so for me it is easier to understand. But regardless of that, it is a shame that Ugandans can't engage with objective news anymore. It is like they are trying to close our eyes."
Twenty-two year-old Justin Agaba's job would normally give him access to multiple newspapers at once.
"Because I work in a coffee shop, I am usually the first one to read all the newspapers, so I like to compare the news," he noted. "The Monitor and Red Pepper are more objective than the government-owned New Vision, I feel like the latter tries to think for you. You only know what they want you to know, you know? Now I use social media to stay informed. Twitter is really good to stay updated and get objective and informed news."
Earlier this week, Paul Ateenyi was having lunch at Kololo One, where he usually discussed the news with his fellow taxi drivers.
As he put it: "Because I am a taxi driver, I wait a substantial amount of the day. Basically, that means I am pretty bored nowadays, because I can't spend my time reading and comparing the news in the different newspapers I usually read."
Although her income was not directly affected by the closure of different media outlets, Sarah said could feel a difference. While selling her roasted bananas on the road, customers would come talk to her about daily news events and the latest gossip.
"People like to stop here for a while, relax, and eat their food," she explained. "In the meantime we chit and chat about the world around us. Now that we are without information, that is not possible anymore. I feel deprived."
One person who benefited from the media siege is Deo Kasiita. He owns an internet and training centre in Kamwokya, a lower-income area in Kampala.
"Ironically, my business thrives more when newspapers are being shut down," he said. "Ugandans now try to get their information online. Despite that, I feel that we need a free press. We all have the right to be informed by objective and independent news."

Original_Search_Title: Chipawo Goes to Uganda
Headline: Uganda: Chipawo Goes to Uganda
Body_Text:
IT was all about talent.
An 18-member team from Chipawo Zimbabwe travelled to Kampala, Uganda, for a two-week-long exchange programme.
The event, held under the theme "My Strength, My Culture and My Identity", showcased different cultural backgrounds through music and dance.
The exchange programme was organised by the Montessori Children's Academy in reciprocation of a visit by the Ugandans to Zimbabwe last year.
According to the organisers, the main objective was to strengthen the children's relationship based on culture and sharing appreciation of each other's countries among others.
It was indeed a fruitful trip for the Zimbabwean children as they learnt a lot about Uganda, its culture, food and lifestyle. The team visited places like the King's Palace and the King's Lake.
Some of the tourists attractions visited included the Ndere Cultural Centre, named in honour of the flute, known as 'ndere'. The centre promotes traditional Ugandan culture and knowledge sharing.
Speaking of the entertainment, the major highlights was the Chipawo versus Chi-Uganda were the children put up scintillating performances at Titate Primary, Uganda's oldest school, and at Silver Spoon Primary School.
The team later stole the show at a local hotel in front of hundreds of people.
Some of the major challenges faced by the team while in Uganda were language and the food on offer.
We also notice that cultural education was not properly practised in Uganda and the exchange programme was a wake-up call to many who witnessed the children's performance.
Apart from performances the children from Chipawo had a chance to learn different dances from Uganda like Baksima, Ding Ding, songs and the language spoken in Uganda.
They also had a taste of Uganda's traditional food of eating peanut butter, matoke (made from steamed banana), chapati, millet porridge and lots of fruits in Uganda.
Patience Manyangadze from Chipawo said the tour will go a long way in helping her understanding of other African cultures.
Patience was happy to board the plane for the first and shared her personal experience on the tour.
"It was my first time and I was very happy being a participant at the festival. We enjoyed our time but the food wasn't my favourite because to me it didn't taste good."
Another participant, Rudo Dube, said Uganda is lovely, and making friends was the best thing she did.
"We shared our cultural background and telling each other stories about our families.
"I am happy that Zimbabwe is rich with culture," she said.
Chipawo has toured Namibia, Botswana, South Africa, Poland and India under such cultural programmes.
However, the tour was made possible by Culture Fund of Zimbabwe, Mayor of Harare Muchadeyi Masumda, eye specialist Dr Solomon Guramatunhu, parents and other individuals who are assisted in raising the air tickets for the children.

Original_Search_Title: Four Arrested With Bhang
Headline: Kenya: Four Arrested With Bhang
Body_Text:
POLICE in Migori have arrested four suspected drug traffickers and netted 63kg of bhang with a street value of Sh63,000. The four were transporting the drug from Tanzania on the Sirare-Migori highway to unknown destination when traffic officers stopped them at a road block.
"We found the drugs stuffed in polythene bags and covered with powder soaps and ripe bananas," said area police boss Alfred Makoma. Makoma said the officers noticed something was amiss when they were inspecting the vehicle. The suspects are being held at the Migori police station.

Original_Search_Title: Marijuana Seller Re-Arrested Over Same Crime
Headline: Uganda: Marijuana Seller Re-Arrested Over Same Crime
Body_Text:
Lwengo Grade One Magistrates' Court had earlier convicted John Kaweesi of trading in marijuana, accordingly sentencing him to two months of community service - cleaning Kinoni trading centre.
However, Kawesi seems to be notorious as he has ben re-arrested over the same illicit trade.
David Byarugaba, the officer in charge of Kinoni Police Post, said the arrest followed a tip-off by residents, who suspected Kawesi of selling marijuana in a basin on a market day.
"This is the only business I can run to get some money to sustain myself," Kawesi reportedly said as he pleaded for forgiveness from the Police personnel, who arrested him.
John Kaweesi admits that he depends on the trade for survival but has not gained much from it. PHOTO/Ali Mambule
He reportedly said he had been in the trade for the last two years, although he had not reaped much from it.
"I normally buy it from Lukaya [town] from a man who grows it deep in a forest," Kawesi is quoted to have told the Police.
He, however, is said to have told the Police that he does not know his supplier's name.
Kawesi reportedly said he sold the drug to casual labourers working in the market, particularly those who load and offload matooke [banana] trucks, adding that he had routine student customers from schools around Kinoni trading centre.
Byarugaba condemned Kawesi's act, saying although it was wrong to sell marijuana to any other Ugandan, it was unacceptable to sell it to students.
He observed that although Kawesi was serving a sentence for trading in marijuana, he did not seem to have reformed.
"He would serve the punishment in the morning, but in the afternoon he would go back to continue with the business for which he was being punished," Byarugaba observed.

Original_Search_Title: Babe of the Week - Chatty Kobusingye Is At Home At Work
Headline: Uganda: Babe of the Week - Chatty Kobusingye Is At Home At Work
Body_Text:
Primrose Kobusingye loves to talk. With a job whose prerequisite is talking and being friendly to everyone, she believes she was born and not made this way.
And throughout this interview, she can't hold back the laughter that comes out intermittently.
"My life has been geared to do this. I remember when I was in O-level, I was head of debate club and this was only for the clever people who could talk a lot," she reminisces, boldly.
Kobusingye, a corporate communications and marketing officer at Orient Bank Limited, surprisingly wanted to be a lawyer as a child because of her intelligence.
She says every time she presented her views, she was correct. Simply put, she had the gut feeling to know what was right and wrong. "Even today, you cannot lie to me. I have the gut feeling that ohhh, this is wrong!"
Kobusingye started working at 18 during her A-level vacation, with her cousin, the managing director at Nina Interiors. She later joined Makerere University in 2006 to pursue a bachelor's degree in Mass Communication.
Now 26, she has acquired experience in journalism and public relations. See, she did her internship at Record TV as a news reporter in her second year. After internship, she returned to Nina Interiors but was met with a gentle rebuff from her cousin.
"She told me to get out of the shop because she believed I was worth more. Luckily, I have never looked back and when I feel I want to go back, I remember her words, 'Go!'" Kobusingye recalls.
She then joined Pulse, an advertising agency, and worked in the public relations department for three months. In 2009, she joined the public relations and communications department of the Presidential Initiative on Banana Industrial Development before joining Scanad, a top advertising agency, for one year.
At Scanad, she handled Warid Telecom as her biggest account. On handling crises that usually mar telecom companies, Kobusingye says there was a lot of testing and pre-testing of the products before they were released on the market.
She generally enjoys her job but for one thing: many think her a liar. "Even when I am telling the truth, people will always say, 'Those PR people are liars.' But usually the case is, people want to be told what they want to hear and not what is on ground."
"PR and communication are not for anyone who feels they can talk and talk and talk. It needs one to seek consent before talking, be a fast thinker and good writer," notes Kobusingye.
Kobusingye is currently pursuing a master's degree in Public Relations and Advertising.
What is the craziest thing you have ever done?
Recently, I left the bar at 10am. I was having a good time with my friends and we did not realise it was morning. It was off the hook.
Describe beauty in less than 10 words.
[Takes time to think] It is the ability to display what is on the inside. It is what someone always has on the inside.
What is your work ethic?
Honesty and integrity. If anything is going to compromise what I am, then it is not ethical.
What unique item do you have in your wallet and why do you carry it around?
Nothing; I do not keep valuable items here because they are always stolen. For the money, it is safe in a small hole within the bag.
If you could try out a job for a day, what would it be?
Traffic policeman [woman]. Those guys have been on my case, they are always giving me letters. So, I would want to be on the other side and see how it feels to issue [letters to] traffic offenders.
Have you ever taken a personality test? If yes, how did the results turn out?
I remember I did not like the results that said I was a sanguine (cheerfully optimistic). I wanted those complex personalities like a choleric. I love to think of myself as a complex character and want others to do so. Would you rather be hated or forgotten?
That's a hard one. Not 10 out of 10 can hate you. I was told when you hate someone, you get cancer. So, yeahh... anyone who would hate me would eventually die of cancer; so, I think I would rather be hated.
What's your favourite website?
Google; because it is your friend and the reason why many graduate from campus.

Original_Search_Title: Wizkid Makes Top Eight Non-American Summer Songs of 2013
Headline: Nigeria: Wizkid Makes Top Eight Non-American Summer Songs of 2013
Body_Text:
Star boy entertainment head and EME singer, Ayo 'Wizkid' Balogun, made it to the top eight non-American summer song of 2013 courtesy of his hit track, 'Azonto'.
According to American entertainment online newspaper, FeedBuzz.com, Wizzy, whose 'Azonto' came 3rd on the chart, is the only African artiste who made the list.
See other songs that made the list.
1. "Blackout" by Wretch 32 feat. Shakka (United Kingdom)
A stripped-down beach house track smothered with beautiful reggae-influenced rapping. It's glorious. Watch video here:
http://youtu.be/IG5bWsKIsQs
2. "Biglang Liko" by Ron Henley feat. Pow Chavez (Philippines)
Released in January, the track (which translates to "Sudden Turn") has fresh synths ideal for summer road trips. The video, however, has a really sick ending.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/B6Ipftnqz4E
3. "Azonto" by WizKid (Nigeria)
The title derives from a popular dance movement in West Africa, originating in Ghana. The dance involves traditional African moves like knee bending and hip swaying, combined with wild, complex hand movements that supposedly mimic everyday activities.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/grPJ8DemVrE
4. "This Love" by Shinhwa (South Korea)
The song talks about the fragility of true love, which goes along with the slow piano at the beginning and ending. It also features a lot of voguing in its choreography, a rarity in boy band territory.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/45wH_HHnJBg
5. "Invader Invader" by Kyary Pamyu Pamyu (Japan)
The eternal 12-year-old who brought you the masterpiece called "PonPonPon" releases her latest single, which is currently at #3 in Billboard's Japan Hot 100.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/jcIOg_m-bp4
6. "This Is What It Feels Like" by Armin van Buuren feat. Trevor Guthrie (Netherlands)
Bringing the beauty of trance music to the beach houses this season. The special appearance of Ron Jeremy in the video should not be missed.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/BR_DFMUzX4E
7. "Big Banana" by Havana Brown feat. R3hab and Prophet (Australia)
Not-so-subtle lyrics aside, the synth hooks from this track is beach-party-worthy.
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/KFzT7ms5kwU
8. "Get Lucky" by Daft Punk feat. Pharrell Williams (France)
Sure, the track features an American artist, but not including this French EDM duo in this list would be like not putting on sunscreen before heading to the beach
Watch video here: http://youtu.be/5NV6Rdv1a3I

Original_Search_Title: NGF Poll and the Dangers Ahead
Headline: Nigeria: NGF Poll and the Dangers Ahead
Body_Text:
Very early in 1993, the State House Correspondents (of which I was one, reporting for African Concord) held an election at the Villa. Because our then 'oga at the top', Double Chief Duro Onabule, presidential spokesman to General Ibrahim Babangida, was suspected of having interest in some candidates, the stakes were raised in what ordinarily might have been a drab contest among reporters.
The campaign preceding the election was therefore interesting as reporters pledged and shifted loyalties. But one of the contenders, then correspondent for Champion newspaper, Mr. Amaechi Dike (now of blessed memory) was not only sure of victory but would also not want to take chances. He therefore sent money to the Champion State House photographer who was then in Port Harcourt, so he could return to Abuja and vote for him.
On D-Day we held the election but to our shock, the late Amaechi scored only one vote which meant he secured no other ballot outside the one he cast for himself. It was very embarrassing, especially for those who had pledged to him their support, apparently with the cold calculation that he would garner some votes and they would claim to be the voters. The scene that followed between the late Amaechi and the "treacherous" photographer is better imagined by readers. From that day, I learnt the lesson that pledges count for nothing when it comes to secret ballot as people often hide behind the fact that they are not likely to be detected to vote their true convictions.
That explains why I was not surprised by the outcome of the Nigerian Governors Forum (NGF) election which held last weekend and was won by Governor Rotimi Chibuike Amaechi of River State. While 19 Governors had signed a piece of paper "endorsing" Governor Jonah Jang of Plateau State, only 16 eventually voted for him, leaving the old man thoroughly embarrassed and confused. I watched Jang on television, asking rather incredulously, why he could lose when 19 Governors had "endorsed" him and I felt really sorry not only about his naivety but also for the manner in which Governor Godswill Akpabio of Akwa Ibom (who authored the paper in which April was changed to May in his handwriting), has carried on after he had been politically out-snookered!
It is instructive that while the NGF has been around for some time now, its affairs did not command as much interest until some presidential proxies waded in and unwittingly turned the election of the chairmanship of what is no more than a social club into a referendum on the popularity of President Goodluck Jonathan. At the end, the conduct of the governors purportedly acting on behalf of the presidency smacks of political desperation and an opportunistic attempt to control their colleagues through intimidation and blackmail.
Against the background that it was only yesterday that we came half way through the four-year mandate for the current office holders, events of recent days are indicative of the fact that at practically all levels, the permutations about 2015 have overtaken the primary responsibility of governance at a time when our country is beset with multiple calamities. Yet there are also lessons from the outcome of the NGF polls. If an election comprising just 35 electors could generate this much heat and acrimony, what would then happen during the 2015 general elections? Even more pertinent is the question: Why would responsible adults like governors subject themselves to a process and then turn round to play truants just because the outcome did not favour them?
Notwithstanding, I still believe that media coverage of the election in which the president was reportedly "floored" by Amaechi carried journalistic mischief too far. In the context in which the election was fought, the person Amaechi actually floored is Akpabio because the River state governor himself is well aware that there is no way he can defeat President Jonathan in a political contest. Yet there is also a potential banana peel that should worry presidential handlers: If governors would sign a paper to back a particular candidate touted to have been endorsed by the Villa and then vote somebody else, what is the guarantee that they cannot do the same during the Peoples Democratic Party (PDP) convention to pick its flag-bearer (where they control the delegates) or at the election proper?
However, the greater worry is the implication of the desperate scenarios that played out in recent weeks on the 2015 elections process. Will the polls be free and fair? Will the outcome be accepted even if it reflects the wishes of the electorate? Are we susceptible to a situation in which the losers would rather that our democracy crash than be gracious in defeat? These are a few of the posers that arise from the outcome of the NGF election and the prognosis does not look good with the way some of the governors have carried on.
Indeed, the real fear now is in what the NGF metaphor portends for our country and the prospects of its survival as a tolerable democracy. An election involving only 35 men is being shamelessly mangled. An election openly conducted with secret ballot with the tally announced by the Director General of the same NGF, a highly principled and thoroughbred professional, is now being derided by those who freely submitted themselves to the exercise, just because they could not have their way. An election within the confined space of a state government lodge, protected by the security details of all governors and in full view of the press is being annulled by subterfuge before our very eyes. However, there may also be a dividend in this nonsense: Nigerians need not look too far for the real culprits in the saga of election manipulations in our country: The faces of the bad losers in the NGF election seem very much familiar. Shame!
Of Globacom and The X FACTOR
I was switching television channels last Sunday afternoon when I saw on AIT a young lady on the heavy side strutting the stage after saying some nice words about her mother who was shown backstage greening almost sheepishly. But the lady sure knew how to sing as I began to watch what I would later discover to be a music talent reality programme being sponsored by Globacom in conjunction with the globally acclaimed The X Factor. What that means in essence is that the telecom company is now investing billions of Naira in the three most promising areas of our national life where the resourcefulness of our young men and women have shone through: Nollywood, football and music.
Given his pioneering role as a Nigerian prospector in our rent-driven oil and gas sector with all the associated risks in the early nineties, and the manner in which he started and turned Globacom to a serious competitor in the global telecoms market within a period of ten years, Dr. Mike Adenuga Jnr has earned the respect of many members of my generation. It is therefore no surprise that Globacom would collaborate with British entertainment genius, Mr. Simon Cowell, to bring in The X Factor, the world's biggest singing TV Reality Show, which follows contestants from auditions through to the booth camp, the judges' homes; to the grand finale. What distinguishes The X Factor from other such brands and particularly endears their shows to millions of viewers around the world is their innovations, like featuring dancers and contestants who could be accompanied to the stage with guitar, piano or any other musical instruments. Their prizes are also at the top end.
My friend, Bode Opeseitan, a divisional director with Globacom, told me at the weekend that the ultimate winner of the spectacular show will cart home a princely sum of $150,000 (about N24 million), the biggest purse among music reality TV shows in Africa. But perhaps far beyond the prize money is the fact that the winner will also secure a recording contract with the internationally acclaimed Sony Music for the recording of an album and professional management of the winning individual or group--a life-changing prospect.
So tempting is the package that I was toying with the idea of putting my own band together until Bode said the month-long pre-auditions had kicked off in Port Harcourt a month ago, to dash my dream. It is, however, interesting to learn that the highpoint of the Port Harcourt audition was a 68-year-old woman, Mrs. Evelyn D. Lawson, a Church chorister, who turned up with her 42-year son. The elderly woman said she did not feel out of place competing with artistes, some of them young enough to be her grand children for the star prize, pointing out that talent "could be discovered at any stage in one's life. And those who advertised this show did not say 'mama, don't come'".
At the Abuja audition which also held last month, a set of 17-year-old identical twins, Clinton and Blair Roberts ("The DNA Twins") reportedly stood out in the crowd of over 1000 aspiring singers. The Ghana auditions were held in Kumasi and Accra early this month with the highlight being the emergence of female twins, Lamberta and Lambertina Hansen, who reportedly declared that they were ready to battle the Nigerian male twins, "The DNA Twins" all the way for the $150,000 prize money. Now, that is one tantalising challenge.
Ever since Susan Boyle stole international limelight as an unfancied contestant (given her looks and accent) on "Britain's Got Talent" in April 2009, to literally bring down the roof with her incredible voice, even otherwise 'old school' people like me who grew up listening to 'omo rapala' Abbas Akande Obesere now follow musical singing competitions. That is why Globacom has got it spot on not only with the idea but also by its choice of judges comprising our own much-respected veteran singer, Onyeka Onwenu; rapper Jude Abaga, widely known as M.I. and Reggie Rockstone, (original name Reginald Osei), an internationally acclaimed Ghanaian hip-hop artiste.
Although the competition has started, I understand the next stages include the auditions in front of live audiences; the evictions which are always emotional events; the judge's day and the grand finale which is expected to be in September. But whatever happens in the weeks ahead, it is almost certain that the lives of some young (and maybe even old) men and women in Nigeria and Ghana will never be the same again. And by partnering with The X Factor, Globacom has now effectively become a leading force that connects the youths within the continent to their entertainment passion.
Motherless Reuben!
Effective last week Thursday, Presidential spokesman, Dr Reuben Abati, officially became a motherless child. Yet, even when 'momo' died at age 81 and left behind good children, it's part of the reality of our existence that losing one's mother is a rather painful experience, regardless of how old they (or we) might be at the time of their passage. I therefore commiserate with Reuben and other members of the Abati family on this sad loss which nonetheless calls for a thanksgiving celebration. Eyin iya ti dara!

Original_Search_Title: Falling Oil Price, Theft Worry Okonjo-Iweala
Headline: Nigeria: Falling Oil Price, Theft Worry Okonjo-Iweala
Body_Text:
Coordinating Minister for the Economy, Dr. Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala has expressed her worries over falling crude oil price in the international market, with the attendant impact on the Excess Crude Account.
Speaking during a visit to the Headquarters of the Federal Inland Revenue Service at the weekend, the minister who acknowledged the efforts of management and staff of the agency at breaking new grounds in revenue collection, noted, "As you can see we have challenges from oil revenue from on two sides. On one side we have experienced a quantity shock because of oil theft. Though we budgeted at $79, prices are coming down. That means the amount we can save in the excess crude is diminishing."
She however, commended the revenue drive of the FIRS under the watch of the acting chairman, Kabiru Mashi.
"In the past, before we had these savings in the excess, we'll be rushing to the IMF or World Bank for funding. Are we doing that now? The answer is no. We are not going for balances of payment support from the IMF or Budget support from the World Bank, because we can cushion ourselves for the next five - six months, whilst we sort out the problem.
And I am very very proud of that as a country. Any country that can manage to do that, then you are not a Banana Republic. We are really working. And that's the kind of pride I want to have.
The minister said she was motivating FIRS management and staff to continue to do their work and put in their best in view of the central role that FIRS occupies in the Nigerian economy.
She noted that though the former chairman (Ifueko Omoigui Okauru) did an outstanding job, she however commended the Acting Chairman, Alhaji Mashi for being able to hold up the institution since then.
"It is not easy to be acting and still get everybody to do his job. So I want to thank him very much for what he has done. And he couldn't have done this but for the love and support of all of you. I want you to continue in the same vein. I don't want any slip. I want to continue to be proud of the institution and the people working in it.
"I want to say that I'm quite proud of the FIRS. And the reason is that when I came in the first time as Minister of Finance in 2003, everywhere I went, people were complaining of so many issues about the Service. So we set out at that time to do something about it. Today, when I walk around, people have only praises for the service. Of course I get some complaints here and there.
"But it's not like it used to be. People see a more professional FIRS, a better FIRS. So I'm coming from a history where something has actually happened. And it couldn't have happened, unless the people at the centre of it were doing well and working together. So for that, I thank you.
On the FIRS Chairmanship, the finance minister noted that the President is keen in filling senior positions in government, while observing balance and excellence. That, she explained, was why the positions were advertised, using Phillip Consultants.
"We have a list of possible people. But he (Mr. President) also has to work with the National Assembly. He is working on it. The decision is now in his hands and I will come back to you when it is time. I just want to share this with you openly.

Original_Search_Title: Okonjo-Iweala Tasks FIRS On Improved Revenue Collection
Headline: Nigeria: Okonjo-Iweala Tasks FIRS On Improved Revenue Collection
Body_Text:
The Minister of Finance and Coordinating Minister for the Economy, Ngozi Okonjo- Iweala, has tasked the Federal Inland Revenue Service, FIRS, on the need for renewed efforts in revenue collection.
This is contained in a statement issued by the Director, Communications and Liaison Department of FIRS, Emmanuel Obeta, on Tuesday in Abuja.
The statement said Ms. Okonjo-Iweala made the call when she visited FIRS headquarters to remind the staff and management of the organisation's role in Nigeria's bid to source more non- oil revenue.
She urged the members of staff to be professional in carrying out their duties.
"As you can see, we have challenges from oil revenue from two sides. On one side, we have experienced a quantity shock because of oil theft.
"Though we budgeted oil at 79 dollars, prices are coming down. That means the amount we can save in the excess crude is diminishing.
"In the past, before we had these savings in the excess, we will be rushing to the IMF or World Bank for funding. Are we doing that now? The answer is no," the minister said.
Ms. Okonjo-Iweala said Nigeria is not going for balance of payment support from the IMF or budget support from the World Bank, because we can cushion ourselves for the next five to six months while we sought out the problem.
"I am very proud of that as a country, any country that can manage to do that is not a Banana Republic," she said.
She also said that trade taxes made up a substantial amount of revenue for the country, adding that with the new reform that restricted importation of certain commodities such as rice, more effort had to be made to source from other sectors.
"What it means is that the import of rice this year has dropped dramatically. Part of it is due to the fact that people imported last year in anticipation, so we had large import last year.
"Now they are not importing, tariffs have gone up and the demand for import is down. People are not importing.
"So trade taxes are down and it is going to stay like that for palm oil, sugar, rice, wheat and all other products.
"We are now trying to grow them ourselves, if that is the case, it means we are losing another source of revenue to the economy," she said.
The statement said the Acting Chairman of FIRS, Kabir Mashi, received Ms. Okonjo-Iweala on arrival and described her visit as a privilege to the service.
"It is a privilege to have Okonjo- Iweala, who is the supervising minister, visit us and provide guidance for the success of our assignment."
Chairman Joint Union Council of the FIRS, Samsom Okere, speaking at the occasion, thanked the minister for her visit.
NAN

Original_Search_Title: Okonjo-Iweala Commends FIRS, Raises Concern About Declining Oil Revenue
Headline: Nigeria: Okonjo-Iweala Commends FIRS, Raises Concern About Declining Oil Revenue
Body_Text:
The Minister of Finance and Coordinating Minister for the Economy, Ngozi Okonjo-Iweala, on Monday, expressed confidence in the ability of the FIRS to meet the expectation of government.
She said she was satisfied that the agency has been able to sustain the trend of improved revenue generation over the years through the professional manner they discharge their responsibilities.
The Minister spoke in Abuja during a whistle stop visit to the FIRS headquarters in Abuja, where she interacted with FIRS staff and Management on the need for a renewed revenue drive and the FIRS role in Nigeria's bid to source more non-oil revenue.
"I want to say that I'm quite proud of the FIRS," Ms. Okonjo-Iweala told the staff when she visited the agency.
"When I came in the first time as Minister of Finance in 2003, everywhere I went, people were complaining of so many issues about the Service. So, we set out at that time to do something about it. Today, when I walk around, people have only praises for the service.
"It's not as it used to be. People see a more professional and better FIRS coming from a history where something has actually happened. And it couldn't have happened, unless the people at the centre of it were doing well and working together. So for that, I thank you."
Urging them to continue to be a professional group, the minister said government expects the management of the agency to mobilize the corps of its young professionals to enable them continue to work with integrity to enhance their productivity.
"I want the young ones who have come into the institution to be inculcated with that same values of hard work, integrity and professionalism. I'm really proud of the institution. And I want you to be better. You've done a great job, bearing in mind where we were in 2003. You've done a great job up until now in 2013. What you've done in terms of results and collections have been quite amazing, especially in terms of the way you've tripled collection across the various tax classes," she said.
Staff must, however, continue to be passionate with their jobs and guard against slipping to the old era where there were often complaints against the conduct of some staff, she admonished.
She commended what she described as the outstanding job by the former Chairman, Ifueko Omoigui-Okauru, and said that the acting Chairman, Kabir Mashi, has been able to hold up the institution since then with the support of the entire staff.
On the FIRS Chairmanship position, the minister said that though government is keen at filling the office, the decision to advertise for qualified professionals using Phillip consultant was in line with President Goodluck Jonathan's desire to observe balance and excellence in the appointment.
"We have a list of possible people. But he (Mr. President) also has to work with the National Assembly. He is working on it. The decision is now in his hands and I will come back to you when it is time," the minister said.
Crude oil theft
The finance minister also said that the high incidence of crude oil 'quantity shock' as a result of increasing crude oil theft is posing serious challenges and is diminishing oil revenue savings for Nigeria in recent times.
She said there was a need for the FIRS to step up efforts to discharge their functions with professionalism and integrity to make up for any revenue shortfall the government may be experiencing as a result of the challenges.
According to the minister, though the Federal Government based its 2013 budget on a crude oil benchmark of $79 per barrel to allow for the excess revenue from the prevailing crude price at the international market to be saved, crude oil prices have been declining sharply in recent times, thereby threatening that objective.
She acknowledged the importance of the savings in the excess revenue account, pointing out that prior to the establishment of the account, Nigeria had been looking up to International Monetary Fund, IMF, or World Bank to fund shortfalls in the annual revenue.
"Are we doing that now? The answer is no. We are not going for balances of payment support from the IMF or Budget support from the World Bank, because we can cushion ourselves for the next five - six months, whilst we sought out the problem. And I am very very proud of that as a country. Any country that can manage to do that, then you are not a Banana Republic. We are really working. And that's the kind of pride I want to have," she said.

Original_Search_Title: Right Cook - Mixing Matooke and Offal
Headline: Uganda: Right Cook - Mixing Matooke and Offal
Body_Text:
Green bananas, commonly known as matooke, peeled and cut into pieces and cooked together with offal is many people's choice for breakfast. Today, we look at preparing this delicious meal. Ingredients
500g matooke
250g offal or a mixture of tripe and sweet breads
2 tomatoes
1 big onion
1 teaspoon curry powder
2 teaspoon salt
500ml water
Method
Cut the offal into small pieces and wash well.
Put in a pan; add ½ teaspoon salt and about four tablespoons water.
Cook covered on low heat till tender and the water has dried out and add the curry powder. Dry fry for a few minutes with chopped onions.
Peel and wash the matooke; put 2/3 in a pan (medium size) and add the chopped vegetables and the meat.
Rinse out the pan where the offals were cooked and pour the water into the matooke. Add salt and the rest of the matooke on top.
Cover and cook for 15 minutes. Reduce heat and cook on fairly low heat for a further 15 minutes. Make sure enough water for sauce is left.
When ready, serve hot.
Recipes from Jojo Cookery and Training Centre

Original_Search_Title: 'Donate Just a Dollar'
Headline: Zimbabwe: 'Donate Just a Dollar'
Body_Text:
THE Zimbabwe Football Trust have launched an ambitious drive for all patriotic football fans to donate a dollar to the game as part of efforts to oil a national sporting discipline that is badly in need of a huge financial injection.
